| 5. Modularize | **Edit → Create SubVI** | Icon Editor, Connector Pane | Reusable `.vi` node |
| 6. Debug | **Highlight Execution**, **Probe** | Debug toolbar | Validated logic |
| 7. Save & Document | **File → Save**, **VI Properties** | — | `.vi` + metadata |

### 3.4 Logical Walkthrough: Why Dataflow Differs from Text-Based Execution

In C/Python, statements execute **sequentially** (line-by-line). In LabVIEW G:

- A node (function/subVI) is a **black box** with input terminals on the left and output terminals on the right.
- The **node fires** when **all** required inputs have data.
- This means **parallelism is implicit**: if two unrelated nodes have their inputs ready, LabVIEW **schedules them in parallel** (on multi-core systems, they run on separate threads).
- **Implication:** The developer does **not** write `Thread.start()` — concurrency is automatic.

**Example — Parallel Execution Analogy:**

```
   [Sensor Read] ──► [Filter] ──► [Display]
         │
         └──────────► [Logger] ──► [File Write]
```

Both `[Filter]` and `[Logger]` may execute concurrently because their inputs are independent.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Seven-Step VI Development Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Problem & Specification] --> B[Step 2: Design Front Panel]
    B --> C[Step 3: Construct Block Diagram]
    C --> D[Step 4: Establish Data Flow & Wiring]
    D --> E[Step 5: Add Structures & SubVIs]
    E --> F[Step 6: Debug & Validate]
    F --> G{Tests Pass?}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[Step 7: Document, Iconize & Save]
    H --> I[Deploy VI]
    I --> J[End: Production Ready VI]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of a Typical LabVIEW VI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FP[Front Panel - User Interface]
        FP1[Knob: Frequency Control]
        FP2[Switch: Start Stop Boolean]
        FP3[Waveform Graph: Output Indicator]
        FP4[LED: Status Indicator]
    end
    subgraph BD[Block Diagram - G Code Logic]
        BD1[Terminal: Frequency]
        BD2[Terminal: StartStop]
        BD3[Sine Waveform Generator VI]
        BD4[While Loop Structure]
        BD5[Filter SubVI]
        BD6[Bundle Signals]
        BD7[Wire to Graph Terminal]
    end
    FP1 --> BD1
    FP2 --> BD2
    BD1 --> BD3
    BD3 --> BD5
    BD5 --> BD6
    BD6 --> BD7
    BD4 -.-> BD3
    BD4 -.-> BD5
    BD7 --> FP3
    BD2 --> FP4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Dataflow Execution in a While Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant FrontPanel as Front Panel Controls
    participant WhileLoop as While Loop Boundary
    participant SineGen as Sine Waveform Generator
    participant Filter as Filter SubVI
    participant Graph as Waveform Graph
    User->>FrontPanel: Adjust Knob to 50 Hz
    User->>FrontPanel: Click Start Boolean TRUE
    FrontPanel->>WhileLoop: Feed Frequency and Stop FALSE
    WhileLoop->>SineGen: Trigger Execution
    SineGen->>Filter: Output Raw Waveform
    Filter->>Graph: Output Filtered Waveform
    Graph-->>User: Display Smoothed Sine
    Note over WhileLoop: Loop iterates every Titer
    User->>FrontPanel: Click Stop Boolean TRUE
    WhileLoop->>WhileLoop: Stop Condition TRUE, Exit
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Debugging Workflow Inside a VI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Run VI] --> L{Execution Error?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Check Error List Window]
    L -- No --> N[Use Highlight Execution]
    M --> O[Locate Broken Wires]
    O --> P[Correct Wire Types]
    P --> Q[Recompile and Re-run]
    N --> R[Place Probes on Wires]
    R --> S[Verify Intermediate Values]
    S --> T{Values Correct?}
    T -- No --> U[Adjust Block Diagram Logic]
    T -- Yes --> V[Save Final VI]
    U --> Q
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`); reserved words like `end`, `graph`, `subgraph` are not used as standalone node names; labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown bold/italics or HTML tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
*List any six steps involved in developing a Virtual Instrument using LabVIEW.*

**Model Answer (6 steps, ½ mark each):**

1. **Define the problem** — identify inputs, outputs, range, accuracy.
2. **Design the Front Panel** — add controls and indicators from the Controls Palette.
3. **Construct the Block Diagram** — place functions and structures from the Functions Palette.
4. **Wire terminals** to establish data flow between nodes.
5. **Modularize using Sub-VIs** — convert reusable code blocks into sub-VIs with icon and connector.
6. **Debug and validate** — use Highlight Execution, Probes, and Breakpoints.
7. **Document and save** — VI properties, icon editor, file save.

*(Any six valid steps fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Explain the role of the Front Panel and Block Diagram in a LabVIEW VI.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Front Panel:** It is the **user interface** of the VI, built using **Controls** (inputs such as knobs, switches, text boxes) and **Indicators** (outputs such as graphs, LEDs, numeric displays). It acts as the interactive "face" of the virtual instrument through which the user provides inputs and views results.

- **Block Diagram:** It is the **graphical source code** window where the program's logic is implemented. Each control/indicator on the Front Panel appears as a **terminal** on the Block Diagram. Functions, structures (While/For loops, Case structures), and sub-VIs are placed and **wired** to define data flow.

- **Relationship:** The Front Panel and Block Diagram are **tightly coupled** — modifying a control on the front panel automatically updates the corresponding terminal on the block diagram, enabling seamless iterative development.

---

### **PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Sub-VI & Modular Development Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the steps involved in developing a Virtual Instrument in LabVIEW, with emphasis on the Front Panel and Block Diagram design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the process of creating a Sub-VI in LabVIEW. Discuss the importance of the **Icon** and **Connector Pane** in modular VI development. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The development of a Virtual Instrument in LabVIEW follows a structured seven-step process:

**Step 1 — Problem Definition:** Identify the measurement or control requirement, the input signals, the output indicators, sampling rate, accuracy, and trigger conditions. A written specification reduces ambiguity.

**Step 2 — Front Panel Design:** Using the **Controls Palette**, the developer adds **Controls** (e.g., knobs, switches) for inputs and **Indicators** (e.g., graphs, LEDs) for outputs. Front panel elements should be logically grouped and labeled using the **labeling tool**.

**Step 3 — Block Diagram Construction:** Switching to the Block Diagram window, the developer accesses the **Functions Palette** to place built-in VIs, arithmetic functions, and structures. Each front panel control/indicator automatically generates a **terminal** on the block diagram.

**Step 4 — Wiring and Data Flow:** Terminals are connected using the **Wiring Tool**. LabVIEW enforces **dataflow semantics** — a node executes only when all its input terminals have valid data. Wire color indicates data type (e.g., blue for integer, orange for floating-point, green for boolean).

**Step 5 — Structures and Modularization:** Control structures (While Loop, For Loop, Case Structure) and modular sub-VIs are added to manage complexity. This step implements the main execution logic.

**Step 6 — Debug and Test:** Tools such as **Highlight Execution**, **Probes**, and **Breakpoints** are used to verify logical correctness and to inspect intermediate data values.

**Step 7 — Documentation and Save:** VI metadata (author, version, description) is added through **VI Properties**. The Icon is edited to provide a unique visual identifier, and the file is saved as `.vi`.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

A **Sub-VI** is a VI that is used as a function within another VI (the **caller**), analogous to a subroutine in text-based programming. The procedure to create one is:

**Procedure to Create a Sub-VI:**

1. **Select** the portion of the block diagram to be modularized using the **Positioning Tool**.
2. From the menu bar, choose **Edit → Create SubVI**.
3. LabVIEW automatically creates a **new VI** with the selected code, assigns a default **icon**, and creates a **connector pane** with input and output terminals corresponding to the data flow of the original region.
4. The developer **customizes the Icon** by right-clicking the connector pane and selecting **Edit Icon**. The Icon Editor allows drawing at multiple resolutions (16×16, 32×32, 256×256).
5. The developer **assigns terminals** on the Connector Pane to specific input controls and output indicators using the **Wiring Tool**.
6. The sub-VI is **saved** with a meaningful name and is now available in the **User Library** or the **Select a VI** dialog.

**Importance of Icon and Connector Pane:**

- **Icon:** Provides a **unique, instantly recognizable graphical identifier** for the sub-VI. When the sub-VI is placed on the block diagram of the calling VI, the icon appears as a single node, making the calling block diagram readable and self-documenting. A well-designed icon is critical in **hierarchical VI architectures** with hundreds of sub-VIs.

- **Connector Pane:** Defines the **input and output interface** of the sub-VI. It enforces a **strict API contract** — required inputs must be wired; recommended inputs are optional. The connector pane supports up to **28 terminals** (14 inputs on the left, 14 outputs on the right) arranged in standardized patterns. It enables **data encapsulation**, **code reuse**, and **parallel development** by different team members.

**Engineering Significance:**

The sub-VI mechanism transforms LabVIEW from a graphical scripting tool into a **modular, hierarchical, and scalable development environment**, suitable for industrial-grade applications such as automated test stations, real-time control systems on cRIO/PXI targets, and large-scale SCADA implementations.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Dataflow & Debugging Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **dataflow execution model** in LabVIEW. How does it differ from conventional text-based sequential programming? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **debugging tools** available in LabVIEW. Explain how Highlight Execution, Probes, and Breakpoints are used to validate a VI. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **dataflow execution model** is the foundational execution paradigm of G-programming in LabVIEW. Its core tenets are:

1. **Node-driven execution:** A node (function, sub-VI, or structure) executes **only when data is available at all of its required input terminals**.
2. **Output availability:** Outputs of a node are produced **only after** the node finishes execution; this output then becomes the input for downstream nodes.
3. **Implicit parallelism:** Multiple independent nodes with ready inputs can execute **concurrently**, with LabVIEW's runtime scheduler distributing them across available CPU cores.
4. **No implicit sequencing:** Unlike text-based languages where execution follows line order, in LabVIEW the developer must explicitly use structures (Sequence Structure, Flat Sequence) if strict ordering is required.

**Comparison with Text-Based Programming:**

| Aspect | C / Python (Text-Based) | LabVIEW (Dataflow) |
|---|---|---|
| Execution driver | Sequential line order | Availability of input data |
| Parallelism | Explicit via threads/processes | Implicit via data dependency |
| Variable scope | Global/local with declarations | Implicit through wire connections |
| Loops | `for`, `while` keyword | While Loop, For Loop structures |
| Function call | `func(args)` syntax | Wire data into sub-VI node |

**Example:**

Consider computing $y = \sin(x) + \cos(x)$ for $x = \pi/4$:

- **In C:** `float x = 3.14159/4; float s = sin(x); float c = cos(x); float y = s + c;` (4 sequential statements).
- **In LabVIEW:** A numeric constant $\pi/4$ is divided by 4, fed simultaneously into two parallel branches — one to the `sin` function and one to the `cos` function — and their outputs are summed at an `Add` node. The `sin` and `cos` nodes can execute in parallel.

**Implication:** The dataflow model is naturally suited to **signal processing**, **real-time control**, and **parallel acquisition** — domains where LabVIEW excels.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

LabVIEW provides an integrated **graphical debugging toolkit** that operates directly on the block diagram:

**1. Highlight Execution (Light Bulb Icon):**

- Activated via the **Highlight Execution** button on the toolbar.
- Animates the data flow: a "data bubble" moves along the wires, and values are displayed at each node as it executes.
- **Use case:** Quickly identifies nodes that do not execute (no data reaching them) and verifies the **order of execution** in complex block diagrams.

**2. Probes:**

- Created by right-clicking a wire and selecting **Probe** (or by using the **Probe Tool** from the Tools Palette).
- A floating **Probe Watch Window** appears, displaying the value passing through the wire in real time.
- **Use case:** Inspects intermediate values without breaking the data flow. Custom probes can be created to display complex data types (e.g., waveform, cluster).

**3. Breakpoints:**

- Set by clicking on a node or wire with the **Breakpoint Tool**, or by right-clicking and selecting **Set Breakpoint** (red dot indicator).
- Pauses execution at the breakpoint, allowing the developer to single-step through subsequent nodes using **Step Into**, **Step Over**, and **Step Out**.
- **Use case:** Halting the VI at a specific point to inspect state, then proceeding manually.

**4. Additional Tools:**

- **Probe Watch Window:** Aggregates multiple probes into a single panel.
- **Error List Window:** Lists all broken wires, unconnected terminals, and warnings before run.
- **Retain Wire Values:** Stores the last value on each wire for post-execution probing via the **Probe** tool.

**Engineering Workflow:**

A typical debugging session for a malfunctioning VI:

1. Click **Run** → if error appears, check **Error List**.
2. Enable **Highlight Execution** to observe flow.
3. Place **Probes** on suspect wires to inspect intermediate values.
4. Set **Breakpoints** to pause at critical decisions (e.g., Case Structure selector).
5. **Step** through logic to localize the fault.
6. Correct the block diagram and **re-run** until all probes return expected values.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students frequently **omit the dataflow trigger condition** in answers, losing 2–3 marks.
> - Confusing **Highlight Execution** with **Step Into** — they are distinct tools with different uses.
> - Failing to mention that **wires, not nodes, are the primary debugging targets** for probes.
> - Not stating the **maximum connector terminals (28)** for sub-VI design — a 1-mark deduction is standard.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ VI development has **7 canonical steps**: Spec → Front Panel → Block Diagram → Wire → Modularize → Debug → Save.
- ✅ **Front Panel** = User Interface (Controls = inputs, Indicators = outputs).
- ✅ **Block Diagram** = Graphical source code using dataflow.
- ✅ **Dataflow rule:** Node executes **only when ALL inputs are ready**; output produced **after** execution.
- ✅ **Wiring color code:** Orange = DBL, Blue = I32, Green = Boolean, Pink = String, Brown = Cluster.
- ✅ **Structures:** While Loop, For Loop, Case Structure, Sequence, Event Structure, Flat Sequence, Stacked Sequence.
- ✅ **Sub-VI** = a VI used as a function; created via **Edit → Create SubVI**.
- ✅ **Icon** = unique visual identifier; **Connector Pane** = input/output terminal map (max **28** terminals).
- ✅ **Debugging tools:** Highlight Execution, Probes, Breakpoints, Step Into/Over/Out, Error List.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — must be applied in DAQmx configuration.
- ✅ **Quantization step:** $\Delta V = V_{range}/2^n$; max error $\pm \Delta V / 2$.
- ✅ **Ideal ADC SNR:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- ✅ **File extensions:** `.vi` (VI), `.ctl` (custom control), `.lvproj` (project), `.llb` (legacy library).
- ✅ **Default sampling rate for audio in KTU labs:** typically **44.1 kHz or 48 kHz**.
- ✅ **Iteration time of a loop:** $T_{iter} = T_{acquire} + T_{process} + T_{display}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always begin a long-answer question with a **one-line definition** of the VI development process, then proceed with **numbered, bold-headed steps** for clarity. Examiners award 1–2 marks for **structured presentation** beyond the technical content.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Time Data Acquisition - Analog and Digital Input/Output Operations in LabVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET833 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Real Time Data Acquisition - Analog and Digital Input/Output Operations in LabVIEW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — REAL-TIME DATA ACQUISITION: ANALOG & DIGITAL I/O IN LABVIEW

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Real-Time Data Acquisition (DAQ)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> *Real-Time Data Acquisition (DAQ) is the process of sampling real-world physical phenomena (analog signals) at a defined rate, converting them into digital data, processing/displaying them on a computer, and simultaneously generating control output signals — all within deterministic, time-bounded windows using hardware (DAQ cards) and software (LabVIEW Virtual Instruments).*

In simpler words — it is the **"digital nervous system"** of any modern instrument. A sensor measures a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, vibration), the DAQ card digitises it, and LabVIEW turns it into a graph, a number, or a control decision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stethoscope of a Smart Factory

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Doctor ↔ Industrial Plant**
> Imagine a doctor examining a patient. The **stethoscope** is the *sensor*, the **ears-to-brain** link is the *signal conditioning + ADC*, the **doctor's brain** is the *LabVIEW program*, and the **prescription** is the *analog/digital output*. Real-time DAQ means the doctor can hear the heartbeat, interpret it, and write a prescription **in the same heartbeat cycle** — without missing a beat. The DAQ card is the "electronic stethoscope", and LabVIEW is the "electronic brain" that never sleeps.

### 1.3 Building Blocks of a LabVIEW DAQ System

| Block | Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor / Transducer** | Converts physical quantity → electrical signal | Thermocouple, RTD, Strain gauge |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplifies, filters, isolates | NI SCXI / SCC modules |
| **DAQ Hardware** | ADC, DAC, Digital I/O lines | NI PCIe-6363, USB-6002, cDAQ-9174 |
| **LabVIEW Software** | Acquisition, processing, display, storage | DAQmx VIs, VIs, Express VIs |
| **Actuator / Output Device** | Receives control signal | Motor, Relay, Heater, Valve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology — Master These:**
> - **ADC** — Analog-to-Digital Converter
> - **DAC** — Digital-to-Analog Converter
> - **DIO** — Digital Input/Output lines
> - **RSE / NRSE / DIFF** — Input wiring topologies
> - **Sample Clock (SCLK)** — Timing source for ADC/DAC
> - **Task** — A collection of virtual channels, timing, and triggering

### 1.4 Why LabVIEW for DAQ?

> [!NOTE]
> LabVIEW (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench) by **National Instruments (NI)** is a *graphical programming* environment. Its **Dataflow Execution** model and built-in **DAQmx driver API** make it the de-facto industry standard for DAQ — used in CERN, Boeing, Tesla, and every KTU electronics/biomedical lab.

### 1.5 Two Pillars of Real-Time I/O

1. **Analog I/O** — Continuous, infinitely variable signals (e.g., 0–10 V, 4–20 mA current loops).
2. **Digital I/O** — Two-state binary signals (e.g., 0 V = LOW, 5 V = HIGH) used for switches, relays, LEDs, encoders.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Analog Input Signal Chain

The journey of an analog signal from the physical world to your LabVIEW front panel follows this **strict, ordered pipeline**:

1. **Transduction** — Sensor converts physical quantity to voltage/current.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification, filtering, isolation, linearisation.
3. **Multiplexing (MUX)** — Multiple channels share one ADC (sample-sequenced or simultaneous).
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — Holds signal constant during ADC conversion.
5. **ADC Conversion** — Quantises the held voltage into a binary code.
6. **Buffer Storage** — Acquired samples stored in FIFO buffer in PC memory.
7. **LabVIEW Processing** — Reading, analysis, display, logging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a buffer?** The ADC may run at **1 MS/s** (1 million samples/second), but LabVIEW can only *process* a few hundred samples at a time. The **FIFO buffer** decouples the hardware from software, preventing sample loss.

## 2.2 Fundamental DAQ Formulas (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** | Hz |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | **Quantisation Step (LSB size)** | Volts |
| 3 | $E_{q_{max}} = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | **Maximum Quantisation Error** | Volts |
| 4 | $SNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ | **Ideal ADC Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | dB |
| 5 | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02}$ | **Effective Number of Bits** | bits |
| 6 | $T_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | **Time Period of One Sample** | seconds |
| 7 | $D_{rate} = f_s \cdot n_{ch} \cdot n_{bytes}$ | **Total Data Throughput** | bytes/s |
| 8 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{\text{Code}}{2^n} \cdot V_{FS}$ | **Digital Code to Voltage Conversion** | Volts |
| 9 | $\text{Bits} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{V_{FS}}{Q}\right)$ | **Resolution in Bits** | bits |
| 10 | $f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{signal} \vert$ | **Aliased Frequency** | Hz |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipes inside formulas:** Notice we use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables in the KTU study sheet above. This is a KTU formatting standard.

## 2.3 Sampling Theorem — The Heart of All DAQ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem (1949)**
> To reconstruct an analog signal from its samples without information loss, the sampling frequency **must be at least twice the highest frequency component** of the signal.

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Where:
- $f_s$ = Sampling frequency (samples/second)
- $f_{max}$ = Highest frequency present in the input signal

**Consequence:** If $f_s < 2 \cdot f_{max}$, the phenomenon of **Aliasing** occurs — the signal appears as a false, lower-frequency waveform.

## 2.4 ADC Resolution & Quantisation

The ADC divides the full-scale input range into a finite number of discrete steps:

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

For example, a **16-bit ADC** with $V_{FS} = 10\text{ V}$ gives:

$$Q = \frac{10\text{ V}}{2^{16}} = \frac{10}{65536} \approx 152.59 \ \mu V$$

This $Q$ value is the **smallest measurable voltage change** — the **LSB (Least Significant Bit)**.

## 2.5 Analog Input Wiring Topologies — A Critical KTU Topic

| Topology | Full Form | Signal Range | Use Case | Noise Rejection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RSE** | Referenced Single-Ended | $V_{signal}$ vs ground | Floating sources, battery-powered sensors | Lowest |
| **NRSE** | Non-Referenced Single-Ended | $V_{signal}$ vs common-mode | Grounded sources, thermocouples | Medium |
| **DIFF** | Differential | $V_{+} - V_{-}$ | High-noise industrial, audio, ECG | Highest |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** When asked "which configuration should you use for a strain gauge in a factory with 50 HP motors?" — always answer **DIFF (Differential)** with shielded twisted-pair cable.

## 2.6 Digital I/O Concepts

Unlike analog, digital I/O works on **discrete voltage levels**:

| Logic Family | LOW Level (V) | HIGH Level (V) | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TTL** | 0.0 – 0.8 | 2.0 – 5.0 | Microcontrollers, legacy logic |
| **CMOS 5V** | 0.0 – 1.5 | 3.5 – 5.0 | Modern ICs |
| **LVTTL 3.3V** | 0.0 – 0.8 | 2.0 – 3.3 | FPGA, modern MCUs |
| **24V Industrial** | 0.0 – 5.0 | 11.0 – 24.0 | PLCs, factory sensors |

### 2.6.1 Digital I/O Modes
- **Static DIO** — Read/Write port once (slow, for switches, LEDs).
- **Counters / Timers** — Measure frequency, pulse width, quadrature encoders.
- **Buffered (Waveform) DIO** — Generate precise digital patterns (PWM, protocols).

## 2.7 The DAQmx Driver Architecture (LabVIEW)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DAQmx — the Industry Standard Driver**
> Every modern NI DAQ card is controlled in LabVIEW through the **NI-DAQmx** driver. The DAQmx API follows a consistent **four-stage model**:

1. **Create Task** → `DAQmx Create Task.vi`
2. **Add Channel(s)** → `DAQmx Create Virtual Channel.vi` (AI, AO, DI, DO, CI, CO)
3. **Configure Timing & Trigger** → `DAQmx Timing.vi`, `DAQmx Trigger.vi`
4. **Start, Read/Write, Stop, Clear** → `DAQmx Start Task.vi`, `DAQmx Read.vi`, `DAQmx Write.vi`, `DAQmx Stop Task.vi`, `DAQmx Clear Task.vi`

**Acquisition Modes:**
- **Finite (1-Sample-On-Demand)** — One or N samples, then stops.
- **Continuous** — Acquires indefinitely, requires a buffer.
- **Hardware-Timed Single Point** — One sample per clock tick, for control loops.

## 2.8 Real-Time Performance Equation (KTU Favourite)

$$T_{loop} = T_{acquire} + T_{process} + T_{display} + T_{output}$$

For **real-time** operation:

$$T_{loop} \leq T_{deadline}$$

If $T_{loop}$ exceeds the deadline, the system has a **jitter** or **missed deadline** — the system is no longer "real-time".

## 2.9 Engineering Real-World Applications

- **Biomedical** — ECG, EEG, EMG (100 Hz – 5 kHz, 16-bit, DIFF).
- **Vibration Analysis** — Bearing fault detection (≥ 20 kHz, IEPE sensors).
- **Process Control** — 4-20 mA current loops, PID control.
- **Automotive** — CAN-bus + analog throttle position sensing.
- **Renewable Energy** — Solar panel I-V curve tracing.
- **Acoustic / Audio** — 44.1 kHz, 16-bit, stereo (CD quality).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nyquist Sampling Theorem (Intuitive Proof)

**Step 1 — Start with a sinusoidal input signal**

$$x(t) = A \sin(2 \pi f_{max} t)$$

**Step 2 — Define sampling as multiplication by an impulse train**

The sampled signal is the original signal multiplied by a periodic impulse train $p(t)$:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(kT_s) \cdot \delta(t - kT_s)$$

where $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period.

**Step 3 — Take the Fourier Transform**

By the multiplication property of the Fourier Transform:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - n f_s)$$

The spectrum of the sampled signal consists of **replicas** of the original spectrum, shifted by integer multiples of $f_s$ and scaled by $1/T_s$.

**Step 4 — Apply the non-overlap condition**

For the original spectrum to be recoverable by a low-pass filter, the replicas must not overlap. This happens only when:

$$f_s - f_{max} \geq f_{max}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to obtain the Nyquist criterion**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

$$\boxed{f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}}$$

**Step 6 — Define the Nyquist Frequency**

$$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2}$$

This is the highest frequency that **can be unambiguously represented** by samples taken at $f_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "at least 2×"?** Strictly, Nyquist requires $f_s > 2 f_{max}$. The equality $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is a degenerate case where reconstruction is theoretically possible but practically impossible (requires an ideal brick-wall filter). Hence the *strict inequality* is used in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantisation Step (LSB) and Resolution

**Step 1 — Define the full-scale voltage range**

The ADC measures signals between $-V_{FS}/2$ and $+V_{FS}/2$ (bipolar) or $0$ to $V_{FS}$ (unipolar).

**Step 2 — Define the number of discrete codes**

An $n$-bit ADC produces $2^n$ distinct output codes.

**Step 3 — Compute the size of one quantisation step**

The full-scale range is divided equally among the $2^n$ codes:

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

**Step 4 — Example (KTU Textbook Style)**

For an 8-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 5\text{ V}$:

$$Q = \frac{5\text{ V}}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} = 19.53 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum quantisation error**

Quantisation error is the difference between the true analog value and the nearest quantised value. The maximum is half of one LSB:

$$E_{q_{max}} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 6 — Converting digital code to voltage**

If the ADC outputs code $C$ (integer from $0$ to $2^n - 1$):

$$V_{in} = \frac{C}{2^n} \cdot V_{FS}$$

For code $C = 32768$ on a 16-bit, $\pm 10\text{ V}$ ADC:

$$V_{in} = \frac{32768}{65536} \cdot 20\text{ V} - 10\text{ V} = 10\text{ V} - 10\text{ V} = 0\text{ V}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical SNR of an Ideal ADC

**Step 1 — Assume quantisation noise is uniformly distributed**

The quantisation error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in the interval $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$.

**Step 2 — Compute the RMS value of quantisation noise**

$$e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{Q} \int_{-Q/2}^{+Q/2} e^2 \, de}$$

$$e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{Q} \cdot \frac{e^3}{3} \Big|_{-Q/2}^{+Q/2}}$$

$$e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{Q} \cdot \frac{2(Q/2)^3}{3}}$$

$$e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{Q^2}{12}} = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{12}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the RMS value of a full-scale sine input**

For a sine wave of amplitude $A$:

$$V_{signal,rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_{FS}/2}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Form the SNR ratio**

$$SNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal,rms}}{e_{q,rms}}\right)$$

Substituting $Q = V_{FS}/2^n$ and simplifying:

$$SNR = 6.02n + 1.76 \quad \text{(dB)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example**

For $n = 16$:

$$SNR = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Aliasing Frequency

**Step 1 — Define the alias**

A sinusoidal signal of frequency $f_{signal}$ sampled at $f_s$ appears as a sinusoid of frequency:

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - N \cdot f_s \vert$$

where $N$ is the integer nearest to $f_{signal}/f_s$.

**Step 2 — Example problem (typical KTU)**

A signal of $f_{signal} = 7\text{ kHz}$ is sampled at $f_s = 2\text{ kHz}$. Find the alias frequency.

$$N = \text{round}\left(\frac{7000}{2000}\right) = 4$$

$$f_{alias} = \vert 7000 - 4 \times 2000 \vert = \vert 7000 - 8000 \vert = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

So the **7 kHz signal appears as a 1 kHz signal** at the output — a textbook alias.

**Step 3 — Engineering remedy**

- Use **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** (cutoff = $f_s/2$).
- Increase sampling rate (over-sampling).
- Use **sigma-delta ADCs** with built-in digital filtering.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — DAQ Throughput and Buffer Sizing

**Step 1 — Total data rate**

For $n_{ch}$ channels sampled at $f_s$ samples/sec, with $n_{bytes}$ bytes per sample:

$$D_{rate} = f_s \cdot n_{ch} \cdot n_{bytes} \quad \text{(bytes/sec)}$$

**Step 2 — Example: 8 channels of 16-bit data at 10 kS/s**

$$D_{rate} = 10000 \times 8 \times 2 = 160{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} = 156.25 \text{ KB/s}$$

**Step 3 — FIFO buffer sizing rule of thumb**

$$\text{Buffer Size} = \frac{D_{rate} \times T_{process}}{1024} \text{ KB}$$

If LabVIEW processes a buffer every 50 ms:

$$\text{Buffer Size} = \frac{156.25 \text{ KB/s} \times 0.05 \text{ s}}{1} = 7.81 \text{ KB}$$

Round up to the next power of 2 → **8 KB minimum** (use 16 KB for safety).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Analog vs Digital I/O

| Parameter | Analog I/O | Digital I/O |
|---|---|---|
| Signal nature | Continuous (V or I) | Discrete (HIGH/LOW) |
| Resolution | 12, 16, 18, 24-bit | 1 bit per line |
| Speed limiting factor | ADC conversion time | Port update rate |
| Susceptibility to noise | High | Low (hysteresis) |
| Typical use | Sensors, waveforms | Switches, relays, protocols |
| KTU Example | Thermocouple → AI ch0 | Limit switch → DI line 0 |
| Time-domain representation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$, $n \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| LabVIEW VI | `DAQmx Read (Analog 1D Wfm NCh N-Samp)` | `DAQmx Read (Digital 1D U32 1Samp 1Line)` |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A vibration sensor outputs a signal with maximum frequency of 8 kHz. The data must be captured with at least 5× oversampling. The DAQ card is 16-bit, with $\pm 10\text{ V}$ range. Calculate:
> 1. Minimum sampling frequency
> 2. Quantisation step (LSB)
> 3. Maximum quantisation error
> 4. Theoretical SNR

**Solution:**

**1. Minimum sampling frequency**

$$f_s = 5 \times 2 \times f_{max} = 5 \times 2 \times 8000 = 80{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 80 \text{ kS/s}$$

**2. Quantisation step**

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{20 \text{ V}}{65536} = 305.18 \ \mu V$$

**3. Maximum quantisation error**

$$E_{q_{max}} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm 152.59 \ \mu V$$

**4. Theoretical SNR**

$$SNR = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Analog Input Wiring Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        D1[AI Positive Terminal AI+]:::dpos --- LOAD[Sensor Load]:::sensor
```

## 4.4 Continuous vs Finite Acquisition — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    F1 --> F2[DAQmx Timing with Sample Mode Finite Samples]:::f2
```
