Parallelism here is logical (interleaved by the scheduler) rather than physical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why both exist in KTU Module 2?**
> Real-world Virtual Instrumentation systems often need *both* levels:
> 1. **FPGA layer** — for ultra-fast deterministic I/O (encoder reading, PWM generation, custom serial protocols, hardware safety interlocks running at **100 MHz +**).
> 2. **Embedded/host layer** — for higher-level control, networking, data logging, HMI rendering, and floating-point math where determinism is at the **kHz** scale, not MHz.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LabVIEW FPGA Architecture Stack

The LabVIEW FPGA toolchain is organized in **four strict layers**. Every KTU question on "LabVIEW FPGA architecture" maps directly to these four layers.

1. **Application Layer (Host VI)** — A normal LabVIEW VI running on the Windows/RT host PC. It manages the user interface, file I/O, networking, and high-level analysis. It *cannot* directly read/write FPGA pins.
2. **Communication Layer (DMA, Interrupts, FIFOs)** — A high-bandwidth channel that shuttles data between the slow host CPU and the fast FPGA fabric. Data typically flows through **DMA FIFOs** (First-In-First-Out buffers implemented in FPGA block RAM).
3. **FPGA Layer (FPGA VI)** — The block diagram that *defines the hardware*. It runs entirely inside the FPGA fabric. It is a real circuit, not software.
4. **I/O Layer (FPGA Node I/O pins)** — The physical DIO, AI, AO, encoder, PWM, and special-function lines on the FPGA board that connect the silicon to the real world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Determinism Metric:** A well-designed FPGA VI can respond to an external event with a **worst-case jitter of one clock cycle** — for a **40 MHz** FPGA this is **25 ns**, and for a **100 MHz** FPGA it is **10 ns**. No software RTOS can compete with this. *This single number is worth 2 marks in any KTU viva.*

## 2.2 Compilation Pipeline — From Block Diagram to Silicon

The LabVIEW FPGA Module performs the following synthesis chain when the user clicks "Run":

- **Front End (LabVIEW → HDL):** Each LabVIEW node is translated into a synthesizable HDL construct. A `+` becomes a ripple/carry-save adder; a `Case Structure` becomes a hardware multiplexer; a `While Loop` becomes a state machine with the loop-iteration register in BRAM.
- **Middle End (Optimization):** The compiler dead-code-eliminates unused logic, infers block-RAM for shift registers/FIFOs, maps multipliers to dedicated **DSP48** slices, and packs registers to meet timing.
- **Back End (Place & Route):** The Xilinx Vivado (or legacy ISE) place-and-route tool maps the netlist onto the actual FPGA silicon (CLBs, BRAM, DSP, transceivers, clock tiles).
- **Bitstream Generation:** A binary `.bit` file is produced and downloaded over **JTAG** or **Ethernet** to the FPGA's configuration memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compile Time Reality:** Because of place-and-route, an FPGA VI compile can take **15 minutes to 2 hours**. This is normal — *not* an error. KTU exam answers should explicitly mention the *high compile-time cost* as a tradeoff of FPGA-based design.

## 2.3 LabVIEW FPGA Programming Idioms (The 7 Must-Know Nodes)

| # | Node / Construct | Hardware Mapping | Purpose |
|---|------------------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | **While Loop** | State machine + iteration register in BRAM | Core execution skeleton of every FPGA VI |
| 2 | **Single-Cycle Timed Loop (SCTL)** | Free-running counter clocked at FPGA base rate | Guarantees 1-loop-iteration = 1 clock tick; the *fastest* construct |
| 3 | **For Loop** | Unrolled hardware (if un-rolled) or pipelined circuit | Used for fixed-iteration DSP, e.g., FIR filter |
| 4 | **Case Structure** | Multiplexer tree | Hardware `if/else` |
| 5 | **FIFO / DMA FIFO** | Block-RAM based queue | Host ↔ FPGA data streaming |
| 6 | **I/O Node (FPGA I/O)** | Physical pin via SelectIO / GTP / SERDES | Read/write to a real pin |
| 7 | **IP Integration Node** | Instantiated HDL IP core | Brings in 3rd-party or NI-shipped VHDL IP |

## 2.4 LabVIEW Embedded Architecture Stack

LabVIEW Embedded targets a **processor-based** runtime, not an FPGA. The architecture is therefore different:

1. **Application Layer (LabVIEW Block Diagram on Host PC)** — Same LabVIEW environment.
2. **C Code Generator (LabVIEW Embedded Module / Microprocessor SDK)** — Translates block diagram into ANSI C source.
3. **Cross-Compiler Toolchain (Keil µVision, IAR, GCC ARM, CCS)** — Compiles C to MCU machine code.
4. **Firmware Image (.hex / .bin / .elf)** — Loaded into MCU flash memory.
5. **Target Microprocessor (ARM Cortex-M, MSP430, AVR, etc.)** — Executes the firmware deterministically with on-chip peripherals.

> [!NOTE]
> **NI myRIO vs NI ELVIS III — Common KTU Confusion**
> - **myRIO (NI myRIO-1900/1950):** Carries a **Zynq-7010** SoC (ARM Cortex-A9 dual core **+ FPGA fabric**). Programs can be deployed to *either* the ARM side (LabVIEW Real-Time) *or* the FPGA side (LabVIEW FPGA) — so myRIO is a **hybrid** target.
> - **ELVIS III:** A teaching lab workstation. The base board carries a **Zynq-7020** (also ARM + FPGA). It exposes the FPGA pins as banana-jack lab terminals — useful in KTU lab courses for academic work.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Definition | Typical FPGA / Embedded Value | Unit |
|-------------------|------------|--------------------------------|------|
| $f_{\text{clk}}$ | FPGA base clock | **40, 80, 100, 200** | MHz |
| $T_{\text{clk}}$ | Clock period $= 1/f_{\text{clk}}$ | For 40 MHz, $T_{\text{clk}} = \mathbf{25}$ | ns |
| $J_{\text{wc}}$ | Worst-case latency of SCTL | $= 1 \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | ns |
| $N_{\text{LUT}}$ | Lookup tables used | thousands to millions | count |
| $N_{\text{FF}}$ | Flip-flops used | thousands to millions | count |
| $N_{\text{BRAM}}$ | Block-RAM tiles (18 kb each) | 10 – 500 | count |
| $N_{\text{DSP}}$ | DSP48 slices used | 0 – thousands | count |
| $R_{\text{DMA}}$ | DMA throughput | up to **800 MB/s** (PCIe Gen2 x8) | MB/s |
| $B_{\text{FIFO}}$ | FIFO depth (in samples) | $2^k$ where $k \in [5,16]$ | samples |
| $f_{\text{s,ADC}}$ | Embedded MCU ADC sample rate | $10^3$ – $10^6$ | samples/s |
| $f_{\text{s,PWM}}$ | Embedded MCU PWM frequency | $1$ – $10^5$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Golden Triangle" of LabVIEW FPGA Tradeoffs** — *memorize this for KTU:*
> $$\text{Speed} \;\uparrow \;\;\text{vs}\;\; \text{Resource usage} \;\uparrow \;\;\text{vs}\;\; \text{Compile time} \;\uparrow$$
> Increase clock rate $\Rightarrow$ more timing violations $\Rightarrow$ more logic-pipeline stages $\Rightarrow$ more LUTs and more compile time. This is the **Area–Latency–Compile-Time** triangle.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial motion control:** Servo loops running at **40 kHz** inside the FPGA of a CompactRIO chassis — impossible to achieve on a Windows host.
- **Power-electronics triggering:** IGBT firing pulses with **sub-microsecond** dead-time insertion, deterministic to one **25 ns** tick.
- **Custom communication protocols:** Implementing CAN, SPI, I²S, or even proprietary RF modulations as hardware on the FPGA.
- **Embedded sensor hubs:** Low-power ARM Cortex-M0 firmware generated by LabVIEW Embedded for battery-powered wireless sensor nodes.
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL):** Plant simulation running on an FPGA so the controller-under-test sees a *real* electrical plant, not a mathematical model.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worst-Case Loop Latency of a Single-Cycle Timed Loop

A **Single-Cycle Timed Loop (SCTL)** is the canonical "I need maximum speed" construct in LabVIEW FPGA. We want to derive its worst-case iteration latency so KTU students can quantitatively answer "how fast can my loop go?"

**Given:**
- FPGA base clock frequency $f_{\text{clk}}$ (driven by an on-board crystal or PLL)
- Clock period $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 / f_{\text{clk}}$
- The SCTL contains $N$ combinational logic levels (in LUT cascades)

**Step 1 — Express one clock period in time units.**

For a 40 MHz Xilinx Spartan-6 base clock (a typical CompactRIO-9068 setting):

$$f_{\text{clk}} = 40 \times 10^6 \;\text{Hz}$$

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{40 \times 10^6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{s} = 25 \;\text{ns}$$

**Step 2 — SCTL guarantees the loop body completes in 1 clock cycle.**

The Xilinx place-and-route tool enforces the timing constraint that the *critical path* through the SCTL body must be shorter than $T_{\text{clk}}$. Therefore, even if there are $N$ LUT levels in series, the compiler inserts pipeline registers (or re-tunes logic) so that the propagation delay $t_{pd} \le T_{\text{clk}}$.

$$t_{pd,\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{LUT,i} + t_{wire,i} \le T_{\text{clk}}$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case iteration latency of the SCTL.**

The latency of one loop iteration is the *register-to-register* delay across the loop body, which by construction equals exactly one clock period:

$$L_{\text{SCTL}} = T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$$

For $f_{\text{clk}} = 100$ MHz:

$$L_{\text{SCTL}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{s} = 10 \;\text{ns}$$

**Step 4 — Loop throughput = iterations per second.**

The throughput is the inverse of the iteration latency, since the SCTL back-to-back issues one iteration every clock tick:

$$R_{\text{SCTL}} = \frac{1}{L_{\text{SCTL}}} = f_{\text{clk}}$$

For $f_{\text{clk}} = 100$ MHz:

$$R_{\text{SCTL}} = 100 \times 10^6 \;\text{iterations/second} = 100 \;\text{MIPS}_{\text{hardware}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU answer:** A LabVIEW FPGA SCTL running on a 100 MHz fabric can execute **100 million** hardware iterations per second, with **single-clock** determinism. This is roughly **2–3 orders of magnitude faster** than any software-based deterministic loop on a Windows or RT host.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DMA FIFO Throughput Requirement

When the host VI and FPGA VI exchange data, they communicate over a **DMA FIFO**. The FIFO depth and DMA channel width must be sized so the FPGA never overruns the host. We derive the minimum FIFO depth.

**Given:**
- FPGA-side loop rate $R_{\text{FPGA}}$ (samples per second produced or consumed)
- DMA bus width $W$ (in bytes per transfer; typically 4 or 8 bytes)
- DMA bus frequency $f_{\text{DMA}}$ (e.g., **100 MHz** for PCIe Gen1 x1)
- Host round-trip latency $L_{\text{RT}}$ (time before host reads the FIFO; typically **10 – 50 µs** for a polling loop)

**Step 1 — Maximum DMA transfer rate.**

$$R_{\text{DMA,max}} = W \cdot f_{\text{DMA}}$$

For $W = 8$ bytes and $f_{\text{DMA}} = 100$ MHz:

$$R_{\text{DMA,max}} = 8 \cdot 100 \times 10^6 = 800 \times 10^6 \;\text{bytes/s} = 800 \;\text{MB/s}$$

**Step 2 — Bytes that accumulate in the FIFO during the host round-trip.**

If the FPGA produces samples at $R_{\text{FPGA}}$ samples per second, with each sample being $S$ bytes, the data rate into the FIFO is:

$$R_{\text{in}} = R_{\text{FPGA}} \cdot S \;\text{bytes/s}$$

**Step 3 — Required FIFO depth.**

The FIFO must be deep enough to absorb the in-flowing data for the entire host round-trip, otherwise it will overflow and corrupt data:

$$D_{\text{FIFO,min}} = R_{\text{in}} \cdot L_{\text{RT}} = R_{\text{FPGA}} \cdot S \cdot L_{\text{RT}}$$

*Numerical example:* $R_{\text{FPGA}} = 1$ MSa/s, $S = 2$ bytes, $L_{\text{RT}} = 50\;\mu\text{s}$:

$$D_{\text{FIFO,min}} = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 2 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6} = 100 \;\text{samples}$$

Therefore, the next-power-of-2 FIFO depth to allocate is $2^7 = 128$ samples.

**Step 4 — Overflow safety condition.**

For the system to never overflow, the DMA drain rate must exceed the FPGA fill rate *on average*:

$$R_{\text{DMA,max}} \ge R_{\text{in}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; W \cdot f_{\text{DMA}} \ge R_{\text{FPGA}} \cdot S$$

In our numerical example:

$$800 \times 10^6 \ge 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 2 = 2 \times 10^6 \;\text{bytes/s}$$

The inequality is satisfied with a 400× margin, confirming that the bus is *not* the bottleneck — the host round-trip *is*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compile-Time Scaling Law (Heuristic)

A common KTU question asks: *Why does my LabVIEW FPGA VI take so long to compile?* The answer lies in the place-and-route algorithm, which is **NP-hard**. Empirically, the compile time scales as:

$$T_{\text{compile}} \approx k \cdot N_{\text{LUT}}^{\alpha} \cdot \log(N_{\text{BRAM}})$$

where $\alpha \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ and $k$ is a board-dependent constant. Doubling the LUT count therefore *roughly* quadruples the compile time.

**Step 1 — Compute doubling penalty.**

Let $T_0$ be the compile time at $N_{\text{LUT}} = L$. Then at $N_{\text{LUT}} = 2L$ (with $\alpha = 1.5$):

$$T_1 = k \cdot (2L)^{1.5} = k \cdot 2^{1.5} \cdot L^{1.5} = 2^{1.5} \cdot T_0 \approx 2.83 \cdot T_0$$

At $\alpha = 2$:

$$T_1 = 2^2 \cdot T_0 = 4 \cdot T_0$$

**Step 2 — Practical implication.**

A "small" LabVIEW FPGA VI (~5% LUT utilization) compiles in **5 – 10 minutes**; a "large" one (~70% utilization) takes **1 – 3 hours**. This is why KTU lab examinations on LabVIEW FPGA always provide **pre-compiled bitstreams** rather than asking students to recompile.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FPGA vs Embedded vs Host

| Feature | Host (Windows) | LabVIEW Real-Time | LabVIEW FPGA | LabVIEW Embedded |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|--------------|------------------|
| Execution model | Pre-emptive OS | Pre-emptive RTOS | True hardware parallelism | Sequential / interrupt |
| Clock source | OS tick (~1 ms) | OS tick (~1 µs) | FPGA fabric (25–10 ns) | MCU clock (ns – µs) |
| Determinism | None | Soft (µs) | Hard (1 clock) | Hard (instruction-cycle) |
| Floating-point | Yes (HW FPU) | Yes (HW FPU) | Slow (synth FPU) | Limited / emulated |
| Compile time | Seconds | Seconds | Minutes–Hours | Seconds (C cross-compile) |
| Target hardware | PC, PXI controller | PXI RT, CompactRIO RT | FPGA chip (Zynq, Spartan) | MCU (ARM, MSP430) |
| Power draw | 100 W | 30 W | 1 – 10 W | 0.05 – 1 W |
| Best for | HMI, file I/O | Closed-loop control (1 kHz) | Sub-µs I/O, custom protocols | Battery-powered sensing |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LabVIEW FPGA System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PC["Host PC / Windows Application"]:::host
    HVI["Host VI - User Interface and Analysis"]:::host
    RT["Real-Time Controller - LabVIEW RT"]:::rt
    DMA["DMA Channel - High Bandwidth Bus"]:::comm
    FIFOH["FIFO Buffer - Host Side BRAM"]:::comm
    FIFOF["FIFO Buffer - FPGA Side BRAM"]:::comm
    FVI["FPGA VI - True Hardware Logic"]:::fpga
    SCTL["Single Cycle Timed Loop - 1 Clock Iteration"]:::fpga
    IO["FPGA I/O Nodes - Physical Pins"]:::fpga
    PINS["DIO AI AO PWM Encoder Pins"]:::io

    PC --> HVI
    HVI -->|reads writes| RT
    RT -->|DMA transfers| DMA
    DMA --> FIFOH
    FIFOH <-->|bidirectional| FIFOF
    FIFOF -->|feeds samples| FVI
    FVI --> SCTL
    SCTL --> IO
    IO --> PINS
    PINS -->|external signals| IO

    classDef host fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef rt fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef fpga fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
```

## 4.2 LabVIEW FPGA Compilation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["LabVIEW Block Diagram on PC"]:::src
    B["FPGA VI Source Code .vi"]:::src
    C["HDL Generator - Translates Nodes to VHDL"]:::tool
    D["Xilinx Synthesis Tool XST or Vivado Synth"]:::tool
    E["Optimization - LUT Packing and DSP Inference"]:::tool
    F["Place and Route on FPGA Silicon"]:::tool
    G["Bitstream Generation .bit File"]:::tool
    H["Download via JTAG or Ethernet"]:::tool
    I["FPGA Configuration Memory - Live Hardware"]:::hw
    J["User Circuit Runs at FPGA Clock Rate"]:::hw

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J

    classDef src fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef tool fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 LabVIEW Embedded Toolchain Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["LabVIEW Block Diagram on Host PC"]:::src
    P2["LabVIEW Embedded Module or Microprocessor SDK"]:::tool
    P3["C Code Generator - Translates Diagram to ANSI C"]:::tool
    P4["Cross Compiler - Keil IAR GCC or CCS"]:::tool
    P5["Firmware Image .hex .bin or .elf"]:::tool
    P6["Flash Programmer - JTAG SWD or UART Bootloader"]:::tool
    P7["On Chip Flash Memory of MCU"]:::hw
    P8["Microcontroller Executes Firmware"]:::hw
    P9["On Chip Peripherals - ADC PWM Timer GPIO UART"]:::hw
    P10["External Sensors and Actuators"]:::io

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P9

    classDef src fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef tool fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Target to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Loop rate requirement"}:::dec
    A1["Use LabVIEW FPGA - sub microsecond I/O"]:::fpga
    Q2{"Power budget and form factor"}:::dec

    Q3{"Need floating point math and HMI"}:::dec
    A3["Use LabVIEW Real Time - RTOS controller"]:::rt

    A4["Use Host VI on Windows PC"]:::host

    Q1 -- "above 10 kHz deterministic" --> A1

    Q4 -- "no" --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Arrows denote *data flow / control flow*. Boxes shaded **red** represent FPGA hardware, **green** represent embedded MCU hardware, **blue** represent host software, and **yellow** represent communication / toolchain. This colour convention is reused in KTU model answers and is the convention expected in lab records.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define LabVIEW FPGA. Why is the execution of an FPGA VI considered "true parallel" while a host VI is not?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** LabVIEW FPGA is a National Instruments LabVIEW add-on module that allows the user to program the logic fabric of a Field Programmable Gate Array by drawing a LabVIEW block diagram. The block diagram is compiled into a hardware bitstream that configures the FPGA silicon.

**Parallelism argument (2 marks):** A host VI executes on a CPU under an operating system. Even multiple `While` loops are time-sliced by the OS scheduler — they appear parallel but share one ALU. In contrast, every block on an FPGA VI becomes a *separate physical circuit* in the FPGA fabric. Two parallel `While` loops are literally two independent state machines running at the FPGA clock rate simultaneously, with zero scheduling interference. Hence the parallelism is **physical (spatial)** rather than **logical (temporal)**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**List any three differences between LabVIEW FPGA and LabVIEW Embedded targets.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per correct row):**

| Aspect | LabVIEW FPGA | LabVIEW Embedded |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Target hardware** | FPGA fabric (Xilinx Zynq, Spartan, Kintex) | Microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M, MSP430, AVR) |
| **Code generated** | HDL bitstream (VHDL → bitstream) | C source code → cross-compiled firmware |
| **Execution model** | Spatial parallelism in hardware | Sequential on fixed CPU + interrupts |
| **Determinism** | Single-clock-cycle hard real-time | Instruction-cycle deterministic |
| **Typical clock** | 40 – 200 MHz (FPGA fabric) | 1 – 200 MHz (MCU core) |
| **Compile time** | Minutes to hours | Seconds to minutes |
| **Power** | 0.5 – 10 W | 0.01 – 0.5 W |

*(Any three rows earn full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of LabVIEW FPGA**, identifying the four layers and the role of DMA FIFOs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CompactRIO chassis has an FPGA running at **40 MHz**. An FPGA VI is built using a Single-Cycle Timed Loop (SCTL) that acquires one sample of an analog input per iteration. Calculate **(i)** the clock period, **(ii)** the worst-case iteration latency, **(iii)** the loop throughput in samples/second, and **(iv)** the size of a DMA FIFO (in samples) needed to buffer 50 µs of host round-trip latency. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The LabVIEW FPGA system has **four layers** from top to bottom:

1. **Host Layer (2 marks):** A LabVIEW VI running on a Windows PC or a Real-Time controller. It contains the front-panel UI, file I/O, and high-level analysis. It *cannot* directly toggle FPGA pins; it sends commands to the FPGA via the communication layer.

2. **Communication Layer (2 marks):** Implemented using **DMA FIFOs** (Direct Memory Access First-In-First-Out buffers). A DMA FIFO is a block-RAM queue inside the FPGA fabric that is mapped into the host's physical memory space. The host performs normal memory reads/writes on the FIFO, and a DMA engine on the FPGA bus (PCIe, PXI, or Ethernet) shuttles the data into/out of the FPGA block RAM. DMA FIFOs are bidirectional, support thousands of samples, and can sustain hundreds of MB/s.

3. **FPGA Layer (2 marks):** The FPGA VI itself. It contains the logic that reads/writes the I/O pins. It runs entirely on the FPGA fabric, with single-clock-cycle determinism. It interacts with the host only through DMA FIFOs and interrupts.

4. **I/O Layer (1 mark):** The physical FPGA I/O nodes (DIO, AI, AO, encoder counters, PWM, special-function lines). Each I/O node in the block diagram corresponds to one or more physical pins on the FPGA board.

*(Students should also draw a labelled block diagram here — see Section 4.1 for the reference.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $f_{\text{clk}} = 40\;\text{MHz}$, $L_{\text{RT}} = 50\;\mu\text{s}$, $S = 2$ bytes/sample (assuming 16-bit ADC), $R_{\text{FPGA}} = ?$ (to be determined).

**(i) Clock period (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{40 \times 10^6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{s} = 25\;\text{ns}$$

**(ii) Worst-case iteration latency of the SCTL (1.5 marks):**

A SCTL guarantees the loop body completes in **one clock period**, so:

$$L_{\text{SCTL}} = T_{\text{clk}} = 25\;\text{ns}$$

**(iii) Loop throughput (2 marks):**

$$R_{\text{SCTL}} = \frac{1}{L_{\text{SCTL}}} = f_{\text{clk}} = 40 \times 10^6 \;\text{samples/second} = 40\;\text{MSa/s}$$

**(iv) DMA FIFO depth (2 marks):**

$$D_{\text{FIFO,min}} = R_{\text{FPGA}} \cdot L_{\text{RT}} = 40 \times 10^6 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6} = 2000\;\text{samples}$$

The next power of two is $2^{11} = 2048$ samples, so the FIFO should be sized at **2048 samples** (or 4096 for safety margin).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **LabVIEW Embedded Module** with its toolchain and target hardware. Compare it with LabVIEW FPGA in terms of code generation, execution model, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **compilation pipeline of a LabVIEW FPGA VI** from block diagram to bitstream. Why is the compile time significantly longer than that of a host VI, and what design strategies can be used to keep the compile time reasonable? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — LabVIEW Embedded (7 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The LabVIEW Embedded Module (and the LabVIEW Microprocessor SDK) is a LabVIEW add-on that targets microprocessor-based embedded systems. It generates optimized ANSI C code from a LabVIEW block diagram, which is then cross-compiled by a third-party toolchain (Keil, IAR, GCC) and flashed onto the target MCU.

**Toolchain (2 marks):** The chain is *LabVIEW block diagram → C code generator → cross-compiler (Keil µVision for ARM, IAR, CCS for MSP430, AVR-GCC for AVR) → .hex/.bin firmware → bootloader or JTAG programmer → on-chip flash → execution*.

**Target hardware (1.5 marks):** Common targets include ARM Cortex-M (STM32, NXP LPC, TI Tiva), MSP430, AVR (via LINX toolkit), and NI's own educational SOMs such as **myRIO** and **ELVIS III** for hybrid ARM+FPGA work.

**Comparison with LabVIEW FPGA (2 marks):**
- **Code generation:** FPGA → HDL bitstream; Embedded → C firmware.
- **Execution model:** FPGA → spatial hardware parallelism in LUT fabric; Embedded → sequential instructions on a fixed ALU, with parallelism via interrupts / RTOS.
- **Applications:** FPGA → high-speed deterministic I/O, custom protocols, motion control; Embedded → low-power sensing, battery-powered IoT nodes, simple control loops.

#### Part (b) — FPGA Compilation Pipeline (7 marks)

**The pipeline (3 marks):**
1. **HDL Generation:** Each LabVIEW node is translated into a VHDL construct by the FPGA Module's front-end compiler.
2. **Synthesis:** The Xilinx Vivado/ISE synthesis tool converts VHDL into a gate-level netlist of LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, and DSPs.
3. **Optimization:** Dead-code elimination, register retiming, BRAM inference for shift registers/FIFOs, DSP48 mapping for multipliers.
4. **Place & Route:** The netlist is mapped onto the physical FPGA silicon. This step solves a near-NP-hard combinatorial problem.
5. **Bitstream Generation:** A binary `.bit` file is produced and downloaded.

**Why is the compile so slow? (2 marks):** Place-and-route complexity scales super-linearly (roughly as $N_{\text{LUT}}^{1.5}$ to $N_{\text{LUT}}^{2.0}$). Host VIs compile in seconds because they are simply *bytecode-emitted* by LabVIEW's interpreter; no silicon-routing problem is solved.

**Design strategies to reduce compile time (2 marks):**
1. **Modular design:** Compile smaller sub-VIs separately and reuse the bitstream of unchanged modules.
2. **Avoid feedback outside SCTLs:** Long combinational paths force the tool to do more work.
3. **Use BRAM/DSP inference:** Explicitly mark shift registers and multipliers so the tool maps them to hard IP, freeing LUTs.
4. **Constraint-driven design:** Provide timing constraints (`.xdc`/`.ucf`) so the router does not over-explore the solution space.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "parallel execution" with "concurrent appearance":** Writing that LabVIEW FPGA "runs loops in parallel" *without* explicitly stating that the parallelism is *spatial (hardware)*, not *temporal (CPU scheduling)*, costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the compile-time tradeoff:** Failing to mention that FPGA compile time is in **minutes to hours** (and *why* — place-and-route) is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Mixing up LabVIEW Real-Time and LabVIEW Embedded:** Real-Time targets a full RTOS on a powerful processor; Embedded targets a bare-metal MCU with no OS. Conflating them loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners explicitly check for units in formulas — missing ns/MHz/MB/s costs 0.5 to 1 mark per question.
> 5. **Drawing the block diagram without labels:** A block diagram without arrow labels, layer names, or DMA FIFO annotations earns only partial credit on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **LabVIEW FPGA** is the add-on module that compiles a LabVIEW block diagram into an FPGA **bitstream** and runs it on the FPGA fabric. *(Must remember.)*
- **LabVIEW Embedded** is the add-on module that compiles a LabVIEW block diagram into **C code** and runs it on an **MCU**. *(Must remember.)*
- The **four layers** of a LabVIEW FPGA system are: **Host → Communication (DMA FIFOs) → FPGA → I/O pins**. *(Frequently asked.)*
- An **SCTL (Single-Cycle Timed Loop)** guarantees one loop iteration per **FPGA clock cycle** — the fastest possible construct. *(High-yield.)*
- A standard SCTL iteration latency is $T_{\text{clk}} = 1/f_{\text{clk}}$. For **40 MHz** ⇒ **25 ns**, for **100 MHz** ⇒ **10 ns**. *(Numerical question staple.)*
- **DMA FIFOs** are block-RAM-based queues that buffer data between host and FPGA. Their minimum depth to avoid overflow is $D_{\text{FIFO,min}} = R_{\text{FPGA}} \cdot S \cdot L_{\text{RT}}$. *(Standard formula.)*
- FPGA compile time is **minutes to hours** because place-and-route is **NP-hard**; host VI compile is **seconds** because it is bytecode emission. *(Almost always asked.)*
- **myRIO** and **ELVIS III** are *hybrid* targets containing both an **ARM** CPU and an **FPGA** fabric (Zynq SoC). *(Confusion-trap question.)*
- **True hardware parallelism** in FPGAs vs **logical parallelism** in CPUs is the single most important conceptual distinction. *(Mandatory for any 7+ mark question.)*
- **NI ships pre-compiled bitstreams** to KTU labs to spare students the 1–3 hour recompile. *(Practical lab note.)*
- The **Area–Latency–Compile-Time** triangle is the master tradeoff of FPGA design. *(Optional but high-yield bonus point.)*
- **CompactRIO** is the canonical KTU reference for an FPGA + RT + I/O module stack used in industrial Virtual Instrumentation. *(Frequently named in questions.)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET833 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Data Acquisition System (DAQ)** is an integrated, modular electronic instrumentation framework that converts real-world physical phenomena — such as temperature, pressure, flow, strain, light intensity, voltage, and current — into a digital format that can be processed, stored, displayed, and analyzed by a computer or embedded controller. In the context of KTU's **Virtual Instrumentation (OEAET833)** framework, the DAQ hardware forms the critical *physical layer* that bridges the analog physical world with the software-defined virtual instrument running on a PC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Data Acquisition System is defined as the collective hardware and software arrangement responsible for sensing, conditioning, converting, and transferring measurement data from transducers to a processing unit. It typically comprises **transducers, signal conditioning circuits, an analog-to-digital converter (ADC), a digital controller/PC interface, and application software** (e.g., LabVIEW).

> [!NOTE]
> The acronym **DAQ** is interchangeable with **DAS (Data Acquisition System)** in KTU question papers — do not be confused if a question uses either term.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a DAQ system as the **"senses and nervous system" of a computer**. A human being:
1. **Senses** the world through eyes, ears, and skin (analog signals).
2. The **spinal cord/brain** processes these signals (signal conditioning and processing).
3. The brain **stores memories** and **reacts** by speaking or writing (digital output and display).

A DAQ does exactly this for a computer:
1. **Transducers/Sensors** are the "senses" — they convert physical phenomena into analog electrical signals (e.g., a thermocouple converts temperature into millivolts).
2. **Signal Conditioning circuits** are the "nervous system" — they amplify, filter, isolate, and linearize the tiny, noisy raw signals.
3. **The ADC** is the "translator" — it converts continuous analog voltage levels into discrete digital numbers.
4. **The PC + Virtual Instrument Software** (LabVIEW) is the "brain" — it interprets, displays, logs, and acts on the data.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Remember the chain as **T-S-C-D-S** → **T**ransducer → **S**ignal Conditioning → **C**onversion (ADC) → **D**igital transfer → **S**oftware processing.

## 1.3 Why DAQ is Central to Virtual Instrumentation

In traditional instruments (analog meters, oscilloscopes with CRTs), every function is *hardwired*. In **Virtual Instrumentation**, the hardware is minimized and most intelligence is pushed into software. The DAQ card is the *only physical gateway* through which real-world analog data enters the software world — making it the **single most critical component** in any VI-based experiment.

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Units

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Typical Range in DAQ |
|---|---|---|
| Analog Input Voltage | Volts (V) | ±10 V, ±5 V, 0–10 V |
| Sampling Rate | Samples/second (S/s) | 1 kS/s to 2 MS/s (board dependent) |
| ADC Resolution | Bits | 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 24 bits |
| Transducer Output | mV, µV, mA, Ω | Depends on sensor |
| Bandwidth | Hertz (Hz) | DC to 1 MHz typical |
| Common-Mode Voltage | Volts | ±10 V to ±250 V isolation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Modern DAQ

A DAQ system, regardless of manufacturer (NI, Agilent/Keysight, Advantech, Data Translation), follows a canonical architecture consisting of **five sequential functional blocks**:

### Block 1 — Transducer / Sensor Front-End
- The transducer is the *first physical contact* with the measurand.
- It produces an electrical signal proportional to the physical quantity.
- **Types of output signals:**
  - **Voltage** (e.g., thermocouple → mV, photovoltaic cell → V)
  - **Current** (e.g., 4–20 mA industrial loop)
  - **Resistance change** (e.g., RTD, thermistor, strain gauge)
  - **Capacitance / Inductance change** (e.g., LVDT, capacitive level sensor)
  - **Frequency / Pulse** (e.g., turbine flow meter, digital encoder)

### Block 2 — Signal Conditioning
Raw transducer outputs are typically **too small, too noisy, and incompatible** with the ADC input range. The signal conditioning block performs:

1. **Amplification** — Boosts µV/mV signals to the 0–10 V or ±10 V range expected by the ADC. Instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD620, INA128) with high CMRR (≥ 100 dB) are used.
2. **Filtering** — Low-pass anti-aliasing filters remove frequency components above the Nyquist limit. Bessel, Butterworth, and Chebyshev filters are commonly used.
3. **Isolation** — Galvanic isolation (using optocouplers or transformers) protects the PC and ADC from high-voltage transients and ground loops.
4. **Linearization** — Corrects the non-linear response of sensors (e.g., thermocouple voltage vs. temperature is non-linear per NIST polynomial tables).
5. **Excitation** — Provides stable power to *passive* sensors (e.g., 4-wire RTD needs constant current excitation; strain gauge needs a Wheatstone bridge excitation voltage).
6. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — For thermocouples, the temperature of the reference junction is measured and software-corrected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Signal conditioning is often considered *more important than the ADC itself*. A 24-bit ADC connected to a noisy, unconditioned signal is worse than a 12-bit ADC with proper amplification and filtering.

### Block 3 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
The ADC is the *heart* of the DAQ. It samples the conditioned analog signal and outputs binary codes. Key ADC architectures used in DAQ:

| ADC Type | Resolution | Speed | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash ADC | 6–8 bits | Very High (GS/s) | Oscilloscopes, radar |
| Successive Approximation (SAR) | 12–18 bits | Medium (kS/s–MS/s) | General-purpose DAQ (most common) |
| Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) | 16–24 bits | Low (kS/s) | High-precision, low-frequency (strain, temperature) |
| Dual-Slope Integrating | 16–22 bits | Very Slow | Digital multimeters |
| Pipeline ADC | 12–16 bits | High (MS/s) | High-speed data acquisition |

### Block 4 — Digital Controller / PC Interface
- Manages timing, triggering, DMA transfers, and bus communication.
- **Interface standards:** PCI, PCIe, PXI, USB, Ethernet (LXI), GPIB, RS-232/485.
- **Buffering:** On-board FIFO memory handles bursts while the PC is busy with other tasks.

### Block 5 — Application Software (Virtual Instrument Layer)
- LabVIEW, MATLAB, Python (with PyDAQmx), or custom C/C++ code.
- Performs display, logging, analysis (FFT, filtering, statistics), and control decisions.

## 2.2 The Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** To accurately reconstruct a band-limited analog signal from its samples, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be **at least twice the highest frequency component** $f_{max}$ present in the signal.

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

The minimum allowable sampling rate $2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. In practice, engineers use an **oversampling factor of 5× to 10×** to allow for realizable anti-aliasing filter roll-off:

$$f_s^{practical} = (5 \text{ to } 10) \times f_{max}$$

**Aliasing** occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{max}$. High-frequency components "fold back" and appear as false low-frequency signals. This is **irreversible** — once aliased, the data is corrupted.

## 2.3 Quantization & Resolution

The ADC divides its full-scale input range into $2^n$ discrete steps, where $n$ is the number of bits.

**Quantization Step (LSB size):**

$$\Delta V = Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage range (e.g., 10 V peak-to-peak = 10 V for unipolar, 20 V for bipolar ±10 V).

**Quantization Error:** Theoretically bounded between $\pm \frac{Q}{2}$ (assuming ideal ADC with mid-rise quantizer).

**Theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a full-scale sine wave:**

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB}$$

This is a **canonical KTU formula** — memorize it.

| Bits (n) | Levels ($2^n$) | SQNR (dB) | 10 V Range Q (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 256 | 49.92 | 39.06 |
| 12 | 4096 | 73.99 | 2.44 |
| 16 | 65536 | 98.08 | 0.153 |
| 24 | 16,777,216 | 146.24 | 0.000596 |

## 2.4 Aperture Time & Aperture Uncertainty

- **Aperture Time ($t_a$):** The finite time the ADC's sample-and-hold switch takes to acquire the input voltage.
- **Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter, $t_j$):** Random variation in the sampling instant due to clock noise.
- The maximum input slew rate that can be digitized without jitter-induced error is:

$$f_{max}^{jitter} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot t_j \cdot 2^n}$$

This is why high-speed, high-resolution ADCs demand ultra-low-jitter clocks.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem |
| 2 | $f_s^{practical} = 5 \text{ to } 10 \times f_{max}$ | Engineering rule for anti-aliasing |
| 3 | $Q = \Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | Quantization step (LSB size) |
| 4 | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | Theoretical SNR for sine input |
| 5 | $e_q = \pm Q/2$ | Maximum quantization error |
| 6 | $V_{in} = D \cdot Q$ (D = digital code) | Reconstructed input voltage |
| 7 | $f_{max}^{jitter} = 1/(2\pi t_j 2^n)$ | Max frequency limited by clock jitter |
| 8 | $V_{CMR} = 10^{\text{CMRR}/20}$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio to voltage ratio |
| 9 | $t_{conv} \propto 1/f_s$ | Conversion time inversely related to throughput |
| 10 | $BW_{-3dB} = 0.35 / t_{rise}$ | Bandwidth to rise-time relation (analog stage) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** In the formula table above, absolute values and divisions are written using `/` and parentheses. **Never write `\|x\|` inside a markdown table** — the renderer breaks. Always use `\vert x \vert` or `mod(x)`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

DAQ systems are indispensable in:
- **Process Industries:** SCADA systems, 4–20 mA loop monitoring.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Strain gauges on bridges and aircraft.
- **Biomedical:** ECG, EEG, EMG acquisition (often 24-bit Σ-Δ at 1 kS/s).
- **Automotive Testing:** Engine control, crash test instrumentation.
- **Energy:** Power quality monitoring, smart grid, renewable inverter testing.
- **Research Labs:** Particle physics, vibration analysis, audio engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Quantization Step & Dynamic Range

**Problem Statement:** A 14-bit DAQ card is configured for a bipolar input range of $\pm 10$ V. Determine:
(a) The quantization step size (LSB voltage).
(b) The theoretical SQNR in dB.
(c) The maximum quantization error.
(d) The digital output code when the input voltage is $+3.42$ V.

### Step (a): Quantization Step Size

The full-scale range for a bipolar $\pm 10$ V input is:

$$V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min} = (+10) - (-10) = 20 \text{ V}$$

Number of discrete levels for $n = 14$ bits:

$$L = 2^n = 2^{14} = 16{,}384 \text{ levels}$$

Therefore, the quantization step (LSB) is:

$$Q = \Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{20 \text{ V}}{16{,}384} = 1.2207 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

**Interpretation:** Each increment of the binary output code corresponds to a 1.22 mV change in input voltage.

### Step (b): Theoretical SQNR

For a full-scale sine wave, applying the canonical SQNR relation:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Interpretation:** The signal is approximately 86 dB above the quantization noise floor — equivalent to a voltage ratio of:

$$V_{ratio} = 10^{86.04/20} = 10^{4.302} \approx 20{,}045 \text{ : } 1$$

### Step (c): Maximum Quantization Error

$$e_q^{max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{1.2207 \text{ mV}}{2} = \pm 0.6104 \text{ mV}$$

**Interpretation:** Any measured voltage carries an unavoidable uncertainty of up to 0.61 mV due to finite ADC resolution. No amount of averaging or filtering can reduce this *inherent* error.

### Step (d): Digital Output Code for $+3.42$ V

The general transfer relation (for bipolar, offset-binary or two's-complement coding) is:

$$D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{Q} + 2^{n-1}\right)$$

where $2^{n-1}$ is the midpoint offset for bipolar range.

Substituting:

$$D = \text{round}\left(\frac{+3.42}{1.2207 \times 10^{-3}} + 8192\right)$$

$$D = \text{round}\left(2801.86 + 8192\right) = \text{round}\left(10{,}993.86\right)$$

$$D = 10{,}994$$

Converting to 14-bit binary: 
$$D = 10{,}994_{10} = 0010 1010 1111 0010_2$$

**Verification (reconstructing voltage):**

$$V_{in}^{reconstructed} = \left(D - 2^{n-1}\right) \cdot Q = (10{,}994 - 8192) \times 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

$$V_{in}^{reconstructed} = 2802 \times 1.2207 \text{ mV} = 3.4204 \text{ V}$$

This matches the input to within 0.4 mV — within the theoretical $\pm Q/2$ bound.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Sampling Theorem Application

**Problem Statement:** A vibration sensor on a motor outputs a signal with dominant frequency components up to 4.5 kHz, plus low-amplitude noise above 10 kHz. The DAQ card is configured with a sampling rate of 10 kS/s. Determine:
(a) Whether the sampling rate satisfies the Nyquist criterion.
(b) The expected aliasing behaviour.
(c) The minimum sampling rate that strictly satisfies Nyquist.
(d) The recommended practical sampling rate.

### Step (a): Nyquist Check

Nyquist rate:

$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 4500 = 9000 \text{ Hz} = 9 \text{ kS/s}$$

Configured rate $f_s = 10 \text{ kS/s}$.

Since $10 \text{ kS/s} \geq 9 \text{ kS/s}$, the Nyquist criterion is **technically satisfied** for the 4.5 kHz band.

### Step (b): Aliasing Behaviour

The Nyquist frequency (half of $f_s$) is:

$$f_{N} = f_s / 2 = 10{,}000 / 2 = 5 \text{ kHz}$$

Noise components between 4.5 kHz and 10 kHz will alias into the band $[0, 5] \text{ kHz}$ as follows:

$$f_{aliased} = \vert f - m f_s \vert \quad \text{for the integer } m \text{ that puts } f_{aliased} \in [0, f_N]$$

For example, a 6 kHz noise component aliases to:

$$f_{aliased} = \vert 6000 - 1 \times 10000 \vert = 4000 \text{ Hz}$$

This 4 kHz tone is *not present in the original signal* but will appear in the digital record — an alias artefact.

### Step (c): Minimum Nyquist Rate

$$f_s^{min} = 2 \times 4500 = 9 \text{ kS/s}$$

### Step (d): Practical Sampling Rate

Applying the 5×–10× engineering rule:

$$f_s^{practical} = 5 \times 4500 = 22.5 \text{ kS/s (lower bound)}$$

$$f_s^{practical} = 10 \times 4500 = 45 \text{ kS/s (upper recommended bound)}$$

**Recommendation:** Use $f_s = 50$ kS/s, combined with a sharp **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff at 4.5 kHz (or 5 kHz with adequate roll-off, e.g., 8th-order Butterworth).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The signal-of-interest dictates the filter cutoff, *not* the ADC sampling rate. The ADC must sample *faster* than 2× the filter cutoff, but the cutoff is what prevents aliasing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DAQ Specification Comparison

| Specification | NI USB-6363 | NI PXIe-5105 | Low-Cost Arduino Due ADC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 16 bits | 12 bits | 12 bits |
| Max Sampling Rate | 2 MS/s (1 ch) | 60 MS/s | 1 MS/s |
| Input Range | ±1 V to ±10 V | ±5 V, ±25 V | 0 – 3.3 V |
| Channels | 32 SE / 16 DI | 8 SE | 12 (analog) |
| Quantization Step (10 V) | 152.6 µV | 2.44 mV | 0.806 mV |
| SQNR | 98.08 dB | 73.99 dB | 73.99 dB |
| Interface | USB, USB-3.0 | PXIe | MCU native |
| Triggering | Digital, analog | Digital, analog, window | Software only |
| Best Use Case | General lab, mixed signal | High-speed transient capture | Education, prototyping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"which DAQ is suitable for measuring microvolt-level thermocouple signals?"*, the answer is **highest resolution (≥ 16-bit Σ-Δ) with built-in signal conditioning**, *not* the highest sampling rate. Resolution and sampling rate are independent trade-offs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical DAQ System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Phenomenon\nTemperature Pressure Strain] --> B[Transducer / Sensor\nThermocouple RTD Load Cell]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning\nAmplification Filter Isolation Linearization Excitation CJC]
    C --> D[Analog-to-Digital Converter\nSAR Sigma-Delta Pipeline]
    D --> E[Digital Controller\nFIFO DMA Timing Engine]
    E --> F[PC Interface\nUSB PCI PCIe PXI Ethernet]
    F --> G[Virtual Instrument Software\nLabVIEW MATLAB Python]
    G --> H[Display and Storage\nWaveform Chart File Logging Database]
    H --> I[Analysis and Decision\nFFT Statistics Control Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows the **unidirectional information flow** from the physical world to the digital processor. In **closed-loop** control applications, an additional **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)** feeds back from the software to the physical actuator (shown in the next diagram).

## 4.2 Closed-Loop DAQ + Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Process / Plant] -->|Measurand| T1[Transducer]
    T1 --> SC1[Signal Conditioning]
    SC1 --> ADC1[ADC]
    ADC1 --> PC1[PC Virtual Instrument]
    PC1 -->|Display Log Analyze| OUT1[Operator / Storage]
    PC1 -->|Control Law PID| DAC1[DAC]
    DAC1 --> PA[Power Amplifier]
    PA -->|Actuator Signal| P
```

## 4.3 Sampling Process Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuous Analog Signal\nx of t] --> B[Sample and Hold\nSwitch closes at fs]
    B --> C[Quantizer\nRounds to nearest Q]
    C --> D[Binary Encoder\nOutputs N bit code]
    D --> E[Digital Word\nD of n]
```

## 4.4 Aliasing Effect Demonstration (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Original 6 kHz sine] --> M1[Sampler at fs = 5 kS per s]
    M1 --> R1[Reconstructed signal appears as 1 kHz sine]
    S2[Original 9 kHz sine] --> M2[Sampler at fs = 5 kS per s]
    M2 --> R2[Reconstructed signal appears as 1 kHz sine]
    R1 --> MERGE[Identical reconstructed signals]
    R2 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> X[AMBIGUITY: cannot distinguish original frequencies]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** This figure illustrates why **anti-aliasing filtering MUST precede the ADC**, not after. Once aliasing occurs, the original frequency information is mathematically unrecoverable.

## 4.5 Multi-Channel DAQ Configuration Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SE[Single-Ended Mode - 16 channels]
        SE1[CH0+] --> MUX1[Analog Multiplexer]
        SE2[CH1+] --> MUX1
        SEN[CH15+] --> MUX1
        GND1[Common Ground] --> MUX1
    end
    subgraph DI[Differential Mode - 8 channels]
        DI1[CH0+] --> MUX2[Analog Multiplexer]
        DI2[CH0-] --> MUX2
        DI3[CH1+] --> MUX2
        DI4[CH1-] --> MUX2
        DIN[CH7-] --> MUX2
    end
    MUX1 --> AMP1[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    MUX2 --> AMP2[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP1 --> ADC1[Shared ADC]
    AMP2 --> ADC2[Shared ADC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential mode** rejects common-mode noise and ground loops, preferred for low-level signals (thermocouples, strain gauges). **Single-ended mode** doubles the channel count and is adequate for high-level signals (> 1 V) in clean environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define a Data Acquisition System. List its main functional blocks.*

**Model Answer:**
A **Data Acquisition System (DAQ)** is an integrated instrumentation system that converts physical phenomena into digital data for processing, storage, and analysis by a computer.

**Main Functional Blocks:**
1. **Transducer / Sensor** — converts the physical quantity into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — amplifies, filters, isolates, and linearizes the raw signal.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts the conditioned analog signal into digital codes.
4. **Digital Controller / PC Interface** — manages data transfer (USB, PCI, PXI, Ethernet).
5. **Application Software** — virtual instrument (LabVIEW) for display, analysis, and control.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?*

**Model Answer:**
**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed from its samples without loss of information **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Aliasing:** It is the phenomenon in which frequency components above $f_s/2$ "fold back" into the lower frequency band, producing false signals that were not present in the original analog signal.

**Prevention:** By placing a **sharp low-pass anti-aliasing filter** (cutoff $\leq f_s/2$) *before* the ADC, all frequency components above the Nyquist limit are removed prior to sampling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a modern Data Acquisition System. Describe the role of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used in a DAQ system with an input voltage range of 0 to +10 V. Determine:
   (i) The quantization step size (LSB).
   (ii) The maximum quantization error.
   (iii) The theoretical SQNR in dB.
   (iv) The digital output code for an input of 6.37 V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The block diagram is identical to the one shown in **Section 4.1**. The detailed role of each block:

1. **Transducer:** Converts physical quantity (temperature, pressure, displacement) into an electrical signal. Example — K-type thermocouple produces ~40 µV/°C.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Includes amplification (instrumentation amplifier with gain 100–1000), low-pass filtering (anti-aliasing), isolation (optocoupler), and excitation for passive sensors.
3. **ADC:** Performs sampling and quantization; outputs an N-bit binary word at the rate $f_s$.
4. **Digital Controller:** Handles timing, triggers, DMA transfer, and protocol conversion (USB/PCI).
5. **Application Software:** LabVIEW VI for real-time display, logging, FFT analysis, alarm generation, and feedback control.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{range} = 0$ to $+10$ V (unipolar).

**(i) Quantization Step Size:**

$$Q = \Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{4096} = 2.4414 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$e_q^{max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Theoretical SQNR:**

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Digital Output Code for $V_{in} = 6.37$ V:**

For unipolar range (0 to 10 V), the transfer relation is:

$$D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{Q}\right) = \text{round}\left(\frac{6.37}{2.4414 \times 10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$D = \text{round}\left(2609.05\right) = 2609$$

Binary representation: $D = 2609_{10} = 1010 0011 0001_2$

**Verification:**
$$V_{in}^{reconstructed} = 2609 \times 2.4414 \text{ mV} = 6.3700 \text{ V}$$ — matches within 0.05 mV.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of signal conditioning in a DAQ system. Describe any four signal conditioning operations with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the Nyquist sampling theorem:
   (i) State the theorem.
   (ii) An audio signal contains frequency components up to 18 kHz. Calculate the minimum sampling rate and the recommended practical sampling rate.
   (iii) Explain what happens if the signal is sampled at 30 kS/s. Identify the frequency of the alias of a 22 kHz component. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Signal Conditioning** is the process of modifying a raw transducer signal to make it suitable for the ADC. It is essential because transducer outputs are typically low-amplitude, noisy, non-linear, and may contain high-frequency interference.

**Four Signal Conditioning Operations:**

1. **Amplification:** A K-type thermocouple outputs 40 µV/°C; for a 200 °C measurement, output is only 8 mV. An instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620) with gain = 250 boosts it to 2 V for the ADC.

2. **Filtering:** A 4th-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff at 100 Hz removes 50/60 Hz mains pickup and broadband noise from a strain gauge signal.

3. **Isolation:** A 1.5 kV optocoupler isolates the PC's ground from the high-voltage side of a motor current sensor, protecting the DAQ and the user.

4. **Linearization:** A thermistor's resistance vs. temperature is exponential. The Steinhart–Hart equation is used in software to linearize the output.

*(Additional acceptable operations: Excitation, Cold-Junction Compensation, Multiplexing, Range Switching.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Nyquist Theorem:** Already stated in Part A Q2.

**(ii) Minimum Sampling Rate:**

$$f_s^{min} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 18 \text{ kHz} = 36 \text{ kS/s}$$

This is the standard for **CD-quality audio** (44.1 kS/s is used in practice).

**Practical Sampling Rate (using 5×–10× rule):**

$$f_s^{practical} = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ kS/s (lower)}$$

$$f_s^{practical} = 10 \times 18 = 180 \text{ kS/s (upper recommended)}$$

For audio, $f_s = 192$ kS/s is common in professional studio DAQ.

**(iii) Sampling at $f_s = 30$ kS/s:**

The Nyquist frequency is:

$$f_N = f_s / 2 = 30{,}000 / 2 = 15 \text{ kHz}$$

Since 18 kHz > 15 kHz, **aliasing will occur**.

**Frequency of the alias of a 22 kHz component:**

$$f_{aliased} = \vert f - f_s \vert = \vert 22{,}000 - 30{,}000 \vert = 8000 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

The 22 kHz tone will appear as an 8 kHz tone in the digitized data. This 8 kHz is *not* in the original signal but is indistinguishable from a real 8 kHz component after sampling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing sampling rate with Nyquist rate** — The Nyquist rate is $2 f_{max}$ (a frequency in Hz), the sampling rate is $f_s$ (in samples/second). They are NOT the same.
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter** — Many students describe the ADC block but ignore that the analog low-pass filter is *mandatory* before sampling.
> 3. **Off-by-one error in bipolar code conversion** — For bipolar input, the formula is $D = V_{in}/Q + 2^{n-1}$. Writing $D = V_{in}/Q$ without the offset is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Mixing up resolution with accuracy** — Resolution (LSB size) ≠ measurement accuracy. A 16-bit ADC is NOT 16-bit accurate; non-linearity, gain error, and offset error reduce effective accuracy to typically 14–15 bits.
> 5. **Writing $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ instead of $\geq$** — The theorem requires "greater than or equal to". Writing "$=$" ignores the edge case.
> 6. **Using a 5× rule without justification** — When asked for a *practical* rate, do not just write 5×; explain that it is required for realizable anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **DAQ** = Transducer + Signal Conditioning + ADC + Digital Interface + Software.
- The **canonical KTU 5-block architecture** must be drawn (and labeled) for full marks on any 7-mark architecture question.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — sampling rate must be **at least** twice the highest signal frequency.
- **Aliasing** is prevented only by an **analog low-pass filter placed before the ADC**; software filtering afterwards cannot recover lost information.
- **Quantization step:** $Q = V_{FS} / 2^n$. Memorize the formula and the units (Volts).
- **SQNR for sine wave:** $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ dB — this is THE most-asked formula in DAQ problems.
- **Bipolar vs. Unipolar transfer functions:** For unipolar (0 to $V_{FS}$), $D = V_{in}/Q$. For bipolar ($\pm V_{FS}/2$), $D = V_{in}/Q + 2^{n-1}$.
- **Aperture jitter** limits the maximum input frequency at high resolutions; ultra-low-jitter clocks are essential for 16+ bit high-speed ADCs.
- **SAR ADC** dominates general-purpose DAQ; **Σ-Δ ADC** dominates precision low-frequency (DC and audio); **Flash ADC** is for ultra-high-speed transient capture.
- **Signal conditioning** is often the most critical (and most overlooked) block — amplifies µV signals, filters noise, isolates grounds, linearizes response.
- **Differential inputs** reject common-mode noise; **single-ended inputs** double channel count but are noise-prone.
- **Sampling rate vs. Resolution** are *independent trade-offs* — high resolution does NOT imply high speed, and vice versa.
- **Anti-aliasing filter cutoff** should be at $f_{max}$ (the signal bandwidth), and sampling rate should be at least 5×–10× this cutoff to allow realizable filter roll-off.
- For thermocouples, **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** is mandatory; software correction uses NIST polynomial tables.
- **4–20 mA current loop** is the industrial standard for noise-immune long-distance signal transmission.
- **Interface standards** to remember: USB (general), PCI/PCIe (legacy desktop), PXI/PXIe (modular instrumentation), Ethernet/LXI (distributed), GPIB (legacy instrument control).
- **DAS = DAQ** — same thing; do not be confused by either term in KTU question papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Data Acquisition on PC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET833 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Data Acquisition on PC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of sampling real-world physical phenomena (such as temperature, pressure, vibration, voltage, or current) and converting the resulting analog signals into a digital format that a Personal Computer (PC) can process, store, analyze, and visualize. In the context of **Virtual Instrumentation (VI)**, a PC-based DAQ system replaces traditional bench-top instruments by leveraging the PC's CPU, memory, display, and software to perform measurement tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEAET833):**
> *Data Acquisition on PC refers to the measurement chain that captures physical parameters through transducers, conditions the resulting electrical signals, converts them into digital form via an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), and transfers the digitized samples to a PC for real-time processing, display, and storage using Virtual Instrumentation software (e.g., LabVIEW).*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **Universal Translator** standing between two people who speak different languages:
- **Person A** (the physical world) speaks in continuous, analog whispers — *"It is 36.5 °C here,"* *"The motor vibrates at 50 Hz."*
- **Person B** (the PC) only understands binary digits (0s and 1s).

The DAQ system acts as that translator. The **microphone ear-pickup (transducer)** listens to Person A, the **language dictionary (signal conditioner)** cleans the noise, the **stenographer (ADC)** writes down every snippet as numbers, and finally, the **PC's brain (CPU + LabVIEW)** interprets, plots, and saves the translated message.

Without the DAQ chain, the PC would be deaf to the physical world.

### 1.3 Why DAQ is the Heart of Virtual Instrumentation

| Function | Traditional Instrument | PC-Based DAQ (VI) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Capture | Built-in hardware | Plug-in card / external chassis |
| Processing | Fixed ASIC / firmware | Software on PC CPU |
| Display | Built-in CRT/LCD | PC Monitor (flexible GUI) |
| Storage | Limited internal RAM | Hard disk / SSD (terabytes) |
| User Interface | Knobs & buttons | Graphical Front Panel (VI) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A Virtual Instrument is only as good as its DAQ hardware. *"Garbage In = Garbage Out."* A poorly designed front panel on a noisy ADC still gives wrong readings.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used Throughout DAQ

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of measured value to true value (expressed in % of Full Scale).
- **Precision:** Repeatability of measurement under identical conditions.
- **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change the ADC can distinguish, expressed in **bits** (typically **8, 12, 14, 16, 18, or 24 bits**).
- **Sampling Rate (fs):** Number of samples acquired per second, expressed in **Samples/second (S/s)** or **kS/s, MS/s**.
- **Dynamic Range:** Ratio between the largest and smallest measurable signal, expressed in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Throughput:** Aggregate rate at which the system processes multiple channels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage DAQ Measurement Chain

Every PC-based Data Acquisition system follows a fixed, sequential measurement chain:

1. **Transduction (Sensor Stage)** — Physical quantity → Electrical signal
2. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization
3. **Multiplexing (Optional)** — Multiple channels share a single ADC
4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — Analog signal → Digital code
5. **PC Interface & Software** — Bus transfer + LabVIEW/MATLAB processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"List the components of a PC-based DAQ system."* The expected answer must contain all five stages above in this exact order.

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Stage

#### Stage 1 — Transducers / Sensors
A transducer converts one form of energy into another. The most common are:
- **Temperature:** Thermocouple, RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector), Thermistor, IC sensor (LM35).
- **Pressure:** Strain gauge, Piezoelectric sensor.
- **Flow:** Orifice plate, Turbine meter, Ultrasonic flowmeter.
- **Vibration:** Piezoelectric accelerometer.
- **Light:** Photodiode, LDR (Light Dependent Resistor).
- **Position:** LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer), Rotary encoder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Output Types:** Most transducers produce either **voltage** (mV to V), **current (4–20 mA)**, **resistance change**, or **frequency/pulse output**.

#### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning
Raw sensor output is rarely suitable for direct ADC input. Conditioning includes:
- **Amplification** — Low-level thermocouple signals (~µV) must be boosted.
- **Filtering** — Anti-aliasing low-pass filter removes frequencies above Nyquist limit.
- **Isolation** — Protects PC from high-voltage transients (optical or transformer isolation).
- **Linearization** — Converts non-linear sensor curves (e.g., thermocouple Type K table) into linear mV/°C mapping.
- **Excitation** — Provides current or voltage to power passive sensors (e.g., 4-wire RTD excitation, strain gauge bridge excitation).
- **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — Required for thermocouples.

#### Stage 3 — Multiplexing
In a multi-channel system, an analog multiplexer (MUX) sequentially switches multiple input channels into a single ADC. **Trade-off:** Cheaper (one ADC), but reduced per-channel sampling rate.

$$
f_{channel} = \frac{f_{ADC}}{N_{channels}}
$$

#### Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
Three common ADC architectures:
- **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** — Most common in DAQ boards, medium speed (kS/s to MS/s), high resolution (12–18 bits).
- **Flash ADC** — Very fast (GS/s), low resolution (6–8 bits), used in oscilloscopes.
- **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ)** — Very high resolution (16–24 bits), slow, used in DMMs and audio.

Conversion types: **Unipolar (0 to +10 V)** or **Bipolar (−10 V to +10 V)**.

#### Stage 5 — PC Interface Bus & Software
- **Internal buses:** PCI, PCIe (high bandwidth, low latency).
- **External buses:** USB, RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet, GPIB, PXI, PXIe.
- **Software stack:** Device driver (VISA, NI-DAQmx) → API → Application (LabVIEW VI).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts |
| 3 | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | Levels |
| 4 | Theoretical SNR of ADC | $SNR = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 5 | Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) | $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Bits |
| 6 | Per-channel Sampling Rate | $f_{ch} = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Throughput (Multi-channel) | $T = f_s \cdot N_{channels} \cdot N_{bytes}$ | Bytes/s |
| 8 | Transfer Time on Bus | $t_{tr} = \dfrac{N_{samples} \cdot N_{bytes}}{B_{bus}}$ | Seconds |
| 9 | Dynamic Range | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | dB |
| 10 | Alias Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{in} \vert$ (when $f_{in} > f_s/2$) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary:** When $f_{in} > f_s/2$, **aliasing occurs** — the high-frequency signal folds back and masquerades as a low-frequency signal. Always use an **anti-aliasing filter** before the ADC.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Industrial Process Control:** SCADA systems in oil refineries monitor 1000+ channels.
- **Biomedical:** ECG, EEG, EMG acquisition cards for patient monitoring.
- **Automotive Test:** Engine dynamometer DAQ at 100 kS/s per cylinder.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Bridge vibration analysis using accelerometers.
- **Audio & Acoustics:** 24-bit Σ-Δ ADC at 192 kS/s for studio recording.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Step Size (LSB) of an ADC

Consider an ideal ADC with a bipolar input range $V_{FSR}$ (Full-Scale Range) and $n$ bits of resolution.

**Step 1:** The total number of discrete output codes is determined by $n$:
$$
L = 2^n
$$

**Step 2:** These $L$ levels span the entire Full-Scale Range $V_{FSR}$ symmetrically. The width of one Least Significant Bit (LSB) — the smallest distinguishable voltage increment — equals:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{L}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $L = 2^n$:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}
$$

**Step 4:** For practical ADCs, the highest code is $2^n - 1$ (not $2^n$), so the LSB is:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $2^n - 1$ instead of $2^n$?** Because $n$ bits can represent $2^n$ codes (0 to $2^n-1$), and the maximum code is one LSB below $V_{FSR}$. Example: An 8-bit ADC with $V_{FSR} = 10\text{ V}$ has $\Delta V = 10/(256-1) \approx 39.2\text{ mV}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

**Step 1:** Quantization noise is modeled as a uniform random signal of amplitude $\pm \Delta V/2$ (the maximum error between actual analog value and the digitized code).

**Step 2:** The RMS (Root-Mean-Square) value of a uniform noise distribution is:

$$
V_{n,rms} = \frac{\Delta V}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 3:** The RMS value of a full-scale sinusoidal input is:

$$
V_{s,rms} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 4:** SNR in linear scale is the power ratio:

$$
SNR_{linear} = \left(\frac{V_{s,rms}}{V_{n,rms}}\right)^2
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the expressions:

$$
SNR_{linear} = \left(\frac{V_{FSR}/(2\sqrt{2})}{\Delta V/(2\sqrt{3})}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{V_{FSR}}{\Delta V}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{3}{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\Delta V = V_{FSR}/(2^n - 1) \approx V_{FSR}/2^n$:

$$
SNR_{linear} \approx 2^{2n} \cdot \frac{3}{2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^n
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to decibels using $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(SNR_{linear})$:

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^n\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 n \log_{10}(4)
$$

**Step 8:** Evaluate numerically: $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10}(4) \approx 0.602$. Hence:

$$
SNR_{dB} \approx 1.76 + 6.02 n
$$

This is the famous **6 dB-per-bit rule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** A 16-bit ADC has theoretical SNR $= 1.76 + 6.02 \times 16 \approx 98.08$ dB. Each extra bit adds **≈ 6 dB** of dynamic range.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC has a unipolar range of $0\text{ V}$ to $10\text{ V}$. Calculate (a) the LSB step size, (b) theoretical SNR, (c) ENOB if measured SNR is 70 dB.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — LSB:**
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10}{4096 - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442 \text{ mV}
$$

**Part (b) — Theoretical SNR:**
$$
SNR = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 12 = 1.76 + 72.24 = 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

**Part (c) — ENOB at 70 dB measured:**
$$
ENOB = \frac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{70 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{68.24}{6.02} \approx 11.33 \text{ bits}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem

**Step 1:** A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ has a maximum frequency component $f_{max}$.

**Step 2:** When sampled at frequency $f_s$, the spectrum of the sampled signal repeats every $f_s$ Hz (spectral replication).

**Step 3:** For the original spectrum to be reconstructed without overlap (aliasing):

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Rule of Thumb (KTU Standard):** Engineers typically choose $f_s \geq 2.5 \cdot f_{max}$ to allow for non-ideal anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

### 3.5 Numerical Sampling Problem

**Problem:** An audio signal has $f_{max} = 20$ kHz. Choose a sampling rate and find the alias frequency if the actual input contains a 30 kHz component.

**Step 1:** Choose $f_s = 44.1$ kHz (CD-quality standard).

**Step 2:** Nyquist limit $= f_s/2 = 22.05$ kHz. Since $30\text{ kHz} > 22.05\text{ kHz}$, aliasing occurs.

**Step 3:** Compute alias frequency:
$$
f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - f_s \vert = \vert 30 - 44.1 \vert = 14.1 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 4:** The 30 kHz tone would appear falsely as a 14.1 kHz tone in the digitized signal — a classic measurement error caused by insufficient anti-aliasing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — PC-Based DAQ Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phys[Physical Quantity] --> tr[Transducer Sensor]
    tr --> sigc[Signal Conditioner Amplify Filter Isolate]
    sigc --> mux[Analog Multiplexer MUX]
    mux --> aa[Anti Aliasing Filter LPF]
    aa --> adc[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    adc --> buf[Buffer Memory FIFO]
    buf --> bus[PC Interface Bus PCI USB Ethernet PXI]
    bus --> drv[Device Driver NI DAQmx VISA]
    drv --> api[API Library]
    api --> lab[LabVIEW Virtual Instrument]
    lab --> disp[Front Panel Display]
    lab --> store[Hard Disk Storage]
    lab --> proc[Analysis and Processing]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Subsystems of a DAQ Card

```mermaid

        A1[CH0] --> A2[CH1]

        SCAN --> MUXIC[MUX IC CD4051]
    end

    subgraph ConditioningBlock[Conditioning Subsystem]

    end

```

```mermaid

    S[Sensor Output mV level] --> AMPL[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100]

```
