
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Data Acquisition. List any four components of a PC-based DAQ system.*

**Model Answer:**
Data Acquisition (DAQ) is the process of sampling real-world physical parameters and converting them into a digital format for PC-based processing, analysis, and storage.

Four components of a PC-based DAQ system are:
1. **Transducer / Sensor** — converts physical quantity into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioner** — amplifies, filters, and isolates the raw signal.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts the conditioned analog signal into a digital code.
4. **PC Interface and Software** — transfers digital data to the computer via PCI/USB/Ethernet and processes it using LabVIEW/MATLAB.

*(Alternative valid components: Multiplexer, Anti-aliasing filter, Sample-and-Hold circuit, Buffer memory, Device driver.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. Why is it important in DAQ?*

**Model Answer:**
**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited analog signal containing frequency components up to a maximum frequency $f_{max}$ must be sampled at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ in order to be reconstructed faithfully from its samples. The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is called the Nyquist rate.

**Importance in DAQ:**
- It sets the **minimum sampling rate** required to avoid **aliasing**.
- Aliasing causes high-frequency signals to appear as false low-frequency signals, leading to incorrect measurements.
- The theorem dictates the design of the **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** placed before the ADC.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the block diagram of a generalized PC-based Data Acquisition system with neat functional blocks. Describe the role of each block in the measurement chain. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 16-bit ADC operates with a bipolar input range of $-10$ V to $+10$ V. Calculate (i) the LSB step size, (ii) theoretical SNR, (iii) the digital output code when the input is $5.32$ V, and (iv) the alias frequency if a $75$ kHz signal is sampled at $50$ kS/s. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation:**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1**. The role of each block:

1. **Transducer:** Converts the physical phenomenon (temperature, pressure, force) into a corresponding electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance).
2. **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies low-level signals, removes noise via filters, isolates the PC from transients, linearizes non-linear sensor outputs, and provides excitation to passive sensors.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX):** Sequentially selects among multiple input channels, allowing a single ADC to serve many sensors — reduces cost.
4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Low-pass filter with cut-off $f_c \leq f_s/2$ prevents high-frequency components from folding back as alias.
5. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Holds the analog voltage constant during ADC conversion, avoiding conversion errors due to input variation.
6. **ADC:** Performs quantization and encoding of the analog sample into an $n$-bit binary code.
7. **Buffer / FIFO Memory:** Temporarily stores digital samples, allowing high-speed burst acquisition independent of PC bus speed.
8. **PC Interface Bus:** Transfers digital data to the computer (PCI, USB, Ethernet, PXI).
9. **Device Driver + API:** Software layer (e.g., NI-DAQmx) that abstracts low-level register access into high-level read/write functions.
10. **Virtual Instrument (LabVIEW VI):** Front panel displays waveforms, performs analysis (FFT, filtering), and stores data.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 16$ bits, bipolar range $= -10$ V to $+10$ V, so $V_{FSR} = 20$ V. Input $= 5.32$ V. $f_{in} = 75$ kHz, $f_s = 50$ kS/s.

**(i) LSB Step Size:**
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{20}{65536 - 1} = \frac{20}{65535} \approx 305.18 \text{ µV}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical SNR:**
$$
SNR = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 16 = 1.76 + 96.32 = 98.08 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iii) Digital Output Code for 5.32 V:**

For a bipolar range, the offset is $-10$ V. The code is:
$$
Code = \frac{V_{in} - V_{min}}{\Delta V} = \frac{5.32 - (-10)}{305.18 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{15.32}{305.18 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 50200
$$

In hexadecimal: $50200 \div 16 = 3137$ remainder $8$. Therefore, the code ≈ **0xC428** (decimal 50200).

**(iv) Alias Frequency:**
Since $f_{in} = 75$ kHz $> f_s/2 = 25$ kHz, aliasing occurs:
$$
f_{alias} = \vert 75 - 50 \vert = 25 \text{ kHz}
$$

The 75 kHz signal will appear falsely as a 25 kHz component in the digitized data.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the operation of an Analog-to-Digital Converter subsystem used in PC-based DAQ. Discuss the difference between **SAR** and **Sigma-Delta** ADC architectures. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Derive the theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) expression for an ideal $n$-bit ADC. A 14-bit DAQ card has a full-scale range of $0$ to $5$ V. Compute (i) the LSB size, (ii) theoretical SNR in dB, and (iii) ENOB if the measured SNR is $80$ dB. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ADC Subsystem Operation:**

Refer to the functional diagram in **Section 4.3**.

The ADC subsystem works as follows:
1. The conditioned analog voltage is fed to a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit, which samples and holds the voltage constant during conversion.
2. The held voltage is fed to the **Quantizer**, which compares it against reference levels spaced $\Delta V$ apart.
3. The **Binary Encoder** converts the quantization level into an $n$-bit parallel digital code.
4. The digital code is sent to the **PC interface** via a buffer and bus.

**SAR vs Sigma-Delta ADC:**

| Parameter | SAR ADC | Sigma-Delta ADC |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 12 to 18 bits | 16 to 24 bits |
| Sampling Rate | Medium (kS/s to few MS/s) | Low (typically ≤ 1 MS/s) |
| Architecture | Binary search using successive approximation | Oversampling + noise shaping + digital decimation |
| Latency | Low (1 conversion cycle) | Higher due to digital filtering |
| Power | Moderate | Low |
| Typical Use | General-purpose DAQ, oscilloscopes | DMMs, audio, high-precision sensors |
| Cost | Medium | Medium-High |

**(b) Derivation and Numerical Solution:**

**Derivation:** Already covered in **Section 3.2**. Final expression:

$$
\boxed{SNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n}
$$

**Numerical:** $n = 14$ bits, $V_{FSR} = 5$ V (unipolar).

**(i) LSB Size:**
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5}{16384 - 1} = \frac{5}{16383} \approx 305.18 \text{ µV}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical SNR:**
$$
SNR = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 14 = 1.76 + 84.28 = 86.04 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iii) ENOB at 80 dB measured:**
$$
ENOB = \frac{80 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{78.24}{6.02} \approx 12.99 \approx 13 \text{ bits}
$$

The card has lost about 1 effective bit due to real-world noise and distortion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Losing Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Bits and Levels:** Writing $2^{n-1}$ instead of $2^n$ for number of levels — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $-1$ in LSB formula:** Using $2^n$ instead of $2^n-1$ in the denominator is acceptable for large $n$, but examiners expect you to know the precise form.
> 3. **Units in SNR:** Always quote SNR in **dB**, not as a linear ratio.
> 4. **Aliasing Logic:** Many students write $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ but forget to identify **when aliasing actually occurs** ($f_{in} > f_s/2$).
> 5. **Block Diagram Missing Labels:** Drawing the DAQ block diagram without labeling each block in the answer sheet costs easy 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Missing Signal Conditioning:** Examiners specifically look for *amplification, filtering, isolation, excitation, and linearization* as separate sub-tasks.
> 7. **No Anti-Aliasing Filter Mentioned:** A common omission — always include it in the diagram between MUX and ADC.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** DAQ = Physical → Electrical → Digital → PC for VI processing.
- **5-Stage Chain:** Transducer → Signal Conditioner → MUX → ADC → PC Interface & Software.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — fundamental to avoid aliasing.
- **LSB Formula:** $\Delta V = V_{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$ — use $2^n - 1$ for exam precision.
- **SNR Rule:** $SNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n$ — **6 dB per bit** is the golden rule.
- **ENOB Formula:** $ENOB = (SNR - 1.76)/6.02$ — measures real-world ADC quality.
- **ADC Architectures:** SAR (general purpose), Flash (very fast), Sigma-Delta (high resolution).
- **Signal Conditioning Functions:** Amplification, Filtering, Isolation, Linearization, Excitation, CJC.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Always a low-pass filter placed **before** the ADC with $f_c \leq f_s/2$.
- **Common Sensors:** Thermocouple (mV), RTD (Ω), Strain Gauge (mV/V), LVDT (V), 4–20 mA current loop.
- **Buses:** PCI/PCIe (internal high speed), USB (external convenient), PXI/PXIe (modular industrial), Ethernet/LXI (distributed).
- **Software Stack:** Hardware → Driver (NI-DAQmx) → API → LabVIEW VI → User Interface.
- **Throughput:** Multi-channel systems sacrifice per-channel rate: $f_{channel} = f_{ADC}/N$.
- **Virtual Instrumentation:** Replaces dedicated instruments using PC + DAQ + software (LabVIEW).
- **Common Pitfall:** Aliasing appears as a low-frequency ghost — always band-limit the input first.
- **Standard Sampling Rates:** Audio 44.1 kHz, Vibration 10–50 kHz, Engine analysis 100 kHz–1 MHz.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of a Data Acquisition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET833 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components of a Data Acquisition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Data Acquisition System (DAQ / DAS)** is a precisely engineered, integrated collection of hardware and software elements that converts real-world **physical phenomena** (temperature, pressure, strain, flow, light intensity, vibration) into **digitized electrical signals** which can be measured, analyzed, stored, displayed, and processed by a digital computer or embedded controller.

In the context of **Virtual Instrumentation (VI)**, a DAQ system acts as the *physical bridge* between the analog real world and the digital processing domain of software (like LabVIEW, MATLAB, or Python). The system must perform three cardinal functions:

1. **Sensing** – converting a physical parameter into an electrical signal.
2. **Conditioning & Conversion** – amplifying, filtering, isolating, and converting the signal from analog to digital form.
3. **Processing & Presentation** – transferring the digital data to a PC/Controller for visualization and analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A DAQ system is a functional arrangement of *transducers, signal conditioning circuits, multiplexers, sample-and-hold amplifiers, analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), and a host computer* working synchronously to measure, digitize, and interpret real-world physical quantities with high fidelity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **human body** as a natural Data Acquisition System:

- Your **eyes, ears, skin, and nose** act as the **Transducers/Sensors** (they convert light, sound, pressure, and chemicals into nerve impulses).
- Your **spinal cord and thalamus** act as the **Signal Conditioning + Multiplexing + Sample-and-Hold** unit (they filter irrelevant noise, prioritize urgent signals, and hold the information briefly).
- The **analog nerve impulse** is converted to a recognizable perception at the brain's **cortex** — analogous to the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.
- Finally, your **conscious brain (the cortex)** is the **Host Computer**, where the digital data is interpreted, stored in memory, and acted upon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** *Sensor = Sense organ → Signal Conditioner = Spinal cord → ADC = Brain's perception layer → PC = Conscious mind.*

### 1.3 The Standard Performance Metrics (Recall in **BOLD**)

- **Resolution (n):** The number of discrete digital bits the ADC uses to represent the analog signal. Typical DAQ cards: **8-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit**.
- **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The maximum peak-to-peak input voltage span the ADC can digitize, typically **±5 V, ±10 V, or 0–10 V**.
- **Sampling Rate ($f_s$):** The number of samples digitized per second, expressed in **Samples per Second (S/s)** or **kS/s** or **MS/s**.
- **Throughput:** The aggregate rate at which a complete multi-channel system digitizes data.
- **Accuracy & Precision:** Closeness to true value vs. repeatability of measurement.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Expressed in **decibels (dB)**.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, the words **transducer, signal conditioner, multiplexer, S/H, ADC, and host computer** must appear verbatim in any 14-mark "explain the DAQ block diagram" question to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a DAQ System

A modern multi-channel DAQ system follows this canonical signal chain:

**Physical Variable → Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer (MUX) → Sample-and-Hold (S/H) → Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) → Host Computer (CPU/Software) → Display / Storage / Control**

Let us dissect every stage rigorously.

---

#### Stage 1: Transducers and Sensors
A **transducer** is a device that converts one form of energy into another. In a DAQ, we specifically use sensors that convert physical energy into **electrical energy (voltage or current)**.

- **Active transducers** generate their own voltage/current (e.g., *thermocouple, piezoelectric crystal, photovoltaic cell*).
- **Passive transducers** require external excitation (e.g., *RTD, strain gauge, LVDT, thermistor*).

**Why needed:** Computers cannot directly understand pressure or temperature. Transducers translate these phenomena into measurable electrical signals (mV, mA, Ω, pF).

---

#### Stage 2: Signal Conditioning Circuit
Raw sensor outputs are typically:
- Too **small** (e.g., a thermocouple outputs ~**40 μV/°C**)
- Contaminated with **noise** (50 Hz mains hum, EMI)
- Prone to **ground loops** and **loading errors**
- **Non-linear** in nature

Signal conditioning sub-blocks include:
1. **Amplification** – Boosts low-level signals (e.g., using an **Instrumentation Amplifier (IN-Amp)**) to match the ADC's FSR. Typical gains: **×1, ×10, ×100, ×1000**.
2. **Filtering** – Removes unwanted frequency content. Common filters: **Low-Pass (Anti-aliasing)**, High-Pass, Band-Pass, and **Notch (50/60 Hz)**.
3. **Isolation** – Uses optical or transformer isolation to break ground loops and protect the PC.
4. **Linearization** – Corrects non-linear sensor curves (e.g., thermocouple polynomial compensation).
5. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** – Specific to thermocouples.
6. **Excitation** – Provides stable current/voltage to passive sensors (e.g., **4–20 mA current loop**, **10 V bridge excitation**).

**Why needed:** Maximizes the ADC's effective resolution by stretching the sensor's tiny output to fill the full input range (FSR), and prevents aliasing by band-limiting the signal.

---

#### Stage 3: Analog Multiplexer (MUX)
A **multiplexer** is an electronically controlled multi-position switch (typically a CMOS analog switch array) that sequentially routes one of many input channels to a single output line.

- A **16:1 MUX** allows 16 input channels to share a single ADC.
- A **dual 8:1 MUX** is used for differential inputs.

**Why needed:** ADCs are expensive. Multiplexing allows a *single high-quality ADC* to serve *many low-cost input channels* sequentially, drastically reducing the system cost and complexity.

---

#### Stage 4: Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit
The S/H circuit uses a **fast MOSFET switch** and a high-quality **hold capacitor** ($C_H$) to capture (sample) the instantaneous analog voltage and freeze it stable during the ADC's conversion time.

- **Track mode:** Switch closed, $C_H$ charges to track input.
- **Hold mode:** Switch open, $C_H$ retains voltage, ADC performs conversion.

**Why needed:** ADC conversion takes finite time (**aperture time**). Without S/H, the input could change by more than **1 LSB** during conversion, producing digital errors called **aperture uncertainty errors**.

---

#### Stage 5: Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
The ADC is the **digital heart** of the DAQ. Common types:
- **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** – Most common in DAQ cards, 12–18 bit, moderate speed.
- **Flash ADC** – Ultra-fast, used in digital oscilloscopes, 6–8 bit.
- **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ)** – Very high resolution (16–24 bit), used in DMMs and audio.
- **Integrating (Dual-Slope)** – Excellent noise rejection, used in digital multimeters.

**Why needed:** Computers operate on **discrete digital codes (binary integers)**, not continuous voltages. The ADC is the translator.

---

#### Stage 6: Host Computer and Software
The digital data is transferred via bus standards (**PCI, PCIe, USB, PXI, Ethernet, RS-485, GPIB**) to the host computer. Software environments include **LabVIEW, MATLAB, Python (PyDAQmx), C/C++, DASYLab**.

The software performs:
- **Data Logging** and **Time-Stamping**
- **Real-time Visualization** (waveform charts, strip charts)
- **Post-processing** (FFT, filtering, statistical analysis)
- **Decision making** and **PID Control** output

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **ADC Resolution (LSB weight)** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$ (ideal) or $\dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$ (practical) | Smallest detectable voltage step (Volts) |
| 2 | **Quantization Error** | $E_q = \pm \dfrac{1}{2}\,LSB = \pm \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Maximum inherent conversion error (Volts) |
| 3 | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling frequency (Hz) |
| 4 | **Nyquist Frequency** | $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Highest representable input frequency (Hz) |
| 5 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Ideal N-bit)** | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB | Theoretical SNR for a perfect N-bit ADC |
| 6 | **Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)** | $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Real-world effective resolution (bits) |
| 7 | **Aperture Uncertainty Error** | $\Delta V_{aperture} = \dfrac{dV}{dt} \cdot t_{ap}$ | Voltage change during aperture time (Volts) |
| 8 | **Amplifier Gain** | $G = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Dimensionless (or dB: $20 \log_{10}G$) |
| 9 | **Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff** | $f_c \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | To satisfy Nyquist, real-world uses $f_c \approx 0.4\,f_s$ |
| 10 | **Throughput (Scanning DAQ)** | $T = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot t_{conv}}$ | Aggregate channels-per-second (S/s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests the formula $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$ for an 8-bit ADC with FSR = 10 V. Memorize $2^8 = 256$, so $\Delta V = 10/256 \approx 39.06$ mV.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

DAQ systems are the foundational backbone of:
- **Industrial Process Control** (refineries, power plants, pharmaceutical manufacturing)
- **Structural Health Monitoring** (bridges, dams, aircraft wings using strain gauges)
- **Medical Diagnostics** (ECG, EEG, patient monitors)
- **Automotive Testing** (crash tests, engine dyno, ECU calibration)
- **Scientific Research** (particle physics at CERN, telescope arrays, climate monitoring)
- **IoT and Edge Computing** (smart sensors feeding cloud dashboards)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: ADC Resolution and Quantization Step Size

**Problem Statement:** A 12-bit ADC is used in a DAQ card. The Full-Scale Range (FSR) is configured as **0 V to +10 V** (unipolar). Calculate:
(a) The voltage resolution (1 LSB).
(b) The maximum theoretical quantization error.
(c) The digital output code for an analog input of **6.437 V**.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

- $n = 12$ bits
- $V_{FSR} = 10\,\text{V} - 0\,\text{V} = 10\,\text{V}$

---

**Step 2 — Apply the resolution formula.**

Using the ideal formula for voltage per LSB:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}
$$

$$
\Delta V = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{4096}
$$

$$
\Delta V \approx 2.4414\,\text{mV per LSB}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the smallest analog voltage change that the ADC can distinguish. Any input variation below 2.44 mV is "invisible" to the system.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum quantization error.**

Theoretical quantization error is bounded by half of one LSB:

$$
E_q = \pm \frac{1}{2}\,LSB = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2}
$$

$$
E_q = \pm \frac{2.4414\,\text{mV}}{2} = \pm 1.2207\,\text{mV}
$$

**Explanation:** No matter how perfect the ADC is, the inherent staircase nature of digital encoding produces an unavoidable error of up to half a step.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the digital output code for 6.437 V.**

The digital code $D$ is given by:

$$
D = \text{ROUND}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right)
$$

$$
D = \text{ROUND}\left(\frac{6.437\,\text{V}}{2.4414\,\text{mV}}\right) = \text{ROUND}(2636.84)
$$

$$
D = 2637
$$

Converting to 12-bit binary representation:

$$
2637_{10} = 101001001101_2
$$

**Verification:** $2637 \times 2.4414\,\text{mV} = 6.43798\,\text{V}$ (matches the input within 1 LSB tolerance).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Determining Minimum Sampling Rate to Avoid Aliasing

**Problem Statement:** A vibration sensor in a turbo-generator outputs a maximum frequency of $f_{max} = 4.5\,\text{kHz}$. The DAQ system must capture this without aliasing. Determine:
(a) The minimum sampling frequency.
(b) The Nyquist frequency.
(c) If a 10 kS/s card is used, what is the consequence?

---

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem.**

To perfectly reconstruct a band-limited signal, the sampling rate must be at least twice the highest frequency component:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 4500\,\text{Hz} = 9000\,\text{Hz}
$$

Therefore the **minimum sampling rate** is $f_{s,min} = 9\,\text{kS/s}$.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Nyquist frequency.**

$$
f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{9000}{2} = 4500\,\text{Hz}
$$

This confirms $f_N = f_{max}$, the theoretical boundary.

---

**Step 3 — Consequences of using a 10 kS/s card.**

At $f_s = 10\,\text{kS/s}$:
- $f_N = 5\,\text{kHz}$
- Since $f_{max} = 4.5\,\text{kHz} < 5\,\text{kHz}$, the system satisfies Nyquist ✓
- The signal is safely captured.

**However**, if a 8 kS/s card is used:
- $f_N = 4\,\text{kHz} < 4.5\,\text{kHz}$
- **Aliasing occurs.** Frequencies above 4 kHz "fold back" into the 0–4 kHz band, producing false low-frequency readings. This is a catastrophic error in vibration analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Engineering Rule:** Always oversample by a factor of **2.5 to 5×** beyond Nyquist to account for real-world anti-aliasing filter roll-off, i.e., use $f_s = 10\times f_{max}$ to $20\times f_{max}$ for safety.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ADC Architectures for DAQ

| Parameter | **SAR ADC** | **Flash ADC** | **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ)** | **Integrating** |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Resolution | 12–18 bit | 6–8 bit | 16–24 bit | 16–22 bit |
| Sampling Speed | Medium (kS/s–MS/s) | Very High (GS/s) | Low–Medium | Very Low |
| Cost | Moderate | High (2ⁿ comparators) | Low–Moderate | Low |
| Noise Rejection | Moderate | Poor | Excellent | Excellent (50/60 Hz) |
| Typical DAQ Use | **General purpose DAQ** (NI 6008/6211) | Digital oscilloscopes | DMMs, audio, load cells | Bench DMMs |
| Anti-aliasing req. | Explicit LPF needed | Explicit LPF needed | Built-in digital filter | Built-in |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Multi-Channel DAQ System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Physical World
    PV[Physical Variable\nTemperature Pressure Strain]:::phys

    %% Sensors
    S1[Sensor 1\nThermocouple]:::sens
    S2[Sensor 2\nStrain Gauge]:::sens
    S3[Sensor 3\nLVDT]:::sens
    S4[Sensor N\nGeneric Transducer]:::sens

    %% Signal Conditioning
    SC1[Amplifier\n+ IN-Amp]:::sc
    SC2[Filter\nAnti-Alias LPF]:::sc
    SC3[Isolation\nOptocoupler]:::sc
    SC4[Linearizer\nCJC and Calib]:::sc

    %% Multiplexer
    MUX[Analog Multiplexer\n16 to 1]:::mux

    %% Sample and Hold
    SH[Sample and Hold\nS and H]:::sh

    %% ADC
    ADC[Analog to Digital\nConverter ADC]:::adc

    %% Host Computer
    CPU[Host Computer\nCPU and RAM]:::cpu
    SW[VI Software\nLabVIEW or MATLAB]:::sw
    DISP[Display and Storage\nHDD or Cloud]:::disp
    ACT[Actuator Output\nDAC and Control]:::act

    %% Connections
    PV --> S1
    PV --> S2
    PV --> S3
    PV --> S4
    S1 --> SC1
    S2 --> SC2
    S3 --> SC3
    S4 --> SC4

    SC3 --> MUX
    SC4 --> MUX

    SH --> ADC

    CPU --> SW
    SW --> DISP
    SW --> ACT

    %% Styling
    classDef phys fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT SIGNAL CHAIN]
        A1[Transducer] --> A2[Signal Conditioner]
    end
    subgraph DIGITIZE[DIGITIZATION STAGE]
        A2 --> A3[Multiplexer MUX]
        A3 --> A4[Sample and Hold S and H]
        A4 --> A5[Analog to Digital ADC]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION STAGE]
        A5 --> A6[Microprocessor or PC Bus]
        A6 --> A7[Virtual Instrument Software]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
        A7 --> A8[Visualization and Storage]
        A7 --> A9[Digital to Analog DAC for Control]
    end
```

### 4.3 Timing Diagram — Multiplexed Channel Sequencing

```mermaid
gantt
    title DAQ Multiplexed Channel Scanning Timeline
    dateFormat ss
    axisFormat %S
    section ADC Activity
    Channel 1 conversion :a1, 00, 1s
    Channel 2 conversion :a2, 01, 1s
    Channel 3 conversion :a3, 02, 1s
    Channel 4 conversion :a4, 03, 1s
    Channel 5 conversion :a5, 04, 1s
    Channel 6 conversion :a6, 05, 1s
    Channel 7 conversion :a7, 06, 1s
    Channel 8 conversion :a8, 07, 1s
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Gantt diagram above illustrates how a single ADC sequentially digitizes 8 channels. Each conversion occupies 1 second of the ADC's aperture time. The multiplexer switches between channels during the *hold* phase to allow the S/H to acquire the next channel's voltage.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any three functional blocks of a Data Acquisition System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Data Acquisition System comprises the following key functional blocks:

1. **Transducer/Sensor (1 mark):** Converts the physical quantity (temperature, pressure, etc.) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance). Example: *Thermocouple* converts temperature to mV.
2. **Signal Conditioner (1 mark):** Performs amplification, filtering, isolation, and linearization on the raw sensor output to make it suitable for the ADC. Example: An *Instrumentation Amplifier* amplifies a 10 mV thermocouple output to 5 V.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) (1 mark):** Converts the conditioned analog voltage into a binary digital code (e.g., 12-bit code) which can be read and processed by a digital computer.

*(Alternative valid blocks: Multiplexer, Sample-and-Hold, Host Computer with VI software.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define "quantization error" of an ADC. What is its theoretical maximum value for an N-bit converter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Quantization error is the inherent, unavoidable error introduced during the analog-to-digital conversion process due to the finite number of discrete digital levels the ADC can produce. It arises because the continuous analog signal must be rounded to the nearest available digital step (LSB).
- **Maximum value (1.5 marks):** For an ideal N-bit ADC, the maximum quantization error is mathematically bounded by half of one Least Significant Bit:

$$
E_{q,max} = \pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB} = \pm \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^{N+1}}
$$

---

### PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)
*(Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE of A or B)*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a generalized Data Acquisition System. Discuss the role of the multiplexer and sample-and-hold circuit in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Data Acquisition System is an integrated assembly of hardware and software used to measure, convert, and analyze real-world physical parameters in digital form.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn):**
*Physical Variable → Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → Sample-and-Hold → ADC → Host Computer → Display/Storage*

**Explanation of each block (4 marks):**

1. **Transducer:** Converts the physical phenomenon (heat, force, light) into an electrical quantity. E.g., RTD converts temperature to resistance.
2. **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies weak signals (e.g., via IN-Amp), filters noise (LPF), and isolates ground loops. E.g., amplifies 30 mV thermocouple output to 5 V.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX):** A multi-channel electronic switch that sequentially connects one of N input channels to a single shared ADC. A 16:1 MUX allows 16 sensors to share one ADC, drastically reducing cost and complexity. Controlled by digital address lines from the host.
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Captures the rapidly varying analog voltage on a hold capacitor via a fast analog switch. During the "track" phase, it follows the input; during the "hold" phase, it freezes the voltage stable for the ADC's conversion aperture time.
5. **ADC:** Performs the core conversion from analog voltage to digital binary code.
6. **Host Computer:** Receives digital data via bus (USB/PCI), runs VI software for real-time display, storage, FFT analysis, and PID control.

**Role of Multiplexer (1.5 marks):**
The multiplexer acts as a *time-sharing switch*. It sequentially scans each input channel, routing the conditioned signal to the S/H and ADC. By enabling one ADC to serve many channels, it optimizes hardware cost. The switching is controlled by a digital address counter synchronized to the conversion cycle.

**Role of Sample-and-Hold (1.5 marks):**
The S/H circuit holds the analog input voltage *constant* during the ADC's conversion time (which can range from microseconds to milliseconds). This prevents the *aperture uncertainty error*, which would otherwise occur if the signal changed by more than 1 LSB during conversion. Without the S/H, fast-changing signals would be digitized inaccurately.

---

**(b) A 12-bit ADC with a full-scale range of ±5 V is used in a DAQ system. Calculate: (i) the resolution in mV/LSB, (ii) the maximum quantization error, and (iii) the digital code (in decimal) for an input voltage of +2.7 V. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- FSR $= +5\,\text{V} - (-5\,\text{V}) = 10\,\text{V}$ (peak-to-peak)
- $V_{in} = +2.7\,\text{V}$

---

**(i) Resolution (1 LSB step size) (2.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{4096} = 2.4414\,\text{mV/LSB}
$$

---

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error (2 marks):**

$$
E_{q,max} = \pm \frac{1}{2}\,LSB = \pm \frac{2.4414}{2} = \pm 1.2207\,\text{mV}
$$

---

**(iii) Digital Code for +2.7 V (2.5 marks):**

For a bipolar ±5 V range, the digital code $D$ is:

$$
D = \text{ROUND}\left(\frac{V_{in} + 5}{\Delta V}\right)
$$

$$
D = \text{ROUND}\left(\frac{2.7 + 5}{2.4414 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = \text{ROUND}\left(\frac{7.7}{0.0024414}\right)
$$

$$
D = \text{ROUND}(3153.86) = 3154
$$

In 12-bit binary (two's complement, since input is positive, MSB = 0):
$D = 110001010010_2$

**Verification:** $3154 \times 2.4414\,\text{mV} - 5\,\text{V} = 7.7\,\text{V} - 5\,\text{V} = +2.700\,\text{V}$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the different types of ADCs commonly used in DAQ systems. Compare SAR, Flash, and Sigma-Delta architectures in terms of resolution, speed, and application. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three dominant ADC architectures used in modern DAQ systems are:

**1. Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC (2 marks):**
- **Working principle:** Uses a binary search algorithm. The internal DAC generates a trial voltage, which is compared with the input via a comparator. The SAR register narrows down the correct code bit-by-bit over N clock cycles.
- **Resolution:** 12 to 18 bits.
- **Speed:** Moderate (10 kS/s to 10 MS/s).
- **Application:** General-purpose DAQ cards (e.g., NI USB-6008, NI PCIe-6321) and microcontrollers.
- **Pros:** Excellent balance of speed, resolution, power, and cost.

**2. Flash ADC (1.5 marks):**
- **Working principle:** Uses $2^N - 1$ parallel comparators referencing a resistor ladder, encoding the result via a priority encoder. Conversion happens in a single clock cycle.
- **Resolution:** 6 to 8 bits.
- **Speed:** Extremely fast (100 MS/s to 10 GS/s).
- **Application:** Digital storage oscilloscopes (DSOs), radar, high-speed data capture.
- **Pros:** Ultra-fast. **Cons:** High cost, high power, low resolution.

**3. Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) ADC (2 marks):**
- **Working principle:** Oversamples the input at very high rate, uses a 1-bit DAC in a feedback loop, and noise-shapes the quantization noise out of the signal band. A digital decimation filter then downsamples to produce high-resolution output.
- **Resolution:** 16 to 24 bits (very high).
- **Speed:** Low to moderate (S/s to kS/s).
- **Application:** Digital multimeters, audio systems, weigh scales, precision temperature/load measurement.
- **Pros:** Excellent noise rejection, built-in digital filtering. **Cons:** Slower conversion rate.

**Comparison Summary (1.5 marks):**

| Architecture | Resolution | Speed | Best Use |
|--------------|------------|-------|----------|
| SAR | 12–18 bit | Moderate | General DAQ |
| Flash | 6–8 bit | Ultra-fast | Oscilloscopes |
| Sigma-Delta | 16–24 bit | Slow | Precision DMMs |

---

**(b) A vibration sensor produces a maximum frequency component of 3.2 kHz. The DAQ card's anti-aliasing filter has a cutoff of 4 kHz. (i) Calculate the minimum sampling rate required by the Nyquist criterion. (ii) Determine the Nyquist frequency. (iii) Will aliasing occur with this filter-sampling combination? Justify. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{max} = 3.2\,\text{kHz}$, $f_c = 4\,\text{kHz}$

---

**(i) Minimum Sampling Rate (2.5 marks):**

Applying the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 3.2\,\text{kHz} = 6.4\,\text{kS/s}
$$

Therefore $f_{s,min} = 6.4\,\text{kS/s}$.

---

**(ii) Nyquist Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{6.4\,\text{kHz}}{2} = 3.2\,\text{kHz}
$$

This matches $f_{max}$ exactly at the Nyquist boundary.

---

**(iii) Will Aliasing Occur? (3 marks):**

**Yes, aliasing will likely occur in a real system.** Reasons:

1. The Nyquist theorem requires the signal to be *strictly band-limited* below $f_N$. The anti-aliasing filter cutoff is 4 kHz, which is **above** 3.2 kHz — meaning significant signal energy *passes* through the filter, contaminating the band right at the Nyquist limit.
2. Real-world filters have a gradual roll-off (typically Butterworth or Bessel), not an ideal brick-wall response. Frequencies between 3.2 kHz and 4 kHz will pass with some attenuation but still produce aliasing artifacts.
3. The minimum sampling rate of 6.4 kS/s is at the *bare minimum* — engineering practice mandates **oversampling** by 2.5× to 5×, i.e., $f_s$ between **8 kS/s and 16 kS/s**.

**Recommendation:** Increase $f_s$ to at least 16 kS/s and/or use a sharper anti-aliasing filter with a cutoff of 3.2 kHz to fully eliminate aliasing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing FSR and FSR/2:** For a bipolar ±5 V ADC, the *peak-to-peak* FSR is **10 V**, not 5 V. Many students incorrectly use 5 V in the resolution formula, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Sample-and-Hold block:** A "DAQ block diagram" answer that omits the S/H circuit will be docked 2 marks, as it is a mandatory KTU 2024 syllabus element.
> 3. **Mixing up Nyquist rate and Nyquist frequency:** $f_s$ (sampling rate) ≠ $f_N$ (Nyquist frequency). KTU paper setters specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram:** In any 7-mark question about DAQ, the *block diagram is compulsory*. A textual answer without a labeled diagram loses a minimum of 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the multiplexer in a multi-channel question:** If the question implies >1 input channel, the multiplexer MUST appear in the diagram.
> 6. **Quoting the resolution formula incorrectly:** Use $V_{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$ (practical) or $V_{FSR}/2^n$ (ideal). Stating $2^{n-1}$ or $2^n - 2$ is a direct mark loss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Anchor:** A DAQ system converts **physical signals → electrical signals → digital data** for computer-based analysis.
- **Mandatory Block Diagram Order:** Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → Sample-and-Hold → ADC → Host Computer → Output (Display/Storage/Control).
- **Signal Conditioner sub-functions:** Amplification, Filtering, Isolation, Linearization, Excitation, CJC.
- **Multiplexer (MUX):** Time-shares one ADC across N channels (e.g., 16:1).
- **Sample-and-Hold:** Freezes analog voltage during ADC conversion to avoid **aperture error**.
- **Key Resolution Formula (High Frequency in KTU):** $\Delta V = V_{FSR}/2^n$.
- **Quantization Error:** Always $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB maximum.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$; $f_N = f_s/2$.
- **Anti-aliasing Rule:** Cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s/2$; oversample by **2.5×–5×** in practice.
- **SAR ADC** = General DAQ; **Flash ADC** = Oscilloscopes; **Σ-Δ ADC** = High-precision DMMs.
- **SNR for ideal N-bit ADC:** $6.02N + 1.76$ dB.
- **Common DAQ Buses:** USB, PCI/PCIe, PXI, Ethernet, GPIB, RS-485.
- **Software Stack:** LabVIEW (gold standard for VI), MATLAB, Python (PyDAQmx), DASYLab.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Formulae to Memorize:**
> 1. $\text{Resolution} = V_{FSR} / 2^n$
> 2. $E_{q,max} = \pm V_{FSR} / 2^{n+1}$
> 3. $f_{s,min} = 2 f_{max}$
> 4. $SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$
> 5. $ENOB = (SNR_{measured} - 1.76) / 6.02$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog vs. Digital Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET833 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Analog vs. Digital Signals ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Analog Signal An **analog signal** is a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal whose instantaneous value varies smoothly with time and can assume any magnitude within a defined range. It is the direct electrical replica of a physical phenomenon (temperature, pressure, velocity, light intensity, etc.), where the signal amplitude is *analogous* to the original measurand. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In Virtual Instrumentation, the vast majority of front-end transducers (RTDs, thermocouples, strain gauges, LVDTs, microphones) output **analog signals**. These MUST be conditioned and converted before being processed by software like LabVIEW. Mathematically, an analog signal is described as: $$x(t) \in \mathbb{R}, \quad \forall t \in \mathbb{R}$$ ### 1.2 Formal Definition — Digital Signal A **digital signal** is a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude signal represented as a sequence of numbers (typically binary words of `n` bits). It is produced by *sampling* an analog signal in time and *quantizing* it in amplitude, so that the signal can be stored, processed, and transmitted by computers and microprocessors. $$x[n] \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^{n}-1\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 — The Water Tap:** Open a tap *slowly*. The water flow can take *any value* between 0 L/s and full blast. This is an **analog** signal — infinitely many levels. Now replace the tap with a tap that has only two positions — fully OFF or fully ON. This is a **digital** signal — only two levels (0 or 1). > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 — The Classroom Attendance:** Imagine a teacher recording the room temperature every 5 minutes. The actual temperature changes continuously (analog). The teacher's notebook, however, only stores discrete readings like `28.4 °C`, `28.6 °C`, etc. — this discretized, finite-precision record is a **digital** signal. ### 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters in Virtual Instrumentation Virtual Instruments (VIs) — built on platforms such as **LabVIEW** — execute code on digital computers. Therefore, every natural analog quantity from the physical world must be converted into a digital equivalent using a Data Acquisition (DAQ) system. Understanding the difference between these two domains is the **foundational prerequisite** for studying ADC/DAC, signal conditioning, and PC-based instrumentation. ### 1.5 Standard Physical Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Standard logic high level (TTL) | $V_{IH}$ | **2.0 V – 5.0 V** | | Standard logic low level (TTL) | $V_{IL}$ | **0.0 V – 0.8 V** | | Standard analog input range | $\pm V_{FS}$ | typically **$\pm 10$ V** or **0–10 V** | | Common ADC resolution | $n$ | **8, 12, 16, 24 bits** | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Characteristics of Analog Signals - **Continuity in time** — defined for *every* instant $t \in \mathbb{R}$. - **Continuity in amplitude** — can take *infinitely many* values within a range. - **Susceptibility to noise** — any additive noise directly corrupts amplitude, because there is no way to "clean" an infinite-resolution value. - **Resolution is theoretically infinite** — limited only by thermal noise and sensor physics. - **Processing requires analog circuits** — op-amps, filters, multipliers. ### 2.2 Characteristics of Digital Signals - **Discrete in time** — exists only at sampling instants $t = nT_s$. - **Discrete in amplitude** — confined to $2^n$ quantization levels. - **Noise immunity** — small perturbations within a quantization band do not change the binary code (the *noise margin*). - **Resolution is finite and deterministic** — set by the bit-width $n$ of the ADC. - **Processing is algorithmic** — done in software/firmware (LabVIEW VIs, DSPs, FPGAs). ### 2.3 The Three-Step Conversion: Analog → Digital > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Pipeline:** **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**. Master this pipeline; every KTU question on data acquisition traces back to these three operations. 1. **Sampling** — Capture instantaneous amplitudes at regular time intervals $T_s$. Governed by the **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)**. 2. **Quantization** — Round each captured sample to the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels defined by the ADC's voltage range. 3. **Encoding** — Represent each quantized level as a unique $n$-bit binary word (e.g., natural binary, two's complement, Gray code). ### 2.4 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon) A band-limited signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples *if and only if* the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ The minimum allowable sampling rate $f_s = 2f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. Sampling below this rate produces **aliasing** — a destructive, irreversible distortion. ### 2.5 Quantization Step (LSB) Size For an ADC with range $[V_{min}, V_{max}]$ and resolution of $n$ bits: $$\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^{n}} \quad \text{(Volts per LSB)}$$ ### 2.6 Quantization Error (Noise) The maximum quantization error of an ideal mid-tread ADC is bounded by half of one LSB: $$e_{q} \in \left[ -\frac{\Delta}{2}, \; +\frac{\Delta}{2} \right]$$ ### 2.7 Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) For a full-scale sinusoidal input, the theoretical SQNR in decibels is: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$$ This famous relation shows that **every additional bit of resolution improves the SQNR by approximately 6 dB** (i.e., doubles the number of distinguishable amplitude levels). ### 2.8 Bit Rate of the Digital Stream For a sampled and quantized digital stream: $$R_{b} = f_s \times n \quad \text{(bits per second)}$$ ### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Nyquist Rate | $f_{s,\min} = 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum to avoid aliasing | | 2 | Quantization step | $\Delta = (V_{max}-V_{min}) / 2^{n}$ | V/LSB | One LSB width | | 3 | Number of levels | $L = 2^{n}$ | dimensionless | Total distinct codes | | 4 | Quantization error | $\vert e_{q} \vert \leq \Delta/2$ | V | Half-LSB bound | | 5 | SQNR (sinusoidal FS) | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB | Universal ADC metric | | 6 | Bit rate | $R_{b} = f_{s} \cdot n$ | bps | Storage/transmission | | 7 | Reconstruction filter cutoff | $f_{c} = f_{s}/2$ | Hz | Ideal low-pass at Nyquist | | 8 | Total storage per sample | $\log_{2}(L) = n$ | bits | Equals ADC resolution | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the design foundation of every commercial DAQ card (NI USB-6363, NI PXIe-5105, etc.). When a student specifies a sensor, the next three design choices are *always* — choose $f_s$, choose $n$, compute required $R_b$ and SQNR. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Analog vs. Digital Side-by-Side | Parameter | Analog Signal | Digital Signal | |---|---|---| | Time nature | Continuous | Discrete (sampled) | | Amplitude nature | Continuous | Discrete (quantized) | | Independent variable | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (sample index) | | Representation | $x(t)$ waveform | Binary word sequence $x[n]$ | | Resolution | Theoretically infinite | Finite: $2^{n}$ levels | | Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent (noise margin) | | Storage | Difficult (tape/film) | Easy (files, memory) | | Processing hardware | Op-amps, analog ICs | CPUs, DSPs, FPGAs | | Bandwidth requirement | Lower | Higher (data rate $f_s \cdot n$) | | Typical VI application | Transducer output | Inside DAQ / LabVIEW block | | Reversibility of noise | Impossible | Possible (re-quantize cleanly) | ### 3.2 Derivation — Number of Quantization Levels from Bit-Width An $n$-bit binary word has $2^n$ unique combinations. Each combination is mapped to one amplitude level. Therefore: $$L = 2^{n}$$ > **Textual logic:** Start with 1 bit → 2 codes (0,1). Add 1 bit → codes double to 4. By mathematical induction, $n$ bits yield $2^n$ codes. ### 3.3 Derivation — Quantization Step Size $\Delta$ The total analog input range is $V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min}$. We must partition this range into $2^n$ equally-spaced bins. Each bin has width: $$\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^{n}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$$ > **Textual logic:** Divide the available "voltage real estate" equally among the available codes. This gives the LSB voltage. ### 3.4 Derivation — Maximum Quantization Error The true analog value can lie anywhere within one LSB-wide bin. The worst-case distance from the bin center to either edge is exactly half a bin width: $$e_{q,\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2}$$ > **Textual logic:** A quantizer rounds to the *nearest* level. The largest possible rounding mistake is half the step size. ### 3.5 Derivation — SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoid Consider $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$. The signal power is: $$P_{sig} = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$ Assuming quantization noise is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, the noise power is: $$P_{noise} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2}\, de = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$ Substituting $\Delta = 2A/2^{n}$ (full-scale range $= 2A$): $$P_{noise} = \frac{(2A/2^{n})^{2}}{12} = \frac{A^{2}}{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}$$ The SQNR ratio becomes: $$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{sig}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{A^{2}/2}{A^{2}/(3 \cdot 2^{2n})} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}$$ Converting to decibels: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}\right) = 20n\log_{10}(2) + 10\log_{10}(1.5)$$ Since $20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$ and $10\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$: $$\boxed{\;\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76\;}$$ ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A 12-bit ADC has an input range of $[0, 10]$ V. The input signal has maximum frequency $f_{max} = 4$ kHz. Compute: (i) quantization step, (ii) maximum quantization error, (iii) minimum sampling rate, and (iv) theoretical SQNR. **Given:** $n = 12$, $V_{min}=0$ V, $V_{max}=10$ V, $f_{max}=4$ kHz. **Step (i) — Quantization step:** $$\Delta = \frac{10 - 0}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441 \text{ mV/LSB}$$ **Step (ii) — Maximum quantization error:** $$e_{q,\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} \approx 1.221 \text{ mV}$$ **Step (iii) — Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist):** $$f_{s,\min} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step (iv) — Theoretical SQNR:** $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$ **Step (v) — Resulting bit rate (at Nyquist):** $$R_{b} = f_{s,\min} \cdot n = 8000 \times 12 = 96{,}000 \text{ bps} = 96 \text{ kbps}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Signal Domain Comparison — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Phenomena
Temperature Pressure Sound] --> B[Transducer / Sensor] B --> C[Analog Signal
x of t - Continuous Time Continuous Amplitude] C --> D[Signal Conditioning
Amplify Filter Linearize] D --> E[ADC Block
Sampling then Quantization then Encoding] E --> F[Digital Signal
x of n - Discrete Time Discrete Amplitude] F --> G[Virtual Instrument
LabVIEW VI on PC] G --> H[Display / Storage / Control Output] F --> I[DAC Block] I --> J[Reconstructed Analog Signal] J --> K[Actuator / Display] style C fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#e6a700,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#e6a700,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 ADC Internal Pipeline — Mermaid Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SAMP [Sampling Stage] S1[Analog Input x of t] --> S2[Sample and Hold Switch] S2 --> S3[Hold Capacitor Ch] S3 --> S4[Sampled Voltage Vs at nTs] end SAMP --> Q subgraph Q [Quantization Stage] S4 --> Q1[Comparator Bank 2 to n] Q1 --> Q2[Priority Encoder] Q2 --> Q3[n bit Binary Word] end Q --> OUT[Digital Output Code] style SAMP fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style Q fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style OUT fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457 ``` ### 4.3 Time-Domain Visual Comparison (Conceptual Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AX [Analog Signal Domain] AX1[Vertical Axis: Continuous Amplitude] --- AX2[Horizontal Axis: Continuous Time] --- AX3[Smooth unbroken curve y = sin of t] end subgraph DX [Digital Signal Domain] DX1[Vertical Axis: Finite levels 0 and 1] --- DX2[Horizontal Axis: Discrete instants] --- DX3[Staircase binary word sequence] end style AX fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33 style DX fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c ``` ### 4.4 Typical LabVIEW DAQ Data Flow (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR DAQ1[DAQ Assistant
Acquire] --> DAQ2[Waveform Graph
Display] DAQ1 --> DAQ3[File Write
tdms or csv] DAQ1 --> DAQ4[Signal Processing
Filter FFT] DAQ4 --> DAQ5[Analysis VI
Statistics Peak] DAQ5 --> DAQ6[Front Panel Indicators] style DAQ1 fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#daa520 style DAQ2 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064 style DAQ4 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between analog and digital signals. List any four points.* **Model Answer (4 key points, 3-mark depth):** 1. **Time nature:** Analog signals are continuous in time; digital signals exist only at discrete sampling instants. 2. **Amplitude nature:** Analog amplitude is continuous (infinite resolution); digital amplitude is quantized to $2^n$ discrete levels. 3. **Noise immunity:** Analog signals are directly corrupted by additive noise; digital signals tolerate noise up to half the quantization step $\Delta/2$. 4. **Processing platform:** Analog requires op-amp-based hardware circuits; digital is processed by computers, DSPs, and FPGAs. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing?* **Model Answer:** The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed without loss from its samples *iff* the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum rate $2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. **Aliasing** is the irreversible distortion that occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ — high-frequency components fold back into the lower spectrum and become indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency content. It is prevented by applying an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = f_{max}$ *before* sampling. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Data Acquisition System (DAS) used in Virtual Instrumentation. Clearly label the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 16-bit ADC operates over the input range $[-10\text{ V}, +10\text{ V}]$. The input signal is a sinusoid with $f_{max} = 5$ kHz. Calculate:     (i) quantization step size $\Delta$,     (ii) maximum quantization error,     (iii) minimum sampling frequency, and     (iv) theoretical SQNR. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution — Part (a) A Virtual Instrumentation DAS contains the following functional blocks (in signal-flow order): | Block | Function | |---|---| | 1. Transducer | Converts physical quantity (T, P, F) into an electrical analog signal (V or mV). | | 2. Signal Conditioner | Amplifies weak signals, filters noise, linearizes response, provides isolation. | | 3. Multiplexer (MUX) | Selects one of many input channels sequentially to share a single ADC. | | 4. Sample-and-Hold (S/H) | Freezes the instantaneous analog value for the duration of ADC conversion. | | 5. ADC | Performs sampling, quantization, encoding → produces $n$-bit digital word. | | 6. Interface (USB/PCI/PXI/Ethernet) | Transfers digital words to the PC. | | 7. Software (LabVIEW VI) | Applies calibration, scaling, analysis, and visualization. | A complete block-level Mermaid rendition is shown below. ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[Transducer] --> T2[Signal Conditioner] T2 --> T3[Multiplexer] T3 --> T4[Sample and Hold] T4 --> T5[ADC] T5 --> T6[PC Interface] T6 --> T7[LabVIEW VI Software] ``` ##### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $n = 16$ bits, $V_{min} = -10$ V, $V_{max} = +10$ V, $f_{max} = 5$ kHz. **(i) Quantization step size:** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10 - (-10)}{2^{16}} = \frac{20}{65536} \approx 3.052 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} \approx 0.305\,\text{mV/LSB}$$ **(ii) Maximum quantization error:** $$e_{q,\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} \approx 0.1526 \text{ mV}$$ **(iii) Minimum sampling frequency (Nyquist):** $$f_{s,\min} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$ **(iv) Theoretical SQNR:** $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$ **Result:** $\Delta \approx 0.305$ mV, $e_{q,\max} \approx 0.153$ mV, $f_{s,\min} = 10$ kHz, SQNR $\approx 98.08$ dB. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]* **(a)** Explain the three fundamental operations involved in Analog-to-Digital conversion: sampling, quantization, and encoding. Mention the role of an anti-aliasing filter. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio of an $n$-bit ADC for a full-scale sinusoidal input. Hence calculate the SQNR of a 24-bit audio ADC. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Sampling:** The continuous-time analog signal $x(t)$ is measured at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$, producing the discrete-time sequence $x[n] = x(nT_s)$. To preserve all information, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion). **2. Quantization:** Each sample $x[n]$ is rounded to the nearest of $2^n$ discrete voltage levels, each of width $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$. The resulting sample is an integer $Q[n] \in \{0, 1, \dots, 2^n - 1\}$. The maximum rounding error is $\Delta/2$. **3. Encoding:** The integer $Q[n]$ is mapped to an $n$-bit binary word (natural binary, two's complement, or Gray code). Gray code is preferred in instrumentation because a 1-bit change in adjacent levels produces only a 1-bit transition, reducing transient errors. **4. Anti-aliasing filter:** A low-pass analog filter placed *before* the ADC, with cutoff frequency $f_c = f_{max}$ (or slightly below $f_s/2$). It removes any spectral content above $f_{max}$ that would otherwise fold back into the baseband via aliasing, which is *irreversible* once it occurs. ##### Model Solution — Part (b) **Derivation (recall from Section 3.5):** For a full-scale sinusoid of amplitude $A$: $$P_{sig} = \frac{A^{2}}{2}, \quad P_{noise} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} = \frac{A^{2}}{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}$$ $$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{sig}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}$$ In decibels: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}\right)$$ Using $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01$: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 20n \log_{10}(2) + 10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 6.02n + 1.76$$ **Numerical calculation for $n = 24$:** $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02(24) + 1.76 = 144.48 + 1.76 = 146.24 \text{ dB}$$ This is why professional 24-bit audio interfaces (such as those used in NI sound and vibration modules) achieve studio-grade dynamic range well beyond the 96 dB limit of 16-bit CD audio. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing up $f_s$ and $f_{max}$.** Many students write $f_s = 2 f_s$ or forget that the Nyquist rate is $2 f_{max}$, not $2 f_s$. Read the question carefully. > 2. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet.** $\Delta$ is in **volts per LSB**, not just volts. Always state units in the final answer. > 3. **Neglecting the anti-aliasing filter.** A block diagram of an ADC without an anti-aliasing filter preceding it loses at least 1–2 marks. > 4. **Wrong sign for bipolar ranges.** When $V_{min} = -10$ V and $V_{max} = +10$ V, the *range* is $20$ V (not $10$ V). Use $V_{max} - V_{min}$, not $V_{max}$ alone. > 5. **Confusing $n$ (bits) with $L$ (levels).** $L = 2^n$; the SQNR formula uses $n$, not $L$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Analog signal** = continuous in **both** time and amplitude; **Digital signal** = discrete in **both**. - [ ] The conversion chain is **Sensor → Conditioner → Anti-Alias Filter → S/H → ADC → PC**. - [ ] **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Below this → **aliasing** (irreversible). - [ ] **Quantization levels:** $L = 2^n$. **Step size:** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$. **Error bound:** $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$. - [ ] **Memorize:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ — every extra bit adds ~6 dB. - [ ] **Bit rate:** $R_b = f_s \cdot n$ — governs storage and transmission bandwidth. - [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter** must be *analog* and placed *before* the ADC. - [ ] **Digital wins** in noise immunity, storage, processing flexibility, and reproducibility. - [ ] **Analog wins** in infinite resolution, instantaneous response, and simplicity for high-frequency RF front-ends. - [ ] In LabVIEW, the *DAQ Assistant* block internally implements sampling, quantization, and encoding; the *Waveform Graph* displays the digital equivalent. - [ ] Gray code is often used in encoder ADCs to minimize transition errors at code boundaries. ---

topics.title: Sampling Fundamentals and Sampling Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET833 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sampling Fundamentals and Sampling Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time (sampled) signal $x(nT_s)$ by capturing instantaneous amplitude values of the signal at uniformly spaced time intervals governed by a periodic sampling clock. Mathematically, the ideal sampling operation can be represented as the multiplication of the continuous signal $x(t)$ with a periodic impulse train $p(t)$, where the sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the reciprocal of the sampling period $T_s$.

The **Sampling Theorem** (also known as the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**) is the foundational theoretical pillar of digital signal processing and virtual instrumentation. It rigorously states:

> A band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ having a maximum frequency component $f_m$ (in Hertz) can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples $x(nT_s)$ without any loss of information, provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition:
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

The minimum permissible sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is defined as the **Nyquist Rate**, and $f_m$ itself is referred to as the **Nyquist Frequency** of the signal under test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Sampling Theorem is the most frequently tested concept in Module 3. Students are expected to derive the theorem, explain aliasing qualitatively and mathematically, and analyze different sampling architectures (Natural, Flat-Top, and Sample-and-Hold).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a **spinning ceiling fan**. The fan blades rotate so fast that, to your eyes, they appear to be almost stationary or even rotating *backwards* — this is a real-world optical aliasing artifact caused by the finite "sampling rate" of your retina (which samples visual input at roughly 30–60 Hz).

If the fan slows down enough, your eyes can clearly resolve the individual blades. The "sampling theorem" simply formalizes this intuition in engineering terms: **you must sample a fast-changing signal frequently enough to capture all its rapid variations.** If you sample too slowly, high-frequency information folds back into lower frequencies and creates a distorted replica — this distortion is what we call **aliasing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Metric to Remember:**
> - $T_s$ (sampling period) = **seconds/sample**
> - $f_s$ (sampling frequency) = **samples/second (Hz)**
> - $f_m$ (highest signal frequency) = **Hz**
> - The fundamental trade-off: $f_s$ must be **at least twice** the highest frequency in $x(t)$.

### 1.3 Why Sampling Matters in Virtual Instrumentation

In a Virtual Instrumentation (VI) system — built upon platforms such as **National Instruments LabVIEW** — real-world analog sensor signals (temperature, pressure, vibration, strain, biomedical ECG, etc.) must be digitized by an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** before being processed inside a PC. The ADC performs three sequential operations:

1. **Sampling** — discretizing the time axis
2. **Quantization** — discretizing the amplitude axis
3. **Coding** — converting the quantized levels into binary codes

Without a proper understanding of the sampling theorem, the entire VI chain becomes mathematically invalid, leading to incorrect measurements regardless of how sophisticated the software is.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sampling Process — Operational Logic

The mathematical model of ideal (impulse) sampling is constructed in three structured steps:

**Step 1 — Define the Impulse Train Generator:**
A periodic train of unit-area impulses $p(t)$ with period $T_s$ is mathematically expressed as:
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

where $n$ is an integer index. The **Fourier Transform** of this impulse train is itself an impulse train in the frequency domain:
$$P(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 2 — Perform Multiplication (Time Domain):**
The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ is the product of the continuous signal $x(t)$ and the impulse train $p(t)$:
$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \, \delta(t - n T_s)$$

This equation confirms that the resulting sampled signal carries the original signal's amplitude $x(nT_s)$ only at the discrete instants $t = nT_s$.

**Step 3 — Transform to Frequency Domain:**
By the modulation property of the Fourier Transform, multiplication in time becomes convolution/periodic repetition in frequency:
$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

The original spectrum $X(f)$ is now replicated periodically around every multiple of $f_s$.

### 2.2 Why the Sampling Theorem Works (The Core Insight)

When $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas of $X(f)$ are spaced sufficiently far apart to **not overlap** with one another. As long as the spectrum replicas do not overlap, a low-pass filter (the **reconstruction filter**) can extract the original baseband spectrum $X(f)$ intact from $x_s(t)$, and the original signal $x(t)$ can be perfectly recovered.

If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas **overlap**, producing an irreversible distortion called **aliasing** — the original signal can never be perfectly reconstructed, no matter how sophisticated the post-processing.

### 2.3 Aliasing — The Critical Failure Mode

Aliasing is a **high-frequency masquerading as a low-frequency** phenomenon. If a signal containing a frequency component $f$ is sampled at $f_s$, the apparent frequency $f_a$ observed in the sampled data is given by:
$$f_a = \vert f - N f_s \vert$$
where $N$ is the integer that minimizes $f_a$. For the standard baseband range:
$$f_a = \vert f - f_s \vert \quad \text{when } f_s / 2 < f < f_s$$

**Example:** A real signal at **110 Hz** sampled at $f_s = 100$ Hz will appear at $f_a = \vert 110 - 100 \vert = 10$ Hz in the digitized data — the system "sees" 10 Hz instead of 110 Hz.

> [!WARNING]
> **Once aliasing occurs in the data acquisition stage, the information about the true high-frequency content is permanently destroyed.** No amount of digital signal processing can recover it.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1 / T_s$ | Samples per second | Hz |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate | Hz |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Highest recoverable baseband | Hz |
| Sampling Period | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | Time between samples | seconds |
| Aliased Frequency | $f_a = \vert f - N f_s \vert$ | Apparent sampled frequency | Hz |
| Number of Samples (Time) | $N = T \cdot f_s$ | Total samples over duration $T$ | dimensionless |
| Spectral Replicas | $X_s(f) = f_s \sum X(f - k f_s)$ | Periodically repeated spectrum | V/Hz |
| Resolution (per period) | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | DFT bin width | Hz |
| Reconstruction Filter Cutoff | $f_c = f_m$ | Anti-imaging filter | Hz |
| Oversampling Ratio | $OSR = f_s / f_{Nyquist}$ | $>1$ to avoid aliasing | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Practical Sampling Architectures in Virtual Instrumentation

In real VI hardware (e.g., NI DAQ cards), three sampling architectures dominate:

**(a) Natural Sampling (Gating):** The signal is passed through an electronic switch (sample gate) that closes for a brief interval $\tau$ around each sampling instant. The output retains the shape of $x(t)$ within $\tau$. The amplitude of the spectral replicas decreases as a sinc envelope — natural roll-off exists.

**(b) Flat-Top (Sample-and-Hold) Sampling:** A Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit charges a capacitor during a brief aperture and holds the value constant until the next acquisition. The output is a **staircase approximation** of the input. This is the architecture used in nearly every modern ADC, including successive-approximation and flash ADCs. The spectrum is weighted by a sinc function $H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \cdot e^{-j\pi f \tau}$, introducing a mild amplitude distortion (aperture effect) that can be corrected digitally.

**(c) Ideal (Impulse) Sampling:** Theoretical abstraction used for mathematical derivations — instantaneous sampling with zero-width pulses.

### 2.6 Anti-Aliasing Filter

A **low-pass analog filter** placed *before* the ADC that band-limits the input signal to a maximum frequency just below $f_s/2$ is called an **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)**. It is an indispensable component of every real-world DAQ system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Choose $f_s \approx 5 \times f_m$ to $10 \times f_m$ in practical VI applications. This generous oversampling relaxes the design requirements of the anti-aliasing filter and improves signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) by approximately **3 dB per octave** of oversampling.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Biomedical:** ECG signals are band-limited to 150 Hz and sampled at 360–500 Hz in clinical systems.
- **Audio Engineering:** CDs use $f_s = 44.1$ kHz for an audio bandwidth of ~20 kHz (Nyquist rate = 40 kHz).
- **Telecommunications:** Software-Defined Radio (SDR) front-ends apply the sampling theorem to digitize RF signals directly.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Modal testing systems sample accelerometer data at 10–50 kHz to capture mechanical resonances.
- **Industrial DAQ:** NI PXIe systems commonly operate at 1–2 MS/s for high-speed process control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectrum of an Ideally Sampled Signal

We begin with a continuous band-limited signal $x(t)$ whose Fourier Transform $X(f)$ is non-zero only for $-f_m \leq f \leq f_m$.

**Step 1: Write the impulse train.**
A periodic impulse train $p(t)$ with period $T_s$ is defined as:
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 2: Express the impulse train as a complex Fourier Series.**
Because $p(t)$ is periodic with period $T_s$, its fundamental frequency is $f_0 = 1/T_s = f_s$. The complex Fourier series representation is:
$$p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, e^{j 2 \pi k f_s t}$$

where the Fourier coefficient $C_k$ is found by integrating over one period:
$$C_k = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} \delta(t) \, e^{-j 2 \pi k f_s t} \, dt$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the coefficient using the sifting property of the delta function.**
Since $\delta(t)$ is non-zero only at $t = 0$ inside the integration interval, the integral reduces to:
$$C_k = \frac{1}{T_s} e^{-j 2 \pi k f_s (0)} = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into the series.**
$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2 \pi k f_s t}$$

**Step 5: Multiply $x(t)$ by $p(t)$ to obtain the sampled signal.**
$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{j 2 \pi k f_s t}$$

**Step 6: Take the Fourier Transform of both sides.**
By linearity of the Fourier Transform and the frequency-shifting property (where multiplication by $e^{j 2 \pi f_0 t}$ shifts the spectrum by $f_0$):
$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

Equivalently, since $1/T_s = f_s$:
$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 7: Interpret the result geometrically.**
The original spectrum $X(f)$ — originally confined to $[-f_m, f_m]$ — is now replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$: at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \pm 3 f_s, \ldots$ Each replica is scaled in amplitude by a factor of $f_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical observation:** If $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the replicas do not touch. The baseband copy $X(f)$ (centered at $k=0$) can be isolated by a low-pass filter of cutoff $f_m$, thereby **perfectly reconstructing** $x(t)$ from its samples.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for No Aliasing

For the spectral replicas centered at $k = 0$ and $k = 1$ to remain non-overlapping, the right edge of the baseband replica $X(f)$ (at frequency $f_m$) must not exceed the left edge of the first replica (at frequency $f_s - f_m$):
$$f_m \leq f_s - f_m$$

Solving for $f_s$:
$$2 f_m \leq f_s$$

Thus:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m \equiv f_{Nyquist}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantification of the Aliased Frequency

Consider a pure sinusoid $x(t) = A \cos(2 \pi f_0 t)$ sampled at $f_s$ where $f_0 > f_s/2$. The sampled values are:
$$x(n T_s) = A \cos(2 \pi f_0 n T_s) = A \cos(2 \pi n f_0 / f_s)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(2\pi n \alpha) = \cos(2\pi n \vert \alpha - m \vert)$ where $m$ is the integer chosen so that $\vert \alpha - m \vert \leq 0.5$:
$$x(n T_s) = A \cos\left(2 \pi n \frac{\vert f_0 - m f_s \vert}{f_s}\right)$$

Defining $f_a = \vert f_0 - m f_s \vert$, we observe that the sampled data is **mathematically indistinguishable** from sampling a sinusoid at the lower aliased frequency $f_a$. This is why aliasing causes irreversible distortion.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample Calculations Table

| Signal Freq $f_0$ (Hz) | Sample Rate $f_s$ (Hz) | Nyquist Satisfied? | Aliased Freq $f_a$ (Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 200 | Yes ($f_s > 2f_m$) | 50 (no aliasing) |
| 110 | 200 | Yes | 110 (no aliasing) |
| 110 | 100 | No | $\vert 110 - 100 \vert = 10$ |
| 700 | 500 | No | $\vert 700 - 500 \vert = 200$ |
| 1500 | 1000 | No | $\vert 1500 - 1000 \vert = 500$ |
| 2400 | 1000 | No | $\vert 2400 - 2000 \vert = 400$ |
| 75 | 100 | Yes | 75 (no aliasing) |

### 3.5 Reconstruction Formula (Ideal D/A)

If the sampling condition is satisfied, the original continuous signal can be reconstructed by passing the impulse-sampled signal $x_s(t)$ through an ideal low-pass filter of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_m$. The output is given by the **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula**:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - n T_s}{T_s}\right)$$

where:
$$\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$$

This formula states that the continuous signal can be perfectly reconstructed as a weighted sum of shifted sinc functions, each centered at a sample location $nT_s$ and weighted by the sample amplitude $x(nT_s)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete Sampling-Reconstruction System
