## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Nyquist Rate and Nyquist Frequency. A sinusoidal signal $x(t) = 5 \sin(200\pi t)$ is to be sampled without aliasing. Calculate the minimum sampling frequency required.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Nyquist Rate** ($f_{Nyquist}$): The minimum sampling rate that allows perfect reconstruction of a band-limited signal, defined as $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m$.
- **Nyquist Frequency** ($f_N$): The highest baseband frequency that can be unambiguously represented, defined as $f_N = f_s / 2$.

For the given signal $x(t) = 5 \sin(200\pi t)$:
$$f_m = \frac{200\pi}{2\pi} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

Therefore the minimum sampling rate is:
$$f_{s,min} = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is aliasing? Explain how it can be prevented in a virtual instrumentation data acquisition system.*

**Model Answer:**
**Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a continuous signal appear as false lower-frequency components in the sampled (discrete) data when the sampling rate is less than twice the maximum signal frequency. Once aliasing occurs, the original high-frequency information is irretrievably lost.

**Prevention Methods:**
1. **Sampling Rate Selection:** Always sample at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. In practice, $f_s = 5 f_m$ to $10 f_m$ is recommended.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Place a low-pass analog filter (cutoff just below $f_s/2$) before the ADC to physically remove all frequency content above $f_s/2$.
3. **Band-Limited Signals:** Ensure the input signal is inherently band-limited, or apply pre-filtering.
4. **Oversampling:** Use ADCs with sampling rates much higher than the Nyquist minimum.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal. State and prove the Sampling Theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a continuous band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$. Let it be sampled by an ideal impulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s$:
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

The Fourier series representation of $p(t)$ is:
$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2 \pi k f_s t}$$

The sampled signal is:
$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{j 2 \pi k f_s t}$$

Taking the Fourier Transform and applying the frequency-shifting property:
$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Statement of Sampling Theorem:**
> A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

**Proof:** From the derived spectrum, $X(f)$ is replicated at integer multiples of $f_s$. For non-overlapping replicas:
$$f_m \leq f_s - f_m \implies f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

When this holds, an ideal LPF of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_m$ extracts the original spectrum, hence $x(t)$ is perfectly recovered.

---

**(b)** Explain the different sampling techniques — Natural Sampling, Flat-Top Sampling, and Sample-and-Hold — with suitable diagrams and waveform equations. Compare their spectral characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Natural Sampling:** A sample gate closes briefly for duration $\tau$ around each $nT_s$. The output retains the natural shape of $x(t)$:
$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot g(t), \quad g(t) = \text{rectangular pulse train with width } \tau$$

Spectrum: Replicas weighted by $\tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau)$ envelope.

**2. Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold):** A capacitor holds the sampled voltage constant for the entire interval $T_s$:
$$x_{flat}(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{t - nT_s - T_s/2}{T_s}\right)$$

Spectrum: Replicas weighted by $T_s \cdot \text{sinc}(f T_s) \cdot e^{-j\pi f T_s}$ — introduces mild amplitude droop at high frequencies (aperture effect), corrected by an equalization filter.

**3. Sample-and-Hold (Practical):** Combination of a switch, hold capacitor, and buffer amplifier. The output is a staircase waveform. This is the architecture used in real ADCs.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Natural | Flat-Top | S/H |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Shape | Pulses | Staircase | Staircase (held) |
| Aperture Effect | Moderate | Strong | Compensated |
| Used In | Theoretical | Practical ADC | Industrial DAQ |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** What is an Anti-Aliasing Filter? With a neat block diagram, explain the complete signal reconstruction process from sampled data, and derive the Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):**
An AAF is a continuous-time low-pass filter placed immediately before the ADC. Its cutoff frequency is set to $f_c \leq f_s/2$ to ensure that no frequency component above the Nyquist frequency reaches the sampler. Without the AAF, out-of-band noise or harmonics can fold back into the baseband and corrupt the measurement.

**Reconstruction Process (Block Diagram):**

```
Sampled Signal x(nTs) -> DAC -> Staircase Output -> Reconstruction LPF (cutoff fm) -> Smoothed x(t)
```

**Derivation of Interpolation Formula:**

The sampled signal in frequency domain:
$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

Multiply by the transfer function of an ideal LPF of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_m$:
$$H(f) = T_s \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{f}{2 f_m}\right)$$

The reconstructed spectrum is:
$$X_{rec}(f) = X_s(f) \cdot H(f) = f_s T_s X(f) = X(f) \quad \text{(since } f_s T_s = 1 \text{)}$$

Taking the inverse Fourier Transform:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - n T_s}{T_s}\right)$$

This is the **Whittaker-Shannon Interpolation Formula**, which expresses the continuous-time signal as a sum of weighted, time-shifted sinc functions.

---

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 3 \cos(100\pi t) + 5 \sin(200\pi t) + 2 \cos(400\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz. Determine:
- (i) The Nyquist Rate of the signal.
- (ii) Whether the chosen $f_s$ causes aliasing.
- (iii) The reconstructed (or aliased) frequency components observed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify frequency components:**
- Component 1: $3 \cos(100\pi t) \implies f_1 = 50$ Hz
- Component 2: $5 \sin(200\pi t) \implies f_2 = 100$ Hz
- Component 3: $2 \cos(400\pi t) \implies f_3 = 200$ Hz

**Step 2 — Determine Nyquist Rate:**
$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 \times f_m = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Check aliasing:**
Given $f_s = 250$ Hz, and $f_{Nyquist} = 400$ Hz, we observe that $f_s < f_{Nyquist}$, so **aliasing WILL occur**.

**Step 4 — Compute aliased frequencies:**

For $f_1 = 50$ Hz (below $f_s/2 = 125$ Hz): No aliasing. Apparent frequency = **50 Hz**.

For $f_2 = 100$ Hz (below $f_s/2 = 125$ Hz): No aliasing. Apparent frequency = **100 Hz**.

For $f_3 = 200$ Hz (above $f_s/2 = 125$ Hz): Aliasing occurs. Apparent frequency:
$$f_{a,3} = \vert 200 - 250 \vert = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Reconstructed signal (what the DAQ will display):**
The aliased 200 Hz component appears at 50 Hz and **adds constructively** with the original 50 Hz component. The observed signal will be:
$$x_{observed}(t) = 5 \cos(100\pi t) + 5 \sin(200\pi t)$$

**Conclusion:** The 200 Hz tone is **permanently lost**, and the 50 Hz component appears with doubled amplitude — a classic aliasing artifact.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit Hz conversion** when going from $\omega$ to $f$: always divide angular frequency by $2\pi$.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency** — Rate is $2f_m$, Frequency is $f_s/2$.
> 3. **Missing the reconstruction filter** in the block diagram — KTU examiners specifically check for it.
> 4. **Skipping the mathematical proof of no spectral overlap** — must show $f_m \leq f_s - f_m$ explicitly.
> 5. **Neglecting to mention the Anti-Aliasing Filter** when describing the sampling process.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sampling** converts continuous time → discrete time; **Quantization** converts continuous amplitude → discrete amplitude.
- The **Sampling Theorem** requires $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ for perfect reconstruction.
- **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_m$ (Hz); **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_s / 2$ (Hz).
- **Aliasing** is irreversible — prevention is the only cure.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is a MUST in every real DAQ front-end; cutoff at $f_s/2$.
- **Three Sampling Architectures:** Ideal Impulse (theoretical), Natural (gated), Flat-Top / S-H (practical ADC).
- **Whittaker-Shannon Formula** reconstructs $x(t)$ using weighted sinc functions.
- **Practical Oversampling Rule:** Use $f_s = 5 f_m$ to $10 f_m$ to relax filter design and improve SNR.
- **Spectral Replicas** are centered at $k f_s$ for $k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$, scaled by $f_s$.
- **Aperture Effect** in flat-top sampling causes sinc-weighted amplitude droop — corrected by an equalizer.
- **LabVIEW / NI DAQ** applications must use at least 2× oversampling for any active sensor signal.
- **Number of samples in $T$ seconds:** $N = f_s \times T$. **Frequency resolution:** $\Delta f = f_s / N$.
- **Sampling applies** in ADC, DSP, communications, biomedical, audio, vibration, control systems.
- **Common Pitfall:** A signal with $f_m = 110$ Hz sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz is fine ($f_s > 2f_m$); but at $f_s = 150$ Hz, it aliases to $\vert 110 - 150 \vert = 40$ Hz.
- **Memory:** A KTU Module 3 favourite — always include the **anti-aliasing filter** in your block diagram to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 – Basics of ADC and DAC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic device or circuit that converts a discrete digital input code (usually binary) into a continuous analog output quantity (voltage or current) proportional to the digital word. In Virtual Instrumentation, DACs are the final stage that bridges the digital processing domain of software (e.g., LabVIEW) with the real-world analog actuators, signal generators, and control outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:** *A DAC is an n-bit binary weighted current/voltage summing network whose analog output is the mathematical sum of binary-weighted reference contributions.*

**Conceptual Analogy – The "Staircase to Slide" Idea:**
Imagine a flight of stairs. Each step represents a discrete binary count (000, 001, 010, … 111). A DAC is like a magical machine that takes the step number (digital code) and draws a *smooth ramp* between the steps so an observer standing far away sees a continuous slope — that slope is the analog output. The smaller the step height (resolution), the smoother the slope.

### 1.2 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** performs the inverse operation: it samples a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal at discrete time intervals and quantizes each sample into a finite-resolution binary code. The three sub-processes are **Sampling**, **Holding**, and **Quantization & Encoding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon):** To reconstruct the original signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be **at least twice** the highest frequency component $f_{max}$ present in the analog input: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

**Conceptual Analogy – The "Digital Camera" Idea:**
Think of taking a photograph of a moving clock hand. The ADC is the camera: it **samples** the hand's position at intervals (frame rate), and **quantizes** the angle into a finite number of pixels (resolution). More pixels → more precise reading; faster frame rate → ability to capture fast motion without blur. The digital photo is the binary code.

### 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (KTU Highlight Metrics)

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution (bits) | $n$ | **8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 24** |
| Full-Scale Range | $\text{FSR}$ | **0–10 V, ±5 V, ±10 V, 0–20 mA** |
| Reference Voltage | $V_{ref}$ | **2.5 V, 5.0 V, 10.0 V** |
| Sampling Rate | $f_s$ | **kS/s to GS/s** |
| Settling Time | $t_s$ | **ns to µs** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always treat the **reference voltage $V_{ref}$** and the **bit-depth $n$** as the two governing parameters of any converter. They define *every* other derived quantity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a DAC

A generic DAC is fundamentally a **summing network**. Depending on the implementation, the binary weights are realized using:

1. **Weighted-Resistor Network** (each bit drives a resistor of value $\frac{R}{2^k}$).
2. **R-2R Ladder Network** (only two resistor values — ideal for IC fabrication).
3. **Inverted/Active R-2R Ladder with Op-Amp summer**.

For an n-bit DAC with digital input word $D = b_{n-1}2^{n-1} + b_{n-2}2^{n-2} + \cdots + b_1 2^1 + b_0 2^0$, the ideal unipolar output is obtained by binary-weighted current summation at a virtual-ground op-amp node.

## 2.2 Internal Architecture of an ADC

Common ADC architectures (in increasing resolution/speed trade-off):

1. **Flash (Parallel) ADC** – uses $2^n - 1$ comparators; **fastest**, lowest resolution.
2. **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** – binary search algorithm; **medium speed, medium resolution** (industry workhorse for data acquisition).
3. **Dual-Slope Integrating ADC** – excellent noise rejection; used in **digital multimeters**.
4. **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\text{-}\Delta$) ADC** – oversampling + noise shaping; **highest resolution**, used in audio/precision measurement.
5. **Pipeline ADC** – high speed with moderate resolution (video, communications).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Governing Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless integer |
| 2 | Resolution (step size, unipolar) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Resolution (bipolar) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Ideal DAC output (unipolar) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Ideal DAC output (bipolar) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2D}{2^n} - 1\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Quantization error (max) | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 7 | RMS quantization noise | $V_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 8 | Theoretical SNR (ideal N-bit) | $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | decibels (dB) |
| 9 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 10 | Oversampling factor | $\text{OSR} = \dfrac{f_s}{2 f_{max}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Throughput equation (single channel) | $\text{Channels} = \dfrac{f_s}{\text{Samples/channel}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Settling time vs. accuracy | $t_s \approx \tau \cdot \ln(2^n)$ | seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute-Value Substitute:** For the table above, any mathematical "$\vert x \vert$" form has been written using the explicit expression rather than the pipe symbol, to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Control & Automation:** DAC drives 4–20 mA current loops to operate valves and actuators.
- **Audio Engineering:** DAC reconstructs PCM digital audio into audible waveforms; bit-depth governs dynamic range.
- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG/EEG front-ends use 16–24 bit $\Sigma\text{-}\Delta$ ADCs to capture microvolt-level signals.
- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** High-speed ADCs (GS/s) sample RF spectra directly.
- **Virtual Instrumentation (LabVIEW):** DAQ cards (e.g., NI PCIe-6363) host 16-bit ADCs and DACs; programming in LabVIEW selects ranges, sample rates, and trigger modes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Unipolar DAC Transfer Equation

We begin with the binary expansion of the digital input word.

$$
D = b_{n-1} 2^{n-1} + b_{n-2} 2^{n-2} + \cdots + b_1 2^1 + b_0 2^0
$$

where each $b_k \in \{0,1\}$.

**Step 1 — Recognize binary weighting:**  
Each bit $b_k$ carries a weight of $2^k$ relative to the least significant bit (LSB, $k=0$).

**Step 2 — Apply proportionality to reference voltage:**  
In a summing network (R-2R ladder or weighted resistor), the analog output is the sum of binary-weighted fractions of $V_{ref}$.

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref}\left( \frac{b_{n-1}}{2^1} + \frac{b_{n-2}}{2^2} + \cdots + \frac{b_1}{2^{n-1}} + \frac{b_0}{2^n} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the common denominator $2^n$:**  
Multiplying numerator and denominator gives:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref}\left( \frac{b_{n-1}\, 2^{n-1} + b_{n-2}\, 2^{n-2} + \cdots + b_0}{2^n} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $D$:**  
The numerator is exactly $D$, hence:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_{ref}\cdot\frac{D}{2^n}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Quantization Step Size (Resolution)

The full-scale range is the maximum possible output, achieved when $D = 2^n - 1$ (all bits = 1). For an unipolar converter spanning $0$ to $V_{ref}$:

$$
\text{FSR} = V_{ref} - 0 = V_{ref}
$$

The resolution is defined as the smallest change in output for a 1-LSB change in input code:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
$$

This is also called the **Least Significant Bit (LSB) voltage**, denoted $V_{LSB}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Quantization Error

Quantization error is the difference between the actual analog value and its digitized value. Since each analog value is rounded to the nearest quantization level, the worst-case error is half of one step.

$$
e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: RMS Quantization Noise Voltage

Assume the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta V/2, +\Delta V/2]$. The mean-square value (variance) of a uniform distribution on interval $[-a, a]$ is $a^2/3$. With $a = \Delta V/2$:

$$
\overline{e_q^{\,2}} = \frac{(\Delta V/2)^2}{3} = \frac{\Delta V^2}{12}
$$

Taking the square root gives the RMS noise voltage:

$$
V_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\overline{e_q^{\,2}}} = \frac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

For a full-scale sinusoidal input of amplitude $V_{ref}/2$, the RMS signal voltage is:

$$
V_{s,rms} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

The SNR in linear scale is the ratio of signal power to noise power:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = \frac{V_{s,rms}^{\,2}}{V_{q,rms}^{\,2}} = \frac{(V_{ref}/2\sqrt{2})^2}{(\Delta V/\sqrt{12})^2}
$$

Substitute $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = \frac{V_{ref}^{\,2}/8}{V_{ref}^{\,2}/(12 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = \frac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2}
$$

Express in decibels using $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR}_{\text{lin}})$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2}\right) = 10\left[\log_{10} 1.5 + n \log_{10} 4\right]
$$

Using $\log_{10} 1.5 \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10} 4 \approx 0.6020$:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{SNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76\,}
$$

This is the famous *6-dB-per-bit* rule of thumb used in KTU valuation scripts.

## 3.6 Worked Example: 12-bit DAC with 5 V Reference

**Given:** $n = 12$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$, digital input $D = 0xA5F$ (hexadecimal).

**Step 1 — Convert to decimal:**

$$
D = (0xA5F)_{16} = 10\cdot 16^2 + 5\cdot 16 + 15 = 2655
$$

**Step 2 — Compute resolution (LSB voltage):**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} \approx 1.2207\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ideal analog output:**

$$
V_{out} = 5\cdot\frac{2655}{4096} = 5\cdot 0.64819 \approx 3.2410\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute theoretical SNR:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 74.00\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute max quantization error:**

$$
e_{q,\max} = \pm\frac{5}{2^{13}} = \pm 0.61035\,\text{mV}
$$

## 3.7 Worked Example: Sampling Theorem Application

**Given:** An audio signal has frequency components up to $f_{max} = 20\,\text{kHz}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Nyquist criterion:**

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot 20\,\text{kHz} = 40\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard CD sampling rate** is $44.1\,\text{kHz}$ to allow a guard band for anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

**Step 3 — Oversampling factor:**

$$
\text{OSR} = \frac{44.1}{2\cdot 20} = 1.1025
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow – DAC Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DIGITAL INPUT WORD D] --> B[INPUT REGISTER LATCH]
    B --> C[BINARY WEIGHTED NETWORK]
    C --> D[OPAMP SUMMING JUNCTION]
    D --> E[ANALOG OUTPUT VOUT]
    F[VREF REFERENCE SOURCE] --> C
    G[CLOCK / UPDATE PULSE] --> B

    classDef io fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000

```

```mermaid
    SAR --> OUT[PARALLEL DIGITAL OUTPUT CODE]
    VREF[REFERENCE VOLTAGE VREF] --> DAC_INT[INTERNAL N BIT DAC]
    DAC_INT --> CMP

```

```mermaid
    end

    W1 --> ADC --> PC --> DAC --> W2
```

## 4.4 ADC Architecture Selection Tree (Decision Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{REQUIRED RESOLUTION} -->|8 BITS OR LESS| Q2{REQUIRED SPEED}
    Q1 -->|10 TO 14 BITS| Q3{APPLICATION TYPE}
    Q1 -->|16 BITS PLUS| Q4{NEED FOR NOISE SHAPING}

    Q2 -->|VERY HIGH| F[FLASH ADC]
    Q2 -->|MODERATE| H[HALF FLASH ADC]

    Q4 -->|NO| PIP[PIPELINE ADC]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Memory Aid for KTU:** *Flash is fastest, Sigma-Delta is most precise, SAR is the balanced workhorse, and Dual-Slope is the multimeter's choice.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] 
**Define the term "Resolution" of an ADC/DAC and explain its significance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Resolution of an ADC/DAC is the smallest change in analog input/output that can be detected/produced, mathematically defined as:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
$$

where $n$ is the number of bits.

**Significance (1 mark):** Resolution determines the *fineness* of measurement and the *accuracy* of reproduction. A higher bit count (larger $n$) yields a smaller $\Delta V$, enabling finer distinction between closely spaced analog values.

**Example (1 mark):** For a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$, $\Delta V = 5/4096 \approx 1.22\,\text{mV}$. A 16-bit ADC with the same reference would have $\Delta V \approx 0.076\,\text{mV}$, giving ~16× finer resolution.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State and explain the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem. What is aliasing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** An analog signal of highest frequency component $f_{max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$
f_s \geq 2\,f_{max}
$$

The minimum permissible sampling rate, $2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

**Aliasing (1.5 marks):** If the signal contains frequency components above $f_s/2$, these higher frequencies fold back into the baseband and appear as false low-frequency components called *aliases*. This is prevented by passing the input through an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before sampling.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, candidates answer ONE full question (14 marks) per module. Each question carries an internal choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 🔹 Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8-bit DAC has a full-scale output of 10 V. Determine: **(i)** the resolution (LSB voltage), **(ii)** the analog output for digital input 10110110, **(iii)** the percentage resolution, and **(iv)** the maximum quantization error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

```
Vin → Sample & Hold → Comparator ←→ Internal DAC ← Vref
                       ↓
                    SAR Logic
                       ↓
                Digital Output Code
```

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. On the START command, the SAR sets the MSB ($b_7$) = 1, all other bits = 0. The digital word is fed to the internal DAC, producing a trial voltage $V_{DAC}$.
2. The comparator checks whether $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$:
   - If **yes**, MSB is retained (= 1).
   - If **no**, MSB is reset to 0.
3. The next bit ($b_6$) is set to 1, and the comparison repeats. This **binary search** continues for all $n$ bits, taking exactly $n$ clock cycles.
4. After $n$ cycles, the SAR holds the final $n$-bit code representing $V_{in}$ within ±½ LSB.

**Advantages (1 mark):** High resolution (12–18 bits), moderate speed (1 MS/s), low power, simple interfacing.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Requires an accurate internal DAC; not suited for very high-speed (>10 MS/s) applications; sensitive to noise during conversion.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 8$, $\text{FSR} = 10\,\text{V}$, digital input $D = (10110110)_2$.

**Step 1 — Compute decimal value of D (1 mark):**

$$
D = 1\cdot 2^7 + 0\cdot 2^6 + 1\cdot 2^5 + 1\cdot 2^4 + 0\cdot 2^3 + 1\cdot 2^2 + 1\cdot 2^1 + 0\cdot 2^0
$$

$$
D = 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 182
$$

**Step 2 — Resolution (LSB voltage) (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^8} = \frac{10}{256} = 0.0390625\,\text{V} \approx 39.06\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Analog output (2 marks):**

$$
V_{out} = \text{FSR}\cdot\frac{D}{2^n} = 10\cdot\frac{182}{256} = 10\cdot 0.71094 = 7.1094\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage resolution (1 mark):**

$$
\%\,\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^n - 1}\cdot 100 = \frac{1}{255}\cdot 100 \approx 0.392\%
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum quantization error (1 mark):**

$$
e_{q,\max} = \pm\frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm\frac{39.06\,\text{mV}}{2} = \pm 19.53\,\text{mV}
$$

---

### 🔹 Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an **R-2R Ladder DAC**. Show that the output voltage is proportional to the digital input word. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 16-bit ADC samples a signal with maximum frequency 5 kHz. Compute: **(i)** the minimum sampling rate as per Nyquist theorem, **(ii)** the number of quantization levels, **(iii)** the resolution if $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$, and **(iv)** the theoretical SNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**  
The R-2R ladder has only two resistor values (R and 2R). Each node represents one bit position. The current through each 2R shunt is binary-weighted by virtue of the ladder's repeating geometry.

**Step-by-Step Proof (4 marks):**

For a 4-bit R-2R ladder, applying Thevenin's theorem from the MSB end:

- At the MSB node, the equivalent resistance looking into the ladder is always **R**, regardless of switch position.
- When bit $b_{n-1} = 1$, the MSB 2R branch is connected to the **virtual ground** (op-amp summing node). Current through it:

$$
I_{n-1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R}
$$

- When $b_{n-1} = 0$, the same current is shunted to **ground** and contributes nothing to the output.
- The LSB 2R branch carries a current $I_0 = I_{n-1}/2^{n-1} = V_{ref}/(2R \cdot 2^{n-1}) = V_{ref}/(2^n R)$.

By superposition, the total current at the virtual ground summing node:

$$
I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R}\left(b_{n-1} + \frac{b_{n-2}}{2} + \frac{b_{n-3}}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^{n-1}}\right) = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R}\cdot\frac{D}{2^{n-1}}
$$

The op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f$ produces:

$$
V_{out} = -I_{total}\cdot R_f = -\frac{V_{ref}\,R_f}{2R}\cdot\frac{D}{2^{n-1}}
$$

For $R_f = 2R$ and taking magnitude:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_{ref}\cdot\frac{D}{2^n}\,}
$$

which confirms proportionality to $D$ (1 mark).

**Advantages (1 mark):** Only two resistor values → easy IC fabrication, excellent matching, monotonic output.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 16$, $f_{max} = 5\,\text{kHz}$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Nyquist minimum sampling rate (2 marks):**

$$
f_{s,\min} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \cdot 5000 = 10\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of quantization levels (1.5 marks):**

$$
L = 2^n = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 3 — Resolution (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{65{,}536} \approx 7.6294 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{V} = 76.29\,\mu\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical SNR (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02\cdot 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08\,\text{dB}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in ADC/DAC Problems:**
> 1. **Sign error in bipolar transfer equation** — KTU examiners frequently award zero marks if the student forgets the $-1$ term in $V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(\frac{2D}{2^n} - 1\right)$. Always state clearly whether the converter is unipolar or bipolar.
> 2. **Confusion between $\text{FSR}$ and $V_{ref}$** — For a unipolar converter, $\text{FSR} = V_{ref}$, but for a bipolar converter, $\text{FSR} = 2 V_{ref}$. Substituting the wrong one doubles or halves every subsequent answer.
> 3. **Percentage Resolution Trap** — Use $\frac{1}{2^n - 1}\cdot 100$, *not* $\frac{1}{2^n}\cdot 100$. The off-by-one difference can cost 1 mark.
> 4. **SNR Formula Sign** — Always include the **+1.76 dB** correction. Examiners specifically test whether students know the full form vs. the approximation $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02\,N$.
> 5. **Nyquist Direction** — Writing $f_s \leq 2 f_{max}$ instead of $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is a classic error. It is a *minimum* sampling rate, not a maximum.
> 6. **Missing units** in final numerical answers result in partial mark deductions under strict KTU marking schemes. Always quote **V, mV, kHz, dB** explicitly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DAC** = Digital → Analog; **ADC** = Analog → Digital. They are inverse operations.
- **Resolution (LSB voltage):** $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$. Smaller $\Delta V$ means finer resolution.
- **Number of levels:** $L = 2^n$. Example: 8 bits → 256 levels; 16 bits → 65,536 levels.
- **Ideal unipolar DAC output:** $V_{out} = V_{ref}\cdot D/2^n$. Bipolar version includes $-1$ offset.
- **Maximum quantization error:** $\pm V_{ref}/2^{n+1}$ = ±½ LSB.
- **RMS quantization noise:** $\Delta V/\sqrt{12}$.
- **Theoretical SNR:** $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB (≈ 6 dB improvement per extra bit).
- **Nyquist Theorem:** Sample at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** is mandatory before the ADC's sample-and-hold stage.
- **Architecture quick-pick:** Flash (fast, low-res) → SAR (balanced) → Dual-Slope (precise DC) → Sigma-Delta (audio/precision) → Pipeline (high-speed).
- **R-2R Ladder** uses only two resistor values → monolithic IC friendly.
- **Weighted Resistor Network** is simple but requires widely varying resistor values (poor for high bit counts).
- **LabVIEW Context (KTU 2024 emphasis):** Select DAQ device in *Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX)*, configure voltage range and sample rate in *DAQmx tasks*, and integrate ADC/DAC operations through *VIs* (Virtual Instruments).
- **Signal conditioning** (amplification, isolation, filtering) precedes the ADC and follows the DAC in any virtual instrumentation signal chain.
- **Monotonicity** of a DAC means the output never decreases as the digital code increases — a key specification for control applications.
- **Throughput rule:** Samples per second × bits per sample = data rate in **bits/s** for digital storage/transmission.
- **Common KTU reference voltages:** 2.5 V, 5.0 V, 10.0 V.
- **One LSB rule of thumb:** A 1 LSB change equals $V_{ref}/2^n$. Memorize this — it appears in 80% of numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview and Need of Signal Conditioning (Amplification and Filtering)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET833 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview and Need of Signal Conditioning (Amplification and Filtering)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Signal Conditioning?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Signal Conditioning* is the process of manipulating an analog signal in such a way that it meets the requirements of the next stage for further processing. It is the essential intermediate stage between the **physical sensor (transducer)** and the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** or data acquisition system.

In the architecture of a Virtual Instrumentation (VI) system, the raw output of a sensor is rarely in a form suitable for direct digitization. It may be too weak (low amplitude), contaminated with noise, contain unwanted frequency components, or have an incompatible impedance. **Signal Conditioning** is the engineering discipline that prepares this raw signal for accurate, reliable measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Components Typically Included in Signal Conditioning:**
> 1. **Amplification** — Boosts weak sensor signals to a usable voltage range.
> 2. **Filtering** — Removes unwanted noise and frequency components.
> 3. **Isolation** — Protects against high voltages and ground loops.
> 4. **Linearization** — Corrects non-linear sensor responses.
> 5. **Excitation** — Provides power to passive sensors (e.g., strain gauges, RTDs).
> 6. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — Used in thermocouple circuits.
> 7. **Range Matching / Scaling** — Aligns signal span with ADC input range.

---

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Signal Conditioning? (The "Need" Aspect)

Real-world signals from physical sensors suffer from several practical issues. The table below lists the **core problems** and **the corresponding conditioning action** required:

| # | Problem in Raw Signal | Why it Happens | Required Conditioning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Very low amplitude (mV, µV) | Sensors like thermocouples, strain gauges produce tiny voltages | **Amplification** |
| 2 | Noise / interference superimposed | EMI, 50 Hz mains hum, thermal noise | **Filtering** (LPF, Notch, BPF) |
| 3 | High common-mode voltages | Industrial ground potential differences | **Isolation** |
| 4 | Non-linear sensor output | Thermistors, RTDs have non-linear T-R curves | **Linearization** |
| 5 | High output impedance | Sensors cannot drive low-impedance loads | **Buffering / Impedance matching** |
| 6 | Incompatible dynamic range | Signal may exceed ADC limits (clipping) | **Attenuation / Range scaling** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask, *"Why is signal conditioning called the 'heart' of a data acquisition system?"* The answer is — because **no amount of digital processing can recover information that was lost due to improper analog conditioning** (e.g., a clipped or noise-saturated signal).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Doctor's Stethoscope 💓

Imagine a doctor using a stethoscope to listen to your heartbeat:

- The **heartbeat sound** is the **raw sensor signal** — soft, low in amplitude, mixed with breathing sounds and background noise.
- The **stethoscope** acts as a **signal conditioner**:
  - It **amplifies** the soft heart sounds so the doctor can hear them.
  - The chest piece **filters out** high-frequency rustling noises and low-frequency rumbling.
  - The tubing **isolates** the listener from the patient's electrical environment (no shocks!).
- The **doctor's trained ear** is the **ADC + processor** — it interprets the clean, conditioned signal into a diagnosis.

**In the same way**, in a VI system, the sensor → signal conditioner → ADC chain ensures that what gets digitized is a **clean, amplified, noise-free representation** of the physical phenomenon.

---

## 1.4 Position of Signal Conditioning in a VI System

A typical Virtual Instrumentation data flow is:

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioner} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Computer (Software)}
$$

The signal conditioner is the **analog front-end (AFE)** that sits between the physical world and the digital processing world. Without it, the digital domain would receive distorted, noisy, or unusable data.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Overview of Signal Conditioning Subsystems

A signal conditioning block is typically modular, meaning each function (amplification, filtering, etc.) can be designed and implemented independently. In a Virtual Instrumentation context using tools like **LabVIEW**, the conceptual model treats each conditioning operation as a sub-VI or processing block.

---

## 2.2 AMPLIFICATION — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Definition

**Amplification** is the process of increasing the magnitude of a signal (voltage or current) by a factor called the **gain**, while preserving its shape and information content.

### 2.2.2 Why Amplification is Needed

1. **ADC Resolution Maximization** — ADCs work best when input signal uses the **full dynamic range** (e.g., 0–10 V). A thermocouple producing 5 mV wastes 99.95% of the ADC's range. Amplification brings it up to a usable level.
2. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — Amplifying the signal *before* it travels through long cables reduces the relative effect of picked-up noise.
3. **Driving Capability** — Long cables have capacitance; a high-output-impedance signal cannot drive them. An amplifier provides low output impedance.

### 2.2.3 Types of Amplifiers Used in Signal Conditioning

| Amplifier Type | Purpose / Use Case | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** | Standard choice for sensor signals | High CMRR, high input impedance, differential input, adjustable gain via single resistor |
| **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** | General purpose voltage amplification | High open-loop gain, requires external feedback |
| **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA)** | Auto-ranging applications | Gain set digitally by software |
| **Isolation Amplifier** | High-voltage or medical applications | Galvanic isolation between input and output |
| **Charge Amplifier** | Piezoelectric sensors | Converts charge output to voltage |
| **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA)** | Photodiodes, current-output sensors | Converts current → voltage |

### 2.2.4 The Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) — The Workhorse

The INA is constructed from **three op-amps**: two input buffer op-amps and one difference amplifier.

**Gain Equation for INA:**

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}
$$

Where:
- $A_v$ = Differential voltage gain (dimensionless)
- $R_1$ = Fixed input resistor
- $R_{gain}$ = External gain-setting resistor (only one variable resistor — easy calibration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The single external resistor $R_{gain}$ in an INA is a **huge practical advantage** because it allows precise, symmetric gain adjustment without disturbing the highly-matched resistor network inside the chip, preserving excellent Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR).

### 2.2.5 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

$$
\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)
$$

Where:
- $A_d$ = Differential gain
- $A_{cm}$ = Common-mode gain
- Higher CMRR (in dB) = better rejection of unwanted common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum appearing equally on both lines).

---

## 2.3 FILTERING — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Definition

**Filtering** is the frequency-selective attenuation or amplification of a signal. A filter passes certain frequencies (the **passband**) and rejects others (the **stopband**).

### 2.3.2 Why Filtering is Needed

1. **Noise Removal** — Eliminates EMI, thermal noise, and 50/60 Hz power-line interference.
2. **Anti-Aliasing** — Prevents high-frequency content from folding back into the lower band during sampling (a **critical** role before an ADC).
3. **Signal Isolation** — Extracts the frequency band of interest from a complex composite signal.
4. **Smoothing** — Removes rapid fluctuations for stable display.

### 2.3.3 The Four Canonical Filter Types

| Filter Type | Symbol | Passes | Rejects | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | LPF | 0 to $f_c$ | $f > f_c$ | Anti-aliasing, smoothing, removing high-frequency noise |
| **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | HPF | $f > f_c$ | 0 to $f_c$ | Removing DC offset, AC coupling |
| **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | BPF | $f_{c1}$ to $f_{c2}$ | Outside band | Selecting a specific channel/signal of known frequency |
| **Band-Stop (Notch) Filter** | BSF | All except a narrow band | A narrow band around $f_n$ | Removing 50 Hz mains hum, removing 60 Hz interference |

### 2.3.4 Ideal vs. Real Filters

An **ideal filter** has:
- A perfectly flat passband.
- Infinite attenuation in the stopband.
- An infinitely sharp transition (brick-wall response).

A **real filter** has:
- A ripple in the passband.
- A finite roll-off rate in the transition band.
- A limited stopband attenuation.

The **roll-off rate** is expressed in **dB/octave** or **dB/decade**:
- **1st-order filter:** −20 dB/decade
- **2nd-order filter:** −40 dB/decade
- **nth-order filter:** −20·n dB/decade

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Amplification & Filtering)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Gain (Amplifier) | $A_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Ratio of output to input voltage |
| 2 | Gain in dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | Logarithmic form of gain |
| 3 | INA Gain | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ | Three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier |
| 4 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio in dB |
| 5 | RC LPF Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | −3 dB point of first-order LPF |
| 6 | RC HPF Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | −3 dB point of first-order HPF |
| 7 | Roll-off Rate (nth order) | $\text{Roll-off} = -20n \text{ dB/decade}$ | Filter attenuation slope |
| 8 | SNR | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio |
| 9 | Anti-aliasing Condition (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Sampling frequency must be ≥ twice the highest signal frequency |
| 10 | Noise Power Spectral Density | $N_0 = k_B T$ | Where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant ($\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$ J/K) and $T$ is temperature in Kelvin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in nearly every KTU board exam paper related to Data Acquisition and Virtual Instrumentation. Mastering them is **non-negotiable** for scoring above 80% in this module.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutoff Frequency of a First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter

### Circuit Description

A first-order RC Low-Pass Filter consists of a **series resistor R** and a **shunt capacitor C**, with the output taken across the capacitor.

### Step 1: Write the Voltage Divider Equation in the Frequency Domain

Using the impedance of the resistor $Z_R = R$ and the capacitor $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$, the output voltage $V_{out}$ is given by the voltage divider rule:

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Impedances

Substitute $Z_R = R$ and $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ into the divider expression:

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Fraction

Multiply the numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ to clear the fraction:

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

### Step 4: Write the Transfer Function

The transfer function $H(j\omega)$ is the ratio of output to input:

$$
H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Magnitude Response

The magnitude $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ is:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

### Step 6: Define Cutoff Frequency

The cutoff frequency $f_c$ (or $\omega_c$) is defined as the frequency at which the output power drops to **half** of its maximum, or equivalently, the voltage drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., **−3 dB point**). At this point, the imaginary and real parts of the denominator are equal:

$$
\omega_c RC = 1
$$

### Step 7: Solve for $\omega_c$ and $f_c$

$$
\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{(in radians per second)}
$$

Converting to Hz using $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$:

$$
\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

### Step 8: Physical Interpretation

- For $\omega \ll \omega_c$ (low frequency): $\vert H \vert \approx 1$ → signal passes unattenuated.
- For $\omega \gg \omega_c$ (high frequency): $\vert H \vert \approx \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ → attenuation grows at **−20 dB/decade**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — High-Pass Filter Cutoff Frequency (Symmetric to LPF)

For an RC High-Pass Filter, the output is taken across the **resistor** (not the capacitor). Repeating the same analysis with roles reversed:

### Step 1: Voltage Divider (with output across R)

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}
$$

### Step 2: Simplify

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

### Step 3: Transfer Function

$$
H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

### Step 4: Magnitude

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

### Step 5: At Cutoff

At the −3 dB point, the real and imaginary parts are equal, giving the **same cutoff expression**:

$$
\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Instrumentation Amplifier Differential Gain

### Circuit Topology (3 Op-Amp INA)

The instrumentation amplifier consists of:
- Two input op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ with a common resistor $R_{gain}$ between their inverting inputs.
- A difference amplifier $A_3$ with feedback resistors $R_2$ and $R_3$.

### Step 1: Analyze the First Stage

The voltage across $R_{gain}$ is $V_1 - V_2$ (the differential input). The current through $R_{gain}$ is:

$$
I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_{gain}}
$$

This same current flows through both $R_1$ resistors (since the op-amp inputs draw no current).

### Step 2: Output Voltages of $A_1$ and $A_2$

By KVL around the feedback loop:

$$
V_{out1} = V_1 + I \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{(V_1 - V_2) R_1}{R_{gain}}
$$

$$
V_{out2} = V_2 - I \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{(V_1 - V_2) R_1}{R_{gain}}
$$

### Step 3: Differential Output of First Stage

$$
V_{out1} - V_{out2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2(V_1 - V_2) R_1}{R_{gain}} = (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Difference Amplifier Gain

The difference amplifier $A_3$ has a gain of $\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ (assuming matched resistors). Its output is:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{out1} - V_{out2})
$$

### Step 5: Final INA Gain Equation

Combining steps 3 and 4:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

Therefore, the **total differential voltage gain** is:

$$
\boxed{A_v = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> If the INA uses matched resistors (i.e., $R_2 = R_3$), this simplifies to the widely-used form:
> $$A_v = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

| Application Scenario | Recommended Conditioner | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Thermocouple (~mV output) | INA + LPF + CJC | Amplify tiny voltage, remove HF noise, compensate cold junction |
| Strain gauge (Wheatstone bridge) | INA + Excitation source + LPF | Bridge needs excitation, differential amp reads mV-level output |
| ECG / biomedical signal | INA + HPF (0.05 Hz) + LPF (150 Hz) + Notch (50 Hz) | Remove DC, muscle noise, mains hum |
| Vibration analysis (high freq) | Charge amp + LPF (anti-alias) + ADC | Piezo sensors give charge; aliasing is critical |
| Pressure transducer (4–20 mA) | Current-to-voltage converter + LPF | Industrial standard current loop |
| Audio signal (20 Hz – 20 kHz) | Op-amp buffer + BPF | Match impedance, select frequency band |

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Signal Conditioning in a VI System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Phenomenon] --> B[Sensor / Transducer]
    B --> C[Raw Analog Signal mV uA]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioner Block]
    D --> D1[Amplification]
    D1 --> D2[Filtering]
    D2 --> D3[Isolation]
    D3 --> D4[Linearization]
    D4 --> D5[Excitation if needed]
    D5 --> E[Conditioned Analog Signal 0 to 10V]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Digital Data Stream]
    G --> H[DAQ Card / DAQ Board]
    H --> I[Computer Software LabVIEW]
    I --> J[Display Analysis Storage]
```

## 4.2 Amplification Subsystem — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputA[Sensor Signal Positive] --> INAP[INA Stage 1 Buffer Op Amp 1]
    InputB[Sensor Signal Negative] --> INAN[INA Stage 1 Buffer Op Amp 2]
    INAP --> INADiff[Difference Amplifier Stage 2 Op Amp 3]
    INAN --> INADiff
    INADiff --> Rgain[Gain Set by Rgain]
    Rgain --> OutAmp[Amplified Output]
    OutAmp --> Vout[Conditioned Signal to Filter or ADC]
```

## 4.3 Filtering Subsystem — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Input Signal from Amplifier] --> Q1{What noise is dominant?}
    Q1 -->|High frequency noise EMI| LPF[Apply Low Pass Filter]
    Q1 -->|DC offset or drift| HPF[Apply High Pass Filter]
    Q1 -->|Mains hum 50 Hz 60 Hz| NOTCH[Apply Notch Filter]
    Q1 -->|Specific frequency band of interest| BPF[Apply Band Pass Filter]
    LPF --> Check1{Frequency below fc ?}
    HPF --> Check2{Frequency above fc ?}
    NOTCH --> OutF[Filtered Clean Signal to ADC]
    BPF --> OutF
    Check1 -->|Yes| PassF[Pass Signal]
    Check1 -->|No| BlockF[Attenuate]
    Check2 -->|Yes| PassF
    Check2 -->|No| BlockF
    PassF --> OutF
    BlockF --> OutF
```

## 4.4 Frequency Response Curves (ASCII Representation)

```
AMPLITUDE (dB)
  |
 0dB  ----------------------------+--------------------
  |                              /
-3dB - - - - - - - - - - - - - + - - - - - - - - -  (LPF Cutoff fc)
  |                            /
-20dB                         /  (slope: -20 dB/decade for 1st order)
  |                          /
-40dB                       /   (slope: -40 dB/decade for 2nd order)
  |                        /
  +--------|-------------|---------------|---------> FREQUENCY (log scale)
         f1             fc              f2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **−3 dB point** corresponds to a power ratio of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ (half-power point), or a voltage ratio of $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
